## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define marine pollution as per UNCLOS Article 1(4) and list any four major types of marine pollutants.**

**Model Answer:**

According to **UNCLOS Article 1(4)**, marine pollution is defined as:

> *"The introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) which results or is likely to result in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, impairment of water quality, and reduction of amenities."*

**Four major types of marine pollutants:**

1. **Oil and Petroleum Hydrocarbons** — from tanker spills, runoff, offshore drilling.
2. **Heavy Metals** — mercury, lead, cadmium, chromium from industrial effluents.
3. **Nutrients (N, P)** — from agricultural runoff and sewage causing eutrophication.
4. **Plastics and Microplastics** — macro, meso, micro, and nano plastic debris from land-based and sea-based sources.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
**Explain the phenomenon of biomagnification with reference to mercury in the marine food chain.**

**Model Answer:**

**Biomagnification** is the progressive increase in concentration of a persistent pollutant at successive trophic levels of a food chain.

**Mercury Biomagnification Pathway:**

1. **Source**: Industrial discharge releases inorganic Hg²⁺ into seawater.
2. **Methylation**: Anaerobic bacteria in sediments convert Hg²⁺ → **methylmercury (CH₃Hg⁺)**, the most toxic organic form.
3. **Bioaccumulation**: Methylmercury is lipid-soluble and accumulates in organism tissue (BAF = 10⁵–10⁶).
4. **Trophic transfer**: Concentration increases as predators consume prey.

**Concentration gradient example (Minamata-style):**

$$
\text{Water} \rightarrow \text{Plankton} \rightarrow \text{Small fish} \rightarrow \text{Large fish} \rightarrow \text{Humans}
$$

$$
0.02 \, \mu g/L \rightarrow 0.05 \, mg/kg \rightarrow 0.3 \, mg/kg \rightarrow 2 \, mg/kg \rightarrow 50+ \, mg/kg
$$

The overall **biomagnification factor** can exceed **2.5 million times** from seawater to humans, causing severe neurological disorders (Minamata disease).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major types of marine pollution. Describe in detail the sources, behavior, and ecological impacts of **oil pollution** in the marine environment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **nitrogen and phosphorus cycle disruption** in coastal waters leading to eutrophication. Discuss the Redfield ratio and its significance.

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Classification of Marine Pollution Types:**

Marine pollution is broadly classified into **three categories**:

**I. Material/Substance Pollution:**
- Oil and petroleum hydrocarbons
- Heavy metals
- Nutrients (N, P)
- Plastics and microplastics
- Sewage and pathogens
- POPs (Persistent Organic Pollutants)
- Radioactive materials

**II. Energy Pollution:**
- Thermal discharge
- Underwater noise
- Light pollution

**III. Biological Pollution:**
- Invasive species
- Pathogenic microorganisms

**Oil Pollution — Detailed Treatment:**

**Sources (with percentages):**
- Land-based runoff: **46%** (urban, refinery, atmospheric)
- Routine shipping discharge: **37%** (bilge water, tank cleaning)
- Accidental spills: **12%** (tanker accidents, blowouts)
- Atmospheric deposition: **5%**

**Behavior of oil in seawater (Fay's process):**
1. **Spreading** — forms thin slick (1 µm to 10 cm thick)
2. **Evaporation** — 20–40% of light fractions evaporate within 24 hours
3. **Emulsification** — water-in-oil "mousse" formation
4. **Dissolution** — water-soluble toxic components dissolve
5. **Oxidation** — photochemical and chemical oxidation
6. **Biodegradation** — by marine bacteria (slow)
7. **Sedimentation** — oil-sediment aggregates sink
8. **Persistence** — heavy fractions persist in sediments for **decades**

**Ecological Impacts:**
- **Smothering** of seabirds (loss of insulation, drowning)
- **Toxic effects** on fish eggs and larvae
- **Coral reef damage** (long-term recovery: 20–50 years)
- **Mangrove destruction** (sediment-bound oil persists 30+ years)
- **Bioaccumulation** in higher trophic levels
- **Economic losses** to fisheries, tourism, desalination

**Example case study:** Exxon Valdez spill (1989, Alaska) — 42 million liters spilled, recovery still incomplete after 35 years.

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Nitrogen and Phosphorus Cycle Disruption & Eutrophication:**

**Normal Marine N and P Cycle:**

In pristine coastal waters, nitrogen enters via:
- N₂ fixation by cyanobacteria
- Riverine input
- Atmospheric deposition
- Upwelling from deep water

Phosphorus enters via:
- Rock weathering → riverine transport
- Atmospheric dust deposition
- Upwelling

These nutrients are utilized by phytoplankton in the **16:1 N:P Redfield ratio**:

$$
\text{C:N:P} = 106:16:1
$$

**Disruption by Anthropogenic Activities:**

Human activities disrupt this balance through:

1. **Agricultural runoff** — synthetic fertilizers (urea, DAP) wash into coastal waters
2. **Municipal sewage** — high in N (urea from urine) and P (detergents)
3. **Aquaculture waste** — fish farm effluent (high N, P)
4. **Atmospheric deposition** — NOx from vehicles, NH₃ from livestock

**Eutrophication Cascade:**

$$
\text{Excess Nutrients} \rightarrow \text{Algal Bloom} \rightarrow \text{Blocked Sunlight} \rightarrow \text{Photic Zone Collapse}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Death of Algae} \rightarrow \text{Bacterial Decomposition} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 \text{ Depletion}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Hypoxia/Anoxia} \rightarrow \text{Fish Death} \rightarrow \text{Dead Zone Formation}
$$

**Significance of Redfield Ratio (16:1):**
- Indicates balanced nutrient availability for phytoplankton
- Deviation → shift in dominant species
- N:P > 16:1 → P-limited → cyanobacterial dominance
- N:P < 16:1 → N-limited → diatom dominance

**Example Case Studies:**
- **Gulf of Mexico Dead Zone** — Mississippi River nutrient loading, **16,700 km²** annually
- **Baltic Sea** — largest hypoxic zone globally, **70,000 km²**
- **Indian context** — Kochi backwaters, Chilika Lake eutrophication

**Schematic: Eutrophication Process**

```
Nutrient Input (N, P)
        ↓
Phytoplankton Bloom
        ↓
Light Blockage (Self-shading)
        ↓
Algal Death + Sinking
        ↓
Bacterial Decomposition
        ↓
Oxygen Depletion (Hypoxia < 2 mg/L O₂)
        ↓
Marine Life Suffocation
        ↓
Dead Zone Formation
```

---

**QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the classification, sources, and ecological impact of **plastic pollution** in the marine environment. Discuss microplastics and nanoplastic concerns.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the sources and impacts of **thermal pollution and noise pollution** in marine ecosystems. Suggest two control measures for each.

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Plastic Pollution — Classification:**

**By Size:**
- **Macroplastics** (> 25 mm) — bottles, bags, fishing nets, ropes
- **Mesoplastics** (5–25 mm) — bottle caps, fragments
- **Microplastics** (1 µm – 5 mm) — microbeads, synthetic fibers, tire wear
- **Nanoplastics** (< 1 µm) — degraded fragments, chemical leachates

**By Origin:**
- **Primary microplastics** — manufactured as small particles (cosmetics, industrial abrasives)
- **Secondary microplastics** — fragmentation of larger plastics via UV, wave action

**By Polymer Type (in ocean, in order of prevalence):**
1. Polyethylene (PE) — 34%
2. Polypropylene (PP) — 17%
3. PET — 10%
4. Polyamide — 8%
5. Polystyrene — 7%
6. PVC — 6%

**Sources:**
- **Land-based (80%)** — improper waste disposal, river input, urban runoff
- **Sea-based (20%)** — fishing gear (ghost nets), shipping, aquaculture
- **Atmospheric** — synthetic fiber shedding from textiles

**Persistence:**
- Standard PE: **400–1000 years** in marine environment
- PET bottles: **450 years**
- Fishing nets (nylon): **600 years**

**Ecological Impacts:**
1. **Entanglement** — seals, turtles, whales in ghost nets
2. **Ingestion** — seabirds, fish, sea turtles mistake plastic for food
3. **Trophic transfer** — microplastics move up food chain
4. **Chemical leaching** — additives (BPA, phthalates, PBDE) leach into tissues
5. **Microplastic vector effect** — adsorbs POPs, transports them in biota
6. **Habitat alteration** — plastic accumulation on beaches and seafloor
7. **Socioeconomic** — tourism loss, fisheries damage, shipping hazards

**Microplastic and Nanoplastic Concerns:**
- **Microplastics (1 µm–5 mm)** — found in drinking water, salt, seafood, atmospheric precipitation
- **Nanoplastics (< 1 µm)** — can cross cell membranes, enter bloodstream, cause inflammation
- **Concentration example:** Average human ingests **5 grams of plastic per week** (equivalent to a credit card — WWF study)

**Control Measures:**
- Single-use plastic bans
- Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)
- Improved waste management and recycling
- Bioplastics development
- International treaties (UNEA Resolution on plastic pollution, 2022)

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Thermal Pollution:**

**Definition:** Discharge of heated water (from cooling systems) into marine environment, raising ambient temperature.

**Sources:**
- **Coastal thermal power plants** (coal, nuclear, gas) — major source
- **Desalination plant brine discharge** (warm, salty)
- **Industrial cooling water** (steel, petrochemical)
- **Steel mills and LNG terminals**

**Impacts on Marine Ecosystem:**
1. **Reduced dissolved oxygen** (warm water holds less O₂)

$$
C_{O_2}(T) = 14.6 - 0.41T + 0.008T^2
$$

   — at 25°C vs 30°C, ~3% loss in DO capacity.

2. **Thermal shock** — fish kills in discharge zones
3. **Altered reproduction** — disrupted spawning cycles
4. **Coral bleaching** — tropical reef systems critically affected
5. **Migration of species** — tropical species shift poleward
6. **Impingement and entrainment** — fish on intake screens

**Control Measures:**
1. **Cooling towers** — closed-loop cooling replacing once-through systems
2. **Effluent diffusers** — multiport diffusers for rapid dispersion
3. **Combined cycle plants** — higher efficiency, less waste heat
4. **Regulatory ΔT limit** — typically **ΔT ≤ 3°C** above ambient (EPA / CPCB standard)

**Noise Pollution:**

**Definition:** Anthropogenic sound introduction into marine environment above natural ambient levels.

**Sources:**
- **Commercial shipping** (continuous, low-frequency, 100–1,000 Hz)
- **Seismic airgun surveys** (impulsive, 10–300 Hz, very high intensity up to 250 dB)
- **Military sonar** (active, mid-frequency, 1–10 kHz)
- **Pile driving** for offshore wind farms and bridges
- **Recreational vessels**

**Sound Pressure Level:**

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \quad [\text{dB re 1 µPa}]
$$

**Reference levels:**
- Whale song: 180 dB
- Shipping noise: 180–190 dB
- Seismic airgun: 250+ dB
- Military sonar: 235 dB

**Impacts on Marine Life:**
1. **Hearing damage** — temporary and permanent threshold shifts
2. **Masking of communication** — whales cannot communicate
3. **Stress hormone elevation** (cortisol)
4. **Disrupted navigation** (cetaceans use echolocation)
5. **Mass stranding events** — linked to naval sonar exercises
6. **Reduced foraging efficiency**
7. **Reproductive failure**

**Control Measures:**
1. **Speed reduction of ships** ("Slow Steaming") — reduces cavitation noise
2. **Quieter propeller design** — skewed, higher pitch propellers
3. **Seasonal restrictions** on seismic surveys during marine mammal breeding
4. **Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)** with acoustic refuges
5. **Acoustic Deterrent Devices (ADDs)** for safe pile driving
6. **Bubble curtains** during offshore construction

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Bioaccumulation and Biomagnification** — bioaccumulation is increase in an organism over time; biomagnification is increase across trophic levels. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Wrong unit for noise** — underwater sound is measured in **dB re 1 µPa**, NOT dB SPL (air). This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if confused.
> 3. **Confusing DO and BOD** — DO is dissolved oxygen present; BOD is oxygen demand for decomposition. KTU examiners frequently ask "What happens to BOD in polluted water?" (Answer: BOD increases).
> 4. **Missing UNCLOS definition** — KTU valuation key specifically requires the UNCLOS Article 1(4) wording for full 3 marks on definition questions.
> 5. **Redfield ratio** — must be stated as **C:N:P = 106:16:1 by moles**, not by mass (common error).
> 6. **Oil spill equation misuse** — Fay's equation requires consistent SI units; converting gallons to m³ or hours to seconds is often done incorrectly.
> 7. **Plastic classification by size** — students often forget nanoplastic category (< 1 µm); examiners deduct marks for incomplete classification.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST:**

- **Definition:** UNCLOS Article 1(4) — introduction of substances or energy by man into marine environment causing harm.
- **3 Master Categories:** Material/Substance pollution, Energy pollution, Biological pollution.
- **9 Major Pollution Types (GESAMP):** Oil, Plastics, Heavy metals, Nutrients, Chemicals (POPs), Sewage, Radioactive, Thermal, Noise.
- **Source Distribution:** Land 80%, Sea 10%, Atmosphere 10%.
- **Bioaccumulation (BAF):** Concentration buildup within one organism over time.
- **Biomagnification (BMF):** Concentration increase across trophic levels.
- **Redfield Ratio:** C:N:P = 106:16:1 (by moles) — threshold for phytoplankton balance.
- **Fay's Oil Spreading:** A(t) = K·V^(1/3)·t^(1/4); K ≈ 100–150.
- **Radioactive Decay:** N(t) = N₀·e^(-λt); t½ = 0.693/λ.
- **Cs-137 half-life:** 30.17 years; Sr-90: 28.8 years.
- **DO Temperature Relation:** C_O₂ = 14.6 − 0.41T + 0.008T² (mg/L, freshwater).
- **Sound Level:** L_p = 20·log₁₀(p/p₀) in dB re 1 µPa (not dB SPL).
- **Plastic Persistence:** PE 400–1000 years; PET 450 years; Nylon 600 years.
- **Critical Hg Concentration:** Water 0.02 µg/L → Humans 50+ mg/kg (BMF 2.5 million×).
- **Regulatory Frameworks:** UNCLOS, MARPOL Annexes I–VI, Stockholm Convention, London Convention, London Protocol.
- **Bathing Water Standard:** Fecal coliform < 200 MPN/100 mL (WHO); EU: E. coli < 100, Enterococci < 130 cfu/100 mL.
- **Largest Dead Zone:** Baltic Sea (~70,000 km²); Gulf of Mexico ~16,700 km².
- **Great Pacific Garbage Patch:** 1.6 million km², 1.8 trillion particles.
- **Thermal Limit:** ΔT ≤ 3°C above ambient at discharge (standard regulatory limit).
- **Minamata Disease (1956):** Landmark case of mercury biomagnification in Japan.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Sources of Marine Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Sources of Marine Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Pollution (UNEP / GESAMP Definition):**
> *"Marine pollution is the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) resulting in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities including fishing, impairment of quality for use of sea water, and reduction of amenities."*

In simpler academic terms, **marine pollution** refers to any anthropogenic (human-induced) or natural input that alters the **physical, chemical, biological, or radiological** integrity of the ocean and its ecosystems, exceeding the natural assimilative capacity of seawater.

## 1.2 Classification of Sources — Top-Level Hierarchy

The KTU syllabus recognizes **three principal pathways** through which pollutants enter the marine environment:

1. **Land-Based Sources (LBS)** — Contribute ~ **80%** of marine pollution globally (UNEP estimate).
2. **Sea-Based Sources (SBS)** — Shipping, offshore platforms, operational/accidental discharges.
3. **Air-Based Sources (ABS)** — Atmospheric transport of particulates, gases, and aerosols.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The syllabus specifically emphasizes **Land-Based Sources** as the dominant contributor, with detailed sub-classification into **Point Sources** and **Non-Point (Diffuse) Sources**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sink" Model

Imagine the ocean as a giant **kitchen sink**, and the Earth as the surrounding countertop.

- **Point Sources** are like a leaking faucet — you can pinpoint the exact tap and turn it off (e.g., a factory pipe).
- **Non-Point Sources** are like splashed dishwater all over the counter — it seeps in from everywhere and is nearly impossible to control (e.g., agricultural runoff).
- **Sea-Based Sources** are like someone pouring waste *directly into the sink* from a moving boat (e.g., bilge water from ships).
- **Air-Based Sources** are like invisible steam condensing back into the sink from above (e.g., mercury vapor from coal plants).

This mental model helps visualize **why marine pollution control is so difficult** — only a fraction comes from easily identifiable "taps."

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Marine Pollution Studies

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand | BOD | mg/L (or ppm) | Measures organic pollution strength |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand | COD | mg/L (or ppm) | Total oxidizable matter |
| Dissolved Oxygen | DO | mg/L | Indicator of water health |
| Total Suspended Solids | TSS | mg/L | Turbidity/particle load |
| Heavy Metal Concentration | $C_{HM}$ | $\mu$g/L or ppb | Toxicity assessment |
| Coliform Count | CFU/100 mL | count | Sewage/fecal contamination |
| Oil & Grease | O&G | mg/L | Hydrocarbon pollution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Standard:** Coastal waters should maintain **DO $\geq$ 5 mg/L** for sustaining marine life, and **BOD$_5$ should remain below 3 mg/L** for bathing waters (per CPCB / WHO bathing water guidelines).

## 1.5 Snapshot — Why This Topic is KTU-High-Yield

- ⭐ Direct 7-mark and 14-mark questions are framed from this topic every semester.
- ⭐ The **80% land-based statistic** is a frequently tested fact.
- ⭐ Students must differentiate **Point vs. Non-Point sources** — a classic 3-mark question.
- ⭐ Examples (Mumbai dumping, MV Wakashio, Gulf oil spill) are examiner favorites.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Land-Based Sources (LBS) — Detailed Breakdown

Land-based sources constitute the largest fraction of marine pollution and originate from coastal and inland human activities that are transported via rivers, runoff, groundwater, and coastal outfalls.

### 2.1.1 Point Sources
Discharges from a **single, identifiable, fixed location** — typically through pipes or ditches.

| Sub-Category | Description | Typical Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| Municipal Sewage Outfalls | Urban wastewater treatment plants discharging to sea | Organic matter, nutrients, pathogens, pharmaceuticals |
| Industrial Effluents | Chemical, petrochemical, textile, pulp & paper plants | Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd, Cr), dyes, phenols, PCBs |
| Power Plant Cooling Water | Thermal discharges | Heat, biocides (chlorine), trace metals |
| Desalination Brine | Reverse osmosis reject | High salinity brine (70,000 ppm TDS) |
| Aquaculture Effluents | Fish/prawn farm discharge | Fecal matter, antibiotics, uneaten feed |

### 2.1.2 Non-Point Sources (Diffuse Sources)
Dispersed contributions from **multiple, scattered locations** — extremely difficult to regulate.

| Sub-Category | Description | Typical Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| Agricultural Runoff | Fertilizers and pesticides washed by rain | Nitrates, phosphates, pesticides, herbicides |
| Urban Stormwater | Rainwater runoff from cities | Oils, litter, PAHs, microplastics |
| Construction & Mining | Erosion and sediment discharge | Suspended solids, heavy metals |
| Forestry Activities | Soil erosion from logging | Sediment, organic matter |
| Atmospheric Deposition (land→river→sea) | Airborne particulates settling into watersheds | Heavy metals, POPs, nitrogen oxides |

## 2.2 Sea-Based Sources (SBS)

Pollutants introduced directly into the marine environment from vessels, offshore structures, and intentional dumping.

### 2.2.1 Shipping-Related Pollution

Shipping contributes approximately **18% of global oil inputs** to oceans (GESAMP estimate).

| Source | Type of Pollution | Reference Incident |
|---|---|---|
| Operational Discharge (Bilge water) | Oil, oily waste | Routine ship operations |
| Accidental Spills (Tanker collisions) | Crude/refined oil | Torrey Canyon (1967), Exxon Valdez (1989) |
| Ballast Water | Invasive species, pathogens | Zebra mussel invasions |
| Anti-fouling Paints | TBT (tributyltin), copper | Imposex in gastropods |
| Container Loss (Nurdles) | Plastic pellets | MV X-Press Pearl (2021) |
| Sewage & Greywater | Organic waste, nutrients | Cruise ships |
| Air Emissions (SO$_x$, NO$_x$) | Acid rain, greenhouse gases | Sulfur Emission Control Areas (SECAs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tributyltin (TBT)** — A KTU examiner favorite. TBT is a biocide used in anti-fouling paints that causes **imposex** (development of male sex organs in female marine snails). Banned globally under IMO AFS Convention (2001), effective **September 17, 2008** for ships, with full entry-into-force in **2008** and complete ban for all vessels by **January 1, 2008** for new applications.

### 2.2.2 Offshore Oil & Gas Operations

- **Drilling Muds & Cuttings** — Discharge of rock cuttings coated with oil-based or synthetic-based muds.
- **Produced Water** — Largest volumetric waste from offshore platforms (formation water with hydrocarbons, heavy metals, radioisotopes).
- **Accidental Blowouts** — Deepwater Horizon (2010) released ~ **4.9 million barrels** of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico.

### 2.2.3 Dumping at Sea

| Convention | Year | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| London Convention | 1972 | Banned ocean dumping of hazardous wastes |
| London Protocol | 1996 | Prohibits all dumping except "harmless" dredged material |

Permitted dumping categories:
- Dredged material (with permits)
- Sewage sludge
- Fish waste (from processing)
- Vessels and platforms (deliberate sinking)

Banned: Industrial waste, radioactive waste, organohalogens, crude oil, heavy metals, plastics, and chemical/biological weapons.

## 2.3 Air-Based Sources (ABS)

Atmospheric transport is the **least visible** but **most widespread** marine pollution pathway.

| Pollutant | Source | Marine Impact |
|---|---|---|
| SO$_2$ / NO$_x$ | Fossil fuel combustion, shipping | Acidification of surface seawater ($\downarrow$ pH) |
| CO$_2$ | Global emissions | Ocean acidification (pH drop from 8.2 to 8.1 since pre-industrial era) |
| Mercury (Hg) | Coal combustion, waste incineration | Bioaccumulation as methylmercury |
| POPs (Persistent Organic Pollutants) | Pesticides, industrial chemicals | Long-range atmospheric transport to polar regions |
| Black Carbon | Diesel, incomplete combustion | Arctic ice darkening, accelerating melt |
| Radioactive Fallout | Nuclear tests, accidents | Cs-137, Sr-90 in marine food chain |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| First-order decay (pollutant in water) | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Modeling BOD exertion, oil degradation |
| BOD ultimate ($L_0$) from $BOD_5$ | $L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | Sewage strength calculation |
| Streeter-Phelps DO Sag | $D(t) = \dfrac{k_1 \cdot L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t}$ | Critical DO deficit in receiving waters |
| Dilution factor (marine outfall) | $S = \dfrac{Q_w}{Q_s}$ where $Q_s$ = seawater flow | Initial dilution of discharge plume |
| Oil weathering rate | $M(t) = M_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$ | Estimating residual oil mass after spill |
| Bioaccumulation Factor | $BAF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Heavy metal uptake in tissues |
| Biomagnification (trophic transfer) | $C_n = C_0 \cdot (BAF)^n$ | Concentration at nth trophic level |
| Half-life of pollutant | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Persistence in marine environment |
| Henry's Law (gas partitioning) | $C_w = K_H \cdot P_{gas}$ | Volatile pollutant distribution |
| Mass emission rate (point source) | $M = Q \cdot C$ (kg/day) | Loading from outfall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid table syntax errors:** In the above, vertical bars have been replaced with parentheses or `\vert` notation as required by the engine protocol.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These concepts underpin practical engineering tasks such as:
- **Outfall Diffuser Design** — determining initial dilution for municipal/industrial discharge.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** — quantifying pollutant loads for coastal projects.
- **Oil Spill Response Modeling** — predicting trajectory and weathering.
- **Marine Protected Area (MPA) Zoning** — buffering critical habitats from pollution plumes.
- **Risk Assessment** — using bioaccumulation/biomagnification models to evaluate seafood safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Model (Land-Based Organic Discharge)

The **Streeter-Phelps equation** predicts the dissolved oxygen deficit downstream of a pollutant discharge — a cornerstone of marine pollution modeling for **municipal outfalls**.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Mass Balance

Consider a river/estuary receiving organic waste with initial BOD = $L_0$ and initial DO deficit = $D_0$.

Two simultaneous processes occur as the water mass travels downstream:

- **Deoxygenation** — Microbial oxidation of organic matter consumes DO at rate $k_1$ (day$^{-1}$).
- **Reaeration** — Atmospheric oxygen dissolves into water at rate proportional to the deficit $D$, with coefficient $k_2$ (day$^{-1}$).

The governing differential equation for DO deficit $D$ is:

$$
\dfrac{dD}{dt} \;=\; k_1 \cdot L \;-\; k_2 \cdot D
$$

where $L$ is the remaining BOD at time $t$.

The remaining BOD follows first-order kinetics:

$$
L(t) \;=\; L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}
$$

### 3.1.2 Solving the Differential Equation

Substituting $L(t)$ into the deficit equation:

$$
\dfrac{dD}{dt} \;+\; k_2 \cdot D \;=\; k_1 \cdot L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}
$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE**. The integrating factor is $e^{k_2 t}$. Multiplying both sides:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(D \cdot e^{k_2 t}\right) \;=\; k_1 \cdot L_0 \cdot e^{(k_2 - k_1)\,t}
$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$
D(t) \cdot e^{k_2 t} - D_0 \;=\; k_1 \cdot L_0 \cdot \int_{0}^{t} e^{(k_2 - k_1)\,\tau}\, d\tau
$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} e^{(k_2 - k_1)\,\tau}\, d\tau \;=\; \dfrac{e^{(k_2 - k_1)\,t} - 1}{k_2 - k_1}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
D(t) \cdot e^{k_2 t} \;=\; D_0 \;+\; \dfrac{k_1 \cdot L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left(e^{(k_2 - k_1)\,t} - 1\right)
$$

Multiply by $e^{-k_2 t}$:

$$
D(t) \;=\; \dfrac{k_1 \cdot L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) \;+\; D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t}
$$

This is the celebrated **Streeter-Phelps DO Sag equation**.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A coastal outfall discharges effluent with $L_0 = 50$ mg/L BOD, $D_0 = 2$ mg/L, into a stream with $k_1 = 0.23$ day$^{-1}$, $k_2 = 0.45$ day$^{-1}$. Find the critical deficit and time-to-critical-point.

**Step 1** — Set $dD/dt = 0$ to find critical time $t_c$. Differentiating the equation:

$$
\dfrac{dD}{dt} \;=\; \dfrac{k_1 \cdot L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t}\right) - k_2 D_0 e^{-k_2 t} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 2** — Rearranging for $t_c$:

$$
k_2 \cdot \left[D_0 \,+\, \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\right] e^{-k_2 t_c} \;=\; \dfrac{k_1^2 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \, e^{-k_1 t_c}
$$

**Step 3** — Solving iteratively (or numerically):

$$
t_c \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln \left[ \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \left(1 - \dfrac{D_0(k_2-k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right) \right]
$$

Plugging values:

$$
t_c \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{0.22} \ln\left[\dfrac{0.45}{0.23}\left(1 - \dfrac{2(0.22)}{0.23 \times 50}\right)\right] \;\approx\; 2.36 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4** — Critical deficit:

$$
D_c \;\approx\; \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 t_c} \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.23 \times 50}{0.45} \cdot e^{-0.23 \times 2.36} \;\approx\; 12.4 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 5** — Interpretation: With DO saturation ~ 9 mg/L, the critical DO is $9 - 12.4 = -3.4$ mg/L → impossible, indicating **complete anoxia**. Remediation (increased reaeration or reduced BOD load) is essential.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bioaccumulation & Biomagnification

### 3.2.1 Bioaccumulation Factor (BAF)

$$
BAF \;=\; \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}
$$

where $C_{organism}$ is the contaminant concentration in the organism (mg/kg wet weight) and $C_{water}$ is the dissolved concentration (mg/L).

### 3.2.2 Biomagnification Across Trophic Levels

At each trophic transfer, the concentration is multiplied by BAF:

$$
C_n \;=\; C_0 \cdot (BAF)^{n}
$$

where $n$ = number of trophic transfers (e.g., $n = 4$ from phytoplankton → zooplankton → small fish → large fish → human).

**Example:** Methylmercury in tuna. If $C_0 = 0.001$ $\mu$g/L in seawater and BAF = 100 per trophic level, then:

$$
C_4 \;=\; 0.001 \times (100)^4 \;=\; 100,000 \,\mu g/L \;=\; 100 \,mg/L
$$

This explains why apex predators exhibit **mercury concentrations 10$^6$–10$^7$ times** higher than ambient seawater.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oil Spill Mass Balance (First-Order Weathering)

$$
M(t) \;=\; M_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha t}
$$

The oil mass $M$ remaining after time $t$ decays via combined processes — evaporation, dissolution, biodegradation, photolysis — each with its own rate constant. The composite weathering rate $\alpha$ is:

$$
\alpha \;=\; k_{evap} + k_{diss} + k_{bio} + k_{photo} + \ldots
$$

**Example:** $M_0 = 10,000$ tonnes, $\alpha = 0.05$ day$^{-1}$:

$$
M(30) \;=\; 10{,}000 \times e^{-0.05 \times 30} \;=\; 10{,}000 \times 0.223 \;\approx\; 2{,}230 \text{ tonnes}
$$

After 30 days, **~77.7% has weathered**, leaving a persistent tar-like residue — the most challenging cleanup phase.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Source → Pollutant → Impact Matrix

| Source Category | Specific Source | Key Pollutants | Marine Impact | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land – Point | Municipal sewage | Organic matter, pathogens | Eutrophication, red tides | Gulf of Mexico Dead Zone |
| Land – Point | Tanneries | Chromium (Cr VI) | Carcinogenic bioaccumulation | Kanpur → Ganges |
| Land – Point | Petrochemical plants | PAHs, phenols | Mutagenic effects, tainting | Mumbai coastal belt |
| Land – Non-point | Agricultural fertilizer | Nitrates, phosphates | Hypoxia, algal blooms | Mississippi → Gulf |
| Land – Non-point | Urban runoff | Microplastics, oils | Ingestion by marine biota | Global |
| Sea – Shipping | Tanker accident | Crude oil | Mass mortality, long-term ecology | Exxon Valdez (1989) |
| Sea – Shipping | Ballast water | Invasive species | Biodiversity loss | Caspian Sea comb jelly |
| Sea – Shipping | Anti-fouling paints | TBT | Imposex in gastropods | Global – AFS ban |
| Sea – Offshore | Drilling discharge | Drilling muds, cuttings | Benthic smothering | North Sea platforms |
| Sea – Dumping | Dredged material | Heavy metals, organics | Habitat burial | Historically global |
| Air – Industrial | Coal combustion | Mercury, SO$_2$ | Bioaccumulation, acidification | Minamata (Japan) |
| Air – Transportation | Diesel exhaust | Black carbon, NO$_x$ | Arctic ice darkening | Arctic shipping routes |
| Air – Nuclear | Tests, accidents | Cs-137, Sr-90 | Radioactive seafood | Fukushima (2011) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Classify the various sources of marine pollution. Give two examples each for point and non-point sources.**

**Model Answer:**

Marine pollution sources are classified into three principal categories:

**1. Land-Based Sources (LBS):**
- **Point Sources:** Identifiable single discharge points.
  - Examples: Municipal sewage treatment plant outfall; Industrial effluent from a petrochemical factory.
- **Non-Point (Diffuse) Sources:** Multiple, scattered origins.
  - Examples: Agricultural fertilizer runoff; Urban stormwater drainage.

**2. Sea-Based Sources (SBS):**
- Examples: Operational discharge of bilge water from cargo ships; accidental crude oil spills from tankers.

**3. Air-Based Sources (ABS):**
- Examples: Atmospheric deposition of mercury from coal combustion; SO$_2$ emissions from shipping causing ocean acidification.

> Land-based sources constitute approximately **80%** of total marine pollution globally.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the phenomenon of biomagnification with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Biomagnification** is the progressive increase in the concentration of a persistent, non-biodegradable pollutant as it moves up successive trophic levels in a food chain.

**Mechanism:** Lipophilic (fat-soluble) pollutants such as DDT, PCBs, and methylmercury are absorbed by organisms at lower trophic levels and are not excreted efficiently. When these organisms are consumed by predators, the accumulated toxins transfer and concentrate further.

**Example — Minamata Disaster (Japan, 1956):**
- Industrial discharge containing methylmercury contaminated Minamata Bay.
- Phytoplankton (10 ppb) → Zooplankton (100 ppb) → Small fish (1,000 ppb) → Large fish (10,000 ppb) → Human consumers (100,000 ppb).
- Resulted in severe neurological disorders, paralysis, and death among local fishing communities — known as **Minamata Disease**.

**Critical condition:** Bioaccumulation Factor (BAF) > 1, and pollutant must be persistent (long half-life) and lipophilic.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"Land-based sources contribute approximately 80% of marine pollution globally." Discuss in detail the various land-based sources of marine pollution, their classification, characteristic pollutants, and control measures.**

#### Part (a) — Classification and Examples (7 Marks)

**1. Magnitude of the Problem:**
According to UNEP/GESAMP estimates, **approximately 80%** of marine pollution originates from land-based activities, reaching the sea via rivers, estuaries, coastal outfalls, and atmospheric deposition.

**2. Classification of Land-Based Sources:**

**A. Point Sources** — Single, identifiable, fixed-location discharges, typically regulated by consent/permit systems.

| Type | Description | Key Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| Municipal Sewage | Urban wastewater after partial treatment | Organic matter, nutrients, pathogens, micropollutants |
| Industrial Effluents | Chemical, textile, petrochemical, tanneries | Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd, Cr), dyes, PAHs, phenols |
| Thermal Discharges | Power plant cooling water | Heat, residual chlorine, trace metals |
| Desalination Plants | RO reject stream | Hyper-saline brine (TDS 70,000+ mg/L) |
| Aquaculture | Fish/prawn farm discharge | Fecal waste, antibiotics, uneaten feed |

**B. Non-Point (Diffuse) Sources** — Multiple, scattered, event-driven, difficult to regulate.

| Type | Description | Key Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| Agricultural Runoff | Fertilizers and pesticides from farmland | Nitrates, phosphates, organochlorines |
| Urban Stormwater | Surface runoff from paved areas | Oil, microplastics, PAHs, litter |
| Mining and Construction | Erosion and sediment transport | Suspended solids, heavy metals |
| Landfill Leachate | Infiltration from waste dumps | Organic chemicals, heavy metals |
| Forestry and Land Clearing | Soil erosion and deforestation runoff | Sediment, organic debris |

**3. Transport Pathways:**
- **Rivers and Estuaries** — Principal conduits; ~ 1,000 rivers carry bulk of LBS load.
- **Coastal Direct Discharge** — Outfall pipes extending into the sea.
- **Groundwater Seepage** — Submarine groundwater discharge with contaminants.
- **Atmospheric Deposition on Land → Runoff** — Indirect pathway.

#### Part (b) — Characteristic Pollutants and Control Measures (7 Marks)

**1. Characteristic Pollutants and Their Impacts:**

| Pollutant Group | Examples | Marine Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Organic Matter (high BOD) | Sewage, food processing waste | Deoxygenation, fish kills |
| Nutrients (N, P) | Fertilizers, detergents | Eutrophication, harmful algal blooms (HABs), hypoxia |
| Pathogens | Coliforms, viruses, parasites | Shellfish contamination, human health risk |
| Heavy Metals | Hg, Pb, Cd, Cr, As | Bioaccumulation, neurotoxicity (Minamata, Itai-Itai) |
| Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) | DDT, PCBs, dioxins | Biomagnification, endocrine disruption |
| Plastics & Microplastics | Bottles, bags, microbeads | Ingestion, entanglement, chemical leaching |
| Oil & Grease | Refineries, urban runoff | Smothering of marine life |
| Radioactive Isotopes | Nuclear plant discharge | Seafood contamination |
| Thermal | Power plants | Coral bleaching, altered species composition |

**2. Control Measures:**

- **Legal Framework:** UNEP GPA (Global Programme of Action for Protection of the Marine Environment from Land-Based Activities, 1995); national legislation like India's Water Act (1974); EPA permits; EU Urban Wastewater Treatment Directive.
- **Engineering Solutions:** Coastal sewage treatment plants (primary → secondary → tertiary); industrial pre-treatment; ocean outfalls with diffusers for initial dilution; constructed wetlands for stormwater.
- **Agricultural Best Practices:** Integrated Nutrient Management (INM); precision farming; buffer strips; no-till agriculture; controlled pesticide application.
- **Monitoring:** Coastal water quality monitoring (DO, BOD, nutrients, metals); Mussel Watch programs; remote sensing for algal blooms.
- **Pollution Prevention:** Cleaner production; green chemistry; zero liquid discharge (ZLD) for industries; plastic bans and EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility).
- **Institutional:** River Basin Management; Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM); MARPOL Convention Annex IV (sewage) and Annex V (garbage).

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Discuss in detail the sea-based sources of marine pollution. Explain the role of shipping, offshore oil & gas operations, and ocean dumping. Mention relevant international conventions.**

#### Part (a) — Shipping-Related Marine Pollution (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction:**
Maritime transport carries ~ **80% of global trade by volume**, making shipping a major sea-based pollution source. While individual incidents can be catastrophic, cumulative operational discharges pose a chronic threat.

**2. Categories of Shipping Pollution:**

| Source | Type of Pollutant | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Operational — Bilge water | Oily waste (typically 15–200 ppm oil) | Chronic, widespread |
| Accidental — Tanker spills | Crude/refined oil (bulk release) | Acute, catastrophic |
| Ballast water | Invasive species, pathogens | Ecological disruption |
| Anti-fouling paints | TBT (tributyltin), copper biocides | Imposex, endocrine disruption |
| Container loss | Plastic pellets (nurdles), packaged goods | Localized but persistent |
| Sewage & greywater | Organic matter, nutrients | Coastal pollution |
| Air emissions | SO$_x$, NO$_x$, CO$_2$, PM | Ocean acidification, climate |
| Garbage & plastics | All waste types | Solid marine litter |

**3. Case Studies (Examiner Favorites):**

- **Torrey Canyon (1967)** — 119,000 tonnes crude oil spilled off UK; led to founding of **IMCO** (precursor to IMO).
- **Exxon Valdez (1989, Alaska)** — 37,000 tonnes spilled; largest US spill until Deepwater Horizon.
- **MV X-Press Pearl (2021, Sri Lanka)** — Burned container ship released ~ **1,680 tonnes of plastic pellets (nurdles)**; one of the worst marine plastic disasters.
- **MV Wakashio (2020, Mauritius)** — Bulk carrier grounding released ~ 1,000 tonnes of fuel oil; declared environmental emergency.

**4. TBT and Imposex:**
Tributyltin, used in anti-fouling paints to prevent biofouling on ship hulls, causes **imposex** — superimposition of male sex organs onto female gastropods (e.g., *Nucella lapillus*), leading to reproductive failure and population collapse. Banned globally by **IMO AFS Convention (2001)**, in force from **September 2008**.

#### Part (b) — Offshore Oil & Gas, Ocean Dumping, and Conventions (7 Marks)

**1. Offshore Oil and Gas Operations:**

- **Drilling Muds and Cuttings** — Water-based, oil-based, or synthetic-based fluids used during drilling; cuttings are rock fragments coated with muds. Discharge can smother benthic communities.
- **Produced Water** — Formation water brought up with crude oil; largest volumetric waste stream from offshore platforms. Contains dissolved hydrocarbons, heavy metals, and naturally occurring radioactive materials (NORMs).
- **Accidental Blowouts** — Example: **Deepwater Horizon (April 20, 2010)** released an estimated **4.9 million barrels** of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over 87 days — the largest marine oil spill in history.

**2. Ocean Dumping:**

Governed by the **London Convention (1972)** and its successor **London Protocol (1996)**.

**Permitted Dumping:**
- Dredged material (with environmental assessment)
- Sewage sludge
- Fish waste from processing
- Vessels and platforms (deliberate sinking)

**Prohibited Dumping:**
- Industrial waste
- Radioactive waste
- Organohalogen compounds
- Crude oil, heavy metals
- Plastics and persistent litter
- Chemical and biological warfare agents

**3. Key International Conventions Governing Sea-Based Pollution:**

| Convention | Year | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **MARPOL 73/78** | 1973/1978 | Prevention of pollution from ships (6 Annexes) |
| **IMO AFS Convention** | 2001 | Control of harmful anti-fouling systems (TBT ban) |
| **BWM Convention** | 2004 | Ballast water management (D-2 standard) |
| **London Convention** | 1972 | Ocean dumping control |
| **London Protocol** | 1996 | Precautionary approach to dumping |
| **OPRC Convention** | 1990 | Oil pollution preparedness, response, cooperation |
| **Intervention Convention** | 1969 | Right to intervene in high seas oil spills |

**4. Mitigation Strategies:**
- Double-hulled tankers (mandatory after Exxon Valdez).
- Segregated ballast tanks.
- Oil Spill Contingency Plans (OSCP) mandatory for ports and terminals.
- Ballast water exchange or treatment (BWMS with UV/filtration).
- TBT-free coatings (silicone, copper-free).
- Real-time satellite monitoring (AIS, Sentinel-1 SAR for oil slick detection).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Marine Pollution Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Point and Non-Point sources** — Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if students give industrial effluent as a non-point source or agricultural runoff as a point source. Memorize: **Point = Single Outlet**, **Non-Point = Scattered/Event-driven**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 80% Land-Based statistic** — Always cite UNEP's **80%** figure when asked about dominant pollution source.
>
> 3. **Confusing MARPOL Annexes** — Annex I = Oil; II = Noxious Liquid Substances; III = Packaged Harmful Substances; IV = Sewage; V = Garbage; VI = Air Pollution. Examiners specifically test Annex mapping.
>
> 4. **Omitting conventions in long answers** — A 14-mark answer on sea-based sources that does NOT mention MARPOL or London Convention typically loses **3–4 marks**.
>
> 5. **Mixing up TBT ban dates** — AFS Convention adopted 2001, entered into force **September 17, 2008**. Complete ban from January 1, 2008 for new applications.
>
> 6. **Using vague examples** — Cite specific incidents (Deepwater Horizon 2010, Exxon Valdez 1989, Minamata 1956) rather than generic "an oil spill."
>
> 7. **Skipping numeric estimates** — Always quantify: 80% LBS, 18% shipping oil, 4.9 million barrels Deepwater Horizon, etc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Three Principal Sources:** Land-Based (~80%), Sea-Based, Air-Based.

✅ **Land-Based = Point Sources + Non-Point Sources.** Point = single identifiable outlet (e.g., factory outfall). Non-point = diffuse, scattered (e.g., agricultural runoff).

✅ **Major Sea-Based Pollutants:** Oil (bilge + accidental), ballast water (invasive species), TBT (imposex), nurdles (plastic pellets), produced water, drilling muds.

✅ **Key Incidents to Remember:**
- **Minamata (1956, Japan)** — Methylmercury, neurological disease.
- **Itai-Itai (Japan, 1950s–60s)** — Cadmium, bone disease.
- **Exxon Valdez (1989, Alaska)** — 37,000 tonnes oil.
- **Deepwater Horizon (2010, Gulf of Mexico)** — 4.9 million barrels oil.
- **Fukushima (2011, Japan)** — Cs-137, Sr-90 radioactive release.
- **MV X-Press Pearl (2021, Sri Lanka)** — Plastic nurdle disaster.

✅ **Critical Conventions:**
- **MARPOL 73/78** — 6 Annexes covering ship-source pollution.
- **London Convention (1972) + London Protocol (1996)** — Ocean dumping.
- **UNEP GPA (1995)** — Global Programme of Action on LBS.
- **AFS Convention (2001)** — Anti-fouling systems (TBT ban).
- **BWM Convention (2004)** — Ballast water management.

✅ **Tributyltin (TBT):** Causes **imposex** in marine snails. Banned globally. Came from anti-fouling paints.

✅ **Biomagnification vs. Bioaccumulation:**
- *Bioaccumulation* — Buildup in a single organism over time.
- *Biomagnification* — Concentration increase across trophic levels.

✅ **Streeter-Phelps Equation:** Predicts dissolved oxygen deficit downstream of organic waste discharge; critical for designing marine outfalls and assessing assimilative capacity.

✅ **Ocean Acidification:** Surface ocean pH dropped from ~ 8.2 (pre-industrial) to ~ 8.1 today — a 26% increase in hydrogen ion concentration. Driven by atmospheric CO$_2$ absorption.

✅ **Eutrophication Drivers:** Excess N and P from agricultural runoff + sewage → algal blooms → hypoxia → fish kills → "Dead Zones" (e.g., Gulf of Mexico ~ 16,000 km² annually).

✅ **Plastics in Ocean:** ~ 8 million tonnes of plastic enter oceans annually (Jambeck et al., 2015 estimate); by 2050, oceans may contain more plastic than fish by weight (WEF projection).

✅ **Quick Formula Recall:**
- $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ (first-order decay)
- $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$
- $L_0 = BOD_5 / (1 - e^{-5k})$
- BAF and Biomagnification power law: $C_n = C_0 (BAF)^n$

✅ **Exam Strategy:** Always answer in **classification → examples → pollutants → impact → control/conventions** framework for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coastal Zone:** The interface region where land meets the sea, defined under the Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification, 2019 (India) as the area extending up to **500 meters** from the High Tide Line (HTL) on the landward side and **12 nautical miles** into the sea on the seaward side.

The **coastal zone** is a transitional ecosystem comprising:
- **Littoral zone** (intertidal area between high and low tide)
- **Sub-littoral zone** (permanently submerged shallow zone)
- **Estuarine zone** (brackish water transition where rivers meet the sea)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone** refers to the introduction by human activity (directly or indirectly) of substances or energy into the marine coastal environment resulting in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, impairment of seawater quality, and reduction of amenity values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of the coastal zone as the **"kitchen sink of civilization."** Just like everything you wash down your kitchen sink ends up in the drainage system, nearly **80% of all marine pollution originates from land-based activities** and flows through the coastal zone before reaching the open ocean. The coastal zone is the **final collection point** where:
- Rivers dump their accumulated waste
- Cities discharge their sewage
- Industries release their effluents
- Ships deposit their ballast and bilge water
- Tourists leave their litter on beaches

Because coastal waters are **shallow, semi-enclosed, and poorly flushed**, pollutants accumulate here far more severely than in the open ocean. This is analogous to a **stagnant pond versus a flowing river** — pollutants stay longer and concentrate more.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics (Bold for Emphasis):**
> - **CRZ-I (Ecologically Sensitive Areas):** No development permitted
> - **CRZ-II (Developed Urban Areas):** Limited construction up to 200 m from HTL
> - **CRZ-III (Rural Areas):** Development permitted with 50 m setback from HTL
> - **CRZ-IV (Water Area):** Regulated aquatic activities only
> - **Coastal population density (global):** ~150 people/km² (vs ~50/km² global average)
> - **Pollution load from coasts:** ~80% of global marine pollution load

## 1.4 Classification of Coastal Pollution Sources

| Source Category | Examples | % Contribution (Global Avg.) |
|----------------|----------|------------------------------|
| Municipal Sewage | Domestic wastewater | ~35% |
| Industrial Discharge | Chemicals, heavy metals | ~25% |
| Agricultural Runoff | Fertilizers, pesticides | ~20% |
| Maritime Activities | Bilge, ballast, oil | ~10% |
| Tourism & Plastic Litter | Beach debris | ~5% |
| Aquaculture | Feed, antibiotics | ~5% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The coastal zone pollution phenomenon can be understood through **three operational pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Source Pathway Analysis
Pollutants reach coastal waters through **six primary pathways**:

1. **Riverine Discharge** — The dominant pathway; rivers act as "conveyor belts" carrying land-based contaminants. The Ganges-Brahmaputra, Nile, and Yangtze alone discharge ~**2.5 million tonnes of plastic annually** to the ocean.

2. **Direct Discharge (Point Sources)** — Outfalls from sewage treatment plants, industrial pipelines, and desalination brine discharge.

3. **Diffuse (Non-Point) Sources** — Agricultural runoff, urban stormwater, atmospheric deposition.

4. **Atmospheric Deposition** — Airborne particulates, heavy metals (Pb, Hg), and persistent organic pollutants (POPs) carried by wind currents.

5. **Maritime Activities** — Vessel discharges (bilge water, ballast), port operations, ship-breaking yards.

6. **Coastal Aquaculture** — Nutrient-rich effluents from shrimp/fish farms.

### Pillar 2: Pollutant Categories in Coastal Waters

> [!NOTE]
> **Nine Major Pollutant Classes in Coastal Zones (GESAMP Classification):**

| # | Pollutant Class | Coastal Examples | Persistence |
|---|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Nutrients** (N, P) | Sewage, fertilizers | Days-Weeks |
| 2 | **Heavy Metals** | Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr | Decades-Centuries |
| 3 | **Petroleum Hydrocarbons** | Oil spills, runoff | Weeks-Years |
| 4 | **Pesticides / POPs** | DDT, PCBs, Dioxins | Decades |
| 5 | **Plastics & Microplastics** | Bags, microbeads | Centuries |
| 6 | **Sewage Pathogens** | E. coli, Vibrio | Days |
| 7 | **Sediments** | Construction runoff | Variable |
| 8 | **Radioactive Substances** | Nuclear plants | Centuries-Millennia |
| 9 | **Thermal Discharge** | Power plant cooling | Hours |

### Pillar 3: Coastal Zone Vulnerability Index (CZVI)

The coastal zone's susceptibility is governed by:

$$\text{CZVI} = f(\text{Hydrodynamics}, \text{Topography}, \text{Biotic Sensitivity}, \text{Pollution Load})$$

Semi-enclosed coastal water bodies like the **Baltic Sea, Mediterranean Sea, Persian Gulf, and Bay of Bengal** have **high CZVI** due to restricted circulation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Dilution Factor** | $D_f = \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{effluent}} = \dfrac{Q_{river} + Q_{effluent}}{Q_{effluent}}$ | Dimensionless | Estuary mixing |
| 2 | **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** | $\text{BOD}_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)$ | mg O₂/L | Organic pollution |
| 3 | **Langmuir Sorption (Heavy Metals on Sediments)** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}$ | mg/g | Metal partitioning |
| 4 | **Tidal Prism (Volume Exchange)** | $P = A \cdot \Delta h$ | m³ | Flushing capacity |
| 5 | **Flushing Time** | $T_f = \dfrac{V}{Q_{freshwater} + Q_{tidal}}$ | Days | Residence time |
| 6 | **Salinity Mixing (Estuary)** | $S_{mix} = \dfrac{Q_r S_r + Q_s S_s}{Q_r + Q_s}$ | PSU | Stratification |
| 7 | **Fick's First Law (Diffusion)** | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | mol/m²·s | Contaminant flux |
| 8 | **Eutrophication Index (TRIX)** | $\text{TRIX} = \dfrac{\log_{10}(Chl\,a \cdot \vert\%O_2\vert \cdot \text{TP} \cdot \text{TN} + 1.5)}{1.2}$ | 0–5 scale | Coastal trophic state |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Marine Engineering & Coastal Management:**
> - **Coastal Engineers** use these formulas for designing breakwaters, outfalls, and dredging schedules.
> - **Port Authorities** apply dilution/dispersion models for ballast water discharge permits.
> - **Environmental Consultants** use CZVI and TRIX to designate Marine Protected Areas (MPAs).
> - **Pollution Control Boards (CPCB/SPCB)** rely on BOD, COD, and heavy metal limits under the **EPA, 1986** and **Water Act, 1974**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Coastal Flushing Time

The flushing time ($T_f$) determines how long a pollutant persists in a coastal embayment.

### Step 1: Define the Control Volume
Consider a coastal embayment (e.g., a bay or estuary) of volume $V$.

### Step 2: Mass Balance Equation

The rate of change of pollutant mass in the embayment equals input minus output plus reactions:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = Q_{in} C_{in} - Q_{out} C_{out} - k M$$

Where:
- $M$ = mass of pollutant (kg)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $C$ = concentration (kg/m³)
- $k$ = first-order decay constant (s⁻¹)

### Step 3: Apply Steady-State Assumption

At steady state ($dM/dt = 0$):

$$Q_{in} C_{in} = Q_{out} C_{out} + k M$$

### Step 4: Define Flushing Time

By definition, flushing time is the ratio of volume to total outflow rate:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{V}{Q_{out}} \\
T_f &= \frac{V}{Q_{river} + Q_{tidal\,exchange}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of logic:** The volume $V$ is "renewed" each cycle, so the time required to replace the entire volume by inflow is the flushing time.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A small coastal bay has volume $V = 5 \times 10^6$ m³. River inflow $Q_r = 50$ m³/s. Tidal exchange volume per cycle = $2 \times 10^5$ m³, with 2 tidal cycles per day.

**Step A:** Convert tidal flow to m³/s.

$$Q_{tidal} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{cycle} \times 2 \, \text{cycles/day}}{86400 \, \text{s/day}}$$

$$Q_{tidal} = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{86400} \approx 4.63 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step B:** Total outflow rate.

$$Q_{out} = Q_r + Q_{tidal} = 50 + 4.63 = 54.63 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step C:** Compute flushing time.

$$T_f = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{54.63} \approx 91522 \, \text{s} \approx 25.4 \, \text{hours} \approx 1.06 \, \text{days}$$

**Interpretation:** A pollutant dumped today will be ~63% flushed out within 1 day. A **longer flushing time = greater pollution accumulation risk.**

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: BOD Kinetics in Coastal Waters

### Step 1: Statement
The oxygen depletion by organic matter follows first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L$$

Where $L$ = remaining oxygen demand at time $t$.

### Step 2: Integrate

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 t$$

### Step 3: Express BOD at time $t$

$$\text{BOD}_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

Given: $L_0 = 250$ mg/L (ultimate BOD), $k_1 = 0.23$ /day (typical coastal seawater), find $\text{BOD}_5$.

$$\text{BOD}_5 = 250 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}\right)$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = 250 \times \left(1 - e^{-1.15}\right)$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = 250 \times (1 - 0.3166)$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = 250 \times 0.6834 \approx 170.85 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Conclusion:** This BOD value far exceeds the **CPCB coastal water standard of 3 mg/L for bathing waters**, indicating severe organic pollution — requiring immediate remedial action.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant Behavior in Coastal Zones

| Pollutant | Dominant Pathway | Residence Behavior | Critical Coastal Impact |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Heavy Metals | Riverine + Atmospheric | Adsorbed on sediments (resuspendable) | Bioaccumulation in shellfish |
| Nutrients (N, P) | Sewage + Agricultural | Dissolved, transported by currents | Eutrophication, algal blooms |
| Petroleum Hydrocarbons | Spills + Urban runoff | Surface slick, evaporation | Mangrove/coastal bird mortality |
| Microplastics | Rivers + Beach litter | Buoyant, persistent | Ingestion by marine fauna |
| Sewage Pathogens | Direct outfall | Rapid decay in saline water | Beach closures, shellfish bans |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) and list its four classification categories with permitted activities.**

**Model Answer:**
The Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) under the CRZ Notification, 2019 governs activities along India's coastline. The four categories are:
1. **CRZ-I (Ecologically Sensitive Areas):** Includes national parks, marine parks, coral reefs, mangroves, and genetically important breeding grounds. **No new construction permitted.**
2. **CRZ-II (Developed Urban Areas):** Areas developed up to or close to the shoreline within municipal limits. **Reconstruction allowed with 200 m setback from HTL.**
3. **CRZ-III (Rural Areas):** Relatively undisturbed rural coastal zones. **Construction permitted with 50 m setback from HTL.**
4. **CRZ-IV (Water Area):** Includes tidal influenced water bodies up to 12 nautical miles. **Only permitted activities like fishing, navigation, and permitted desalination allowed.**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Point Source and Non-Point Source pollution in the coastal zone with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Point Source | Non-Point Source (Diffuse) |
|---------|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Definition** | Pollution from a single, identifiable discharge point | Pollution from multiple, dispersed sources |
| **Identification** | Easily traced to a pipe/outfall | Difficult to trace |
| **Control** | Easier to regulate and monitor | Difficult to control |
| **Examples (Coastal)** | Sewage treatment plant outfall, industrial effluent pipe, desalination brine discharge | Agricultural runoff carrying fertilizers, urban stormwater runoff, atmospheric deposition of POPs |
| **Regulation** | Permits and effluent limits (CPCB) | Best Management Practices (BMPs) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the major **land-based sources of marine pollution** in the coastal zone, with examples specific to the Indian coastline.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **eutrophication** as a coastal pollution phenomenon. Describe the mechanism, consequences, and two case studies (one Indian, one global).

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Introduction:**
Approximately **80% of marine pollution originates from land-based activities**. The coastal zone receives the cumulative discharge of rivers, cities, industries, and agricultural regions.

**Major Land-Based Sources:**

1. **Municipal Sewage Discharge:**
   - Untreated/partially treated domestic wastewater containing organic matter, nutrients (N, P), pathogens, and pharmaceuticals.
   - Indian example: Mumbai's coastal outfalls release ~**2700 MLD** of treated/untreated sewage into the Arabian Sea.

2. **Industrial Effluents:**
   - Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr), dyes, phenols, and PCBs from tanneries, petrochemicals, refineries.
   - Indian example: Chrompet industrial area discharging Cr into the Palar estuary (Tamil Nadu).

3. **Agricultural Runoff:**
   - Fertilizer-driven nitrogen and phosphorus; pesticides (DDT, Endosulfan — though banned, persistent).
   - Indian example: Kerala's Vembanad Lake experiences severe eutrophication from coconut and paddy runoff.

4. **Plastic and Solid Waste:**
   - Rivers carry an estimated **0.8 to 2.7 million tonnes/year** of plastic to India's coastal waters.
   - Urban centers like Chennai and Kochi contribute via the Adyar and Periyar rivers respectively.

5. **Tourism Waste:**
   - Beach resorts release sewage, food waste, and litter directly to coastal waters.

**Conclusion:**
Comprehensive management requires **integrated coastal zone management (ICZM)** with watershed-level controls.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Definition:**
**Eutrophication** is the excessive enrichment of water bodies with nutrients (especially N and P), leading to algal blooms, oxygen depletion, and ecosystem collapse.

**Mechanism (Step-by-step):**
1. Nutrient loading from sewage/agriculture enters coastal waters.
2. Phytoplankton populations explode (algal bloom).
3. Light penetration decreases, harming benthic flora.
4. When algae die, aerobic bacteria decompose them, consuming dissolved oxygen.
5. Hypoxic (DO < 2 mg/L) or anoxic (DO = 0) conditions develop.
6. Marine life suffocates — **"dead zones"** form.

**Consequences:**
- Fish kills, biodiversity loss, harmful algal toxins (red tides), public health hazards.

**Case Study 1 — Indian: Vembanad Lake, Kerala**
- A Ramsar site experiencing severe eutrophication due to coconut plantation runoff, urban sewage, and tourism.
- Documented hypoxic events and clam mortality.

**Case Study 2 — Global: Gulf of Mexico Dead Zone**
- Formed annually due to Mississippi River nutrient runoff.
- Size in 2023: ~**6,500 km²** — incapable of supporting marine life.

**Conclusion:**
Eutrophication is the **most widespread coastal pollution issue** and demands watershed-level nutrient management.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **sources, fate, and effects of heavy metal pollution** in the coastal zone, with emphasis on bioaccumulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of plastics and microplastics** as emerging coastal pollutants. Describe their sources, distribution, and ecological impacts.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Introduction:**
Heavy metals are among the most persistent coastal pollutants due to their **non-biodegradable nature** and tendency to accumulate in sediments and biota.

**Major Sources in Coastal Zones:**
- **Industrial effluents** — Tanneries (Cr), battery plants (Pb), chlor-alkali plants (Hg).
- **Mining and smelting** — Coastal Goa, Gujarat (Fe, Mn, Cu).
- **Atmospheric deposition** — Coal combustion (Hg, As), vehicular emissions (Pb).
- **Agricultural runoff** — Phosphate fertilizers contain Cd as a contaminant.
- **Antifouling paints** — TBT (tributyltin) on ship hulls.

**Fate in Coastal Waters:**
- Dissolved metals adsorb onto suspended particulate matter (SPM).
- Settle onto sediments, becoming **reservoirs of contamination**.
- Remobilized during dredging or storm events — secondary pollution.
- Bioaccumulated in filter feeders (mussels, oysters) and transferred up the food chain.

**Bioaccumulation & Biomagnification:**
$$\text{Bioaccumulation Factor (BAF)} = \frac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$$
$$\text{Biomagnification Factor (BMF)} = \frac{C_{predator}}{C_{prey}}$$

**Example:** Mercury biomagnifies up to **10⁶-fold** from water to top predatory fish, causing Minamata disease in humans.

**Coastal Impacts:**
- Shellfish contamination → export bans
- Mangrove dieback
- Fish kills and reproductive failure

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Definition:**
**Plastics** are synthetic polymer-based materials that persist in marine environments for **hundreds to thousands of years**. **Microplastics** are particles <5 mm in size.

**Sources:**
1. **Primary microplastics:** Microbeads in cosmetics, industrial abrasives, plastic pellets (nurdles).
2. **Secondary microplastics:** Fragmentation of larger plastic debris via UV radiation and wave action.

**Coastal Distribution:**
- Concentrate in **gyres** (Great Pacific Garbage Patch — ~1.6 million km²).
- Accumulate on beaches and in sediments.
- **Indian beaches:** Chennai's Marina Beach and Mumbai's Juhu report microplastic densities >100 particles/m².

**Ecological Impacts:**
- **Ingestion:** Sea turtles confuse bags with jellyfish; filter feeders ingest microplastics.
- **Chemical leaching:** Plastics release BPA, phthalates, and adsorbed POPs (DDT, PCBs).
- **Microplastic vector effect:** Acts as substrate for microbial biofilms including pathogens.
- **Trophic transfer:** Microplastics move up food webs to commercially important fish consumed by humans.

**Regulatory Response:**
- India announced a ban on identified single-use plastics in **2022**.
- UNEA adopted a resolution to negotiate a **Global Plastics Treaty**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse CRZ classifications** — students often write outdated CRZ-2011 norms. Always use **CRZ Notification, 2019**.
> 2. **Numerical problems on BOD, flushing time, and dilution factor** demand unit conversions (s to days, mL to L). Mismatch is the #1 reason for losing 2-3 marks in Part B derivations.
> 3. **For eutrophication questions**, students forget to mention **hypoxia thresholds** (DO < 2 mg/L) — explicitly stating numerical thresholds fetches full marks.
> 4. **For plastic questions**, including both **primary and secondary microplastics** is mandatory — partial answers lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Indian case studies (Vembanad, Mumbai outfalls, Chennai Marina)** score significantly higher than generic global examples. Examiners reward contextual application.
> 6. **Always cite the Act/Notification year** (Water Act 1974, EPA 1986, CRZ 2019, MARPOL 73/78) — bare statements without legal backing lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Coastal Zone Definition:** Land–sea interface up to **500 m landward** and **12 nautical miles seaward**.
- **CRZ 2019 Classification:** CRZ-I (ESA), CRZ-II (Urban), CRZ-III (Rural), CRZ-IV (Water).
- **Pollution Sources:** ~80% land-based, ~10% maritime, ~10% atmospheric.
- **Major Pollutant Classes:** 9 GESAMP categories (nutrients, metals, hydrocarbons, POPs, plastics, pathogens, sediments, radioactive, thermal).
- **Eutrophication Mechanism:** Nutrient loading → algal bloom → decomposition → hypoxia → dead zones.
- **Heavy Metals:** Persistent, adsorb on sediments, bioaccumulate up food chain (Hg biomagnifies 10⁶×).
- **Microplastics:** <5 mm particles; primary (microbeads) and secondary (degraded); persistent for centuries.
- **Flushing Time Formula:** $T_f = V / (Q_r + Q_{tidal})$ — longer flushing = higher pollution risk.
- **BOD Equation:** $\text{BOD}_t = L_0 (1 - e^{-k_1 t})$ — first-order kinetics.
- **Key Indian Acts:** Water Act 1974, EPA 1986, CRZ 2019, MARPOL Convention.
- **Case Studies to Remember:** Vembanad Lake (Kerala), Mumbai sewage outfalls, Gulf of Mexico Dead Zone, Minamata (Japan — Hg), Great Pacific Garbage Patch.
- **Coastal Vulnerability:** Semi-enclosed seas (Baltic, Mediterranean, Bay of Bengal) have highest CZVI.
- **Sorption:** Langmuir isotherms govern heavy metal partitioning to sediments.
- **Threshold for Hypoxia:** DO < 2 mg/L; Anoxia: DO = 0 mg/L.
- **Bathymetric/Legal Boundary:** Territorial waters = 12 nautical miles; EEZ = 200 nautical miles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Levels Mapped (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy — KTU 2024):**
> - *Remember/Understand:* CRZ classification, pollutant types
> - *Apply:* BOD calculations, flushing time
> - *Analyze:* Eutrophication mechanism, heavy metal pathways
> - *Evaluate:* Case study comparison, regulatory framework
> - *Create:* ICZM plans, pollution mitigation strategies

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution from Ships and Offshore Assets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pollution from Ships and Offshore Assets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**
> *Pollution from ships and offshore assets* refers to the deliberate, accidental, or operational release of harmful substances — including hydrocarbons, noxious liquids, sewage, garbage, ballast-borne invasive species, atmospheric exhaust gases, and drilling/production discharges — into the marine environment from commercial vessels, offshore oil & gas platforms, Floating Production Storage and Offloading (FPSO) units, drilling rigs, and ancillary subsea infrastructure.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating City" Model

Imagine a modern merchant vessel (say, a 300 m VLCC) as a **self-contained floating city**. Like any city, it generates waste — but unlike a city on land, it has nowhere to dump that waste except into the ocean. Now imagine an offshore oil platform as a **small industrial township bolted to the seabed** — it drills, refines, separates oil from water, and burns diesel continuously. Both are essentially pollution factories the moment we ignore their effluent streams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** The KTU paper typically expects students to *categorize* pollution sources (point vs. non-point, chronic vs. acute, operational vs. accidental). Memorize the **6 MARPOL Annexes** as the regulatory backbone.

### 1.2 Classification of Ship-Sourced Pollution

| Source Category | Typical Pollutants | Nature of Release |
|---|---|---|
| **Operational** | Bilge oil, ballast water, sewage, gray water, garbage | Continuous, chronic |
| **Accidental** | Oil spills (tank ruptures, collisions, groundings) | Sudden, catastrophic |
| **Airborne** | SOₓ, NOₓ, CO₂, particulate matter (PM), black carbon | Continuous via stack |
| **Anti-fouling** | Biocides (TBT, copper compounds), antifouling paint leachates | Continuous, hull-derived |

### 1.3 Classification of Offshore Asset Pollution

| Platform Type | Primary Pollutants | Source Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Platform (Steel Jacket)** | Drill cuttings, mud, produced water, oil | Drilling & separation train |
| **FPSO (Floating Production)** | Produced water, oil sheen, gas flaring | On-board processing |
| **Semi-Submersible Rig** | Diesel exhaust, drilling fluids, brine | Power generation + drilling |
| **Subsea Manifold** | Slow seepage, hydrate inhibitor leaks | Slow chronic leakage |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

- **1 nautical mile (nm)** = **1852 m**
- **1 barrel (oil)** ≈ **0.159 m³** ≈ **159 L**
- **API Gravity** (industry standard for crude oil density):

$$ \text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{\text{SG}_{\text{oil}}} - 131.5 $$

where SG_oil is the specific gravity of oil relative to water at **15.6 °C**.

> [!TIP]
> Crude oils with **°API > 31** are *light* (evaporate fast, form persistent slicks); **°API < 22** are *heavy* (form emulsions, persist for years). Always quote this in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Discharge Pathway of a Tanker

The pollution chain follows a strict operational sequence that KTU examiners love to test:

1. **Loading** → vapor emissions (VOC release to atmosphere via ullage space).
2. **Ballast uptake** → ballast water contamination with residual oil; cross-contamination risk.
3. **Voyage** → antifouling leaching, SOₓ/NOₓ from main engine.
4. **Unloading** → slop tank generation, line-flushing residues.
5. **Tank washing** → high-volume oily wastewater → slop tank → OWS (Oily Water Separator).
6. **Discharge at sea** → regulated under **MARPOL Annex I Reg. 34** (oil content ≤ **15 ppm** instantaneous).

### 2.2 The Six MARPOL Annexes — KTU Cheat Sheet

| Annex | Subject | Key Discharge Limit | KTU Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Oil | ≤ 15 ppm in bilge discharge | "1-5 = Oil 15" |
| **II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) | Categorized A–D | "A is the worst" |
| **III** | Harmful Substances in Packaged Form | Marking & stowage rules | "Package = P" |
| **IV** | Sewage | No discharge within 4 nm | "4 nm = Sewage" |
| **V** | Garbage | No plastic discharge anywhere | "Plastic = zero" |
| **VI** | Air Pollution | S ≤ 0.5% (outside ECAs), 0.1% (ECA) | "Sulfur 5 → 0.5 → 0.1" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Emission Control Areas (ECAs):** Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American (incl. US Caribbean), and the **Mediterranean Sea (designated 2024, effective in stages)**. Memorize the SOₓ cap reduction: **3.5% (2020) → 0.5% global, 0.1% ECA**.

### 2.3 Offshore Drilling Fluid Discharge Physics

Drilling fluids (muds) are classified into two categories, each with very different pollution footprints:

- **Water-Based Muds (WBM)** — density controlled using **barite (BaSO₄)**. Lower toxicity, generally discharged offshore after screening.
- **Oil-Based Muds (OBM) / Synthetic-Based Muds (SBM)** — banned for discharge in many jurisdictions (e.g., **OSPAR**); cuttings must be shipped ashore.

The cuttings retention on shaker screens follows a basic mass-balance:

$$ m_{\text{cuttings, retained}} = m_{\text{feed}} \cdot (1 - \eta_{\text{shaker}}) $$

where **η_shaker** is the screening efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95 for mesh sizes between **120–200 µm**).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (No Pipe-Symbol Conflicts)

| Equation | Formula | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fay Stage 1 (Gravity-Inertia)** | $R_1 = K_1 \cdot (g \cdot \Delta V \, t^{2})^{1/4}$ | R = slick radius [m], g = 9.81 m/s², Δ = density difference [kg/m³], V = volume [m³], t = time [s] | Initial rapid spread driven by gravity vs. inertia |
| **Fay Stage 2 (Gravity-Viscous)** | $R_2 = K_2 \cdot (g \cdot \Delta^{2} V^{2} t^{3/2} / \nu^{1/2})^{1/6}$ | ν = kinematic viscosity of seawater ≈ 1×10⁻⁶ m²/s | Spreading dominated by viscous dissipation |
| **Fay Stage 3 (Surface Tension)** | $R_3 = K_3 \cdot (\sigma \, t^{2} / \rho_{w}^{2} \nu)^{1/4}$ | σ = surface tension ≈ 0.025 N/m, ρ_w = 1025 kg/m³ | Final thin film governed by interfacial tension |
| **Empirical Constant Range** | K₁ ≈ 1.21, K₂ ≈ 1.45, K₃ ≈ 1.60 | Dimensionless | Fay (1969, 1971) experimentally derived |
| **Oil Slick Thickness** | $h = V / (\pi R^{2})$ | h [m], V [m³], R [m] | Average film thickness at any stage |
| **API Gravity** | $\text{API} = 141.5/\text{SG} - 131.5$ | SG = specific gravity | Classification: light > 31, heavy < 22 |
| **Mass of Oil Spilled (Estimation)** | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | ρ ≈ 850–950 kg/m³ | Total pollutant mass |
| **Drilling Cuttings Volume** | $V_c = (\pi/4) \cdot D^{2} \cdot L \cdot (1 - \phi)$ | D = hole diameter, L = length drilled, φ = porosity | Theoretical cuttings generation |
| **Produced Water Oil-in-Water** | $\text{OIW} = C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}$ | mg/L, target ≤ 30 mg/L (OSPAR) | Hydrocyclone / flotation efficiency |
| **Stack Gas SOₓ Conversion** | $\text{SO}_{x\,[\text{g/kWh}]} = 2 \cdot \text{Fuel-S}_{[\%]} \cdot \text{SFC}$ | SFC = specific fuel consumption [g/kWh] | IMO MARPOL Annex VI compliance check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fay equations are **time-regime dependent**. Always state the regime before applying: Stage 1 dominates for **t < ~1 hour**, Stage 2 for **1 hr < t < ~100 hr**, Stage 3 for **t > ~100 hr**. KTU questions often give a time and ask which stage applies.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil spill contingency planning**: Fay's equations feed into NOAA's **ADIOS (Automated Data Inquiry for Oil Spills)** model used by Indian Coast Guard.
- **Platform decommissioning**: Produced water toxicity drives OSPAR-compliant reinjection vs. discharge decisions.
- **ECA designation impact**: A vessel switching to **0.1% S fuel** reduces SOₓ mass by ~**97%** vs. HFO (3.5% S).
- **Drill cuttings transport modeling**: Stokes' Law settling velocity,

$$ w_s = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_w) g \, d^{2}}{18 \mu} $$

where μ = dynamic viscosity, d = particle diameter, predicts the **near-field deposition footprint** around a rig.
