| Antarctic melt | Mass addition | + | + (far field), – (near field GIA) | Centuries–millennia |
| Mountain glacier melt | Mass addition | + | + | Decadal |
| Groundwater depletion | Mass addition (indirect) | + | + | Decadal |
| Reservoir impoundment | Mass removal | – | – | Decadal |
| Glacial Isostatic Adjustment | Land rebound | 0 | – (rebounding), + (peripheral) | Millennia |
| Tectonic uplift | Land motion | 0 | – | Millennia |
| Subsidence (local) | Land sink | 0 | + | Decadal |
| Salinity decrease (halosteric) | Density drop | + | + | Decadal |
| Salinity increase | Density rise | – | – | Decadal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow-Chart of Sea Level Change Drivers

```mermaid
graph TD
    SLC[SEA LEVEL CHANGE] --> EU[EUSTATIC GLOBAL VOLUME]
    SLC --> ISO[ISOSTATIC LOCAL LAND MOTION]
    EU --> ST[STERIC THERMAL HALINE]
    EU --> MS[MASS EXCHANGE ICE GROUNDWATER]
    EU --> DY[DYNAMIC OCEAN CIRCULATION]
    ISO --> GIA[GLACIAL ISOSTATIC ADJUSTMENT]
    ISO --> TC[TECTONIC UPLIFT SUBSIDENCE]
    ISO --> AN[ANTHROPOGENIC SUBSIDENCE]
    ST --> RSL1[GLOBAL MEAN SEA LEVEL RISE]
    MS --> RSL1
    DY --> RSL1
    GIA --> RSL2[REGIONAL SEA LEVEL CHANGE]
    TC --> RSL2
    AN --> RSL2
    RSL1 --> IMP1[COASTAL FLOODING]
    RSL1 --> IMP2[SALINITY INTRUSION]
    RSL1 --> IMP3[COASTAL EROSION]
    RSL1 --> IMP4[CONTAMINANT MOBILIZATION]
    RSL2 --> IMP1
    RSL2 --> IMP2
    RSL2 --> IMP3
    RSL2 --> IMP4
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — From Climate Forcing to Coastal Pollution

```mermaid
graph LR
    CF[CLIMATE FORCING GREENHOUSE GASES] --> OT[OCEAN HEAT UPTAKE]
    CF --> IC[CRYOSPHERE RESPONSE]
    OT --> ST1[THERMOSTERIC EXPANSION]
    IC --> MS1[MASS ADDITION TO OCEAN]
    ST1 --> GMSL[GLOBAL MEAN SEA LEVEL]
    MS1 --> GMSL
    GMSL --> DY1[DYNAMIC REDISTRIBUTION]
    DY1 --> RSL[RELATIVE SEA LEVEL AT COAST]
    GMSL --> RSL
    RSL --> CZ[COASTAL ZONE HAZARD]
    CZ --> FL[INUNDATION OF LANDFILLS]
    CZ --> SA[SALTWATER INTRUSION IN AQUIFERS]
    CZ --> ER[BEACH AND CLIFF EROSION]
    CZ --> MG[MANGROVE AND WETLAND LOSS]
```

## 4.3 Measurement Techniques — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[TIDE GAUGE NETWORKS PSMSL] --> H1[HISTORICAL RSL RECORDS]
    M2[SATELLITE ALTIMETRY TOPEX JASON] --> H2[GLOBAL MSL TIME SERIES]
    M3[GPS REFERENCE STATIONS] --> H3[ABSOLUTE LAND MOTION]
    M4[GRACE FO SATELLITE GRAVIMETRY] --> H4[ICE AND WATER MASS CHANGE]
    M5[ARGO FLOAT NETWORK] --> H5[STERIC COMPONENT PROFILE]
    H1 --> ANA[DATA FUSION AND ANALYSIS]
    H2 --> ANA
    H3 --> ANA
    H4 --> ANA
    H5 --> ANA
    ANA --> OUT[GMSL DECOMPOSITION STERIC MASS DYNAMIC]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Linear Superposition of Sea Level Signals

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[MEAN SEA LEVEL DATUM MSL] --> B[STERIC COMPONENT alpha Delta T H]
    A --> C[MASS COMPONENT V over A_o]
    A --> D[DYNAMIC COMPONENT CIRCULATION]
    A --> E[ISOSTATIC COMPONENT GIA TECTONIC]
    A --> F[ANTHROPOGENIC SUBSIDENCE]
    B --> SUM[SUPERPOSITION SIGNAL]
    C --> SUM
    D --> SUM
    E --> SUM
    F --> SUM
    SUM --> TG[TIDE GAUGE READING]
    SUM --> SA2[SATELLITE ALTIMETRY READING]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** Examiners love the *superposition principle*. A correctly written Part-B (14-mark) answer must explicitly state that the observed RSL = sum of all four components, with sign convention (+ for rise, – for fall) clearly shown.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q1. (3 marks)**
> *Distinguish between eustatic and isostatic sea level changes with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Eustatic Sea Level Change** refers to a *global* change in the volume of ocean water, measured relative to a fixed reference such as the Earth's centre of mass. It is caused by thermal expansion of seawater, addition of meltwater from ice sheets and glaciers, and changes in land-water storage. Example: The post-1900 global mean sea level rise of ~21–24 cm is a eustatic signal.
>
> **Isostatic Sea Level Change**, in contrast, is a *local or regional* effect caused by the vertical movement of the land itself, on which the tide gauge is fixed. It is driven by Glacial Isostatic Adjustment (GIA), tectonics, volcanism, and anthropogenic subsidence. Example: The coast of Scandinavia is presently *falling* in relative sea level terms (by ~3–4 mm/yr) because the land is rebounding from the last ice age, even though the global ocean is rising.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q2. (3 marks)**
> *List the major contributors to current global mean sea level rise and state their approximate contributions.*

**Model Answer:**

> Based on IPCC AR6 estimates for the period 2006–2018:
> 1. **Thermosteric expansion** — ~1.5 mm/yr (~45 %)
> 2. **Greenland Ice Sheet melt** — ~0.7 mm/yr (~21 %)
> 3. **Antarctic Ice Sheet melt** — ~0.4 mm/yr (~12 %)
> 4. **Mountain glaciers and ice caps** — ~0.5 mm/yr (~15 %)
> 5. **Land-water storage change** — ~0.2 mm/yr (~7 %)
>
> The total observed GMSL rise is approximately **3.3 mm/yr**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1, Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]**

> **(a) Derive the thermosteric sea level rise formula starting from the definition of the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient. Compute the rise when the top 1000 m of the global ocean warms by 0.15 °C. Assume $\alpha = 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) Explain the role of Glacial Isostatic Adjustment (GIA) in modulating regional sea level. Why do some coastlines experience falling relative sea level even when the global ocean is rising? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution to A(a):**

We begin from the **definition of the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient** for a fluid parcel at constant pressure and salinity:

$$
\beta_V = \frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{p,S}
$$

For an ocean column of constant horizontal cross-section $A$, $V = A \cdot H$, so:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{H} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{p,S}
$$

Differential form:

$$
dH = \alpha \, H \, dT
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $(H_0, T_0)$ to $(H, T)$:

$$
\int_{H_0}^{H} \frac{dH}{H} = \int_{T_0}^{T} \alpha \, dT
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{H}{H_0} \right) = \alpha (T - T_0)
$$

Using the small-argument approximation $\ln(1 + x) \approx x$ for $\alpha \Delta T \ll 1$:

$$
\frac{\Delta H}{H_0} = \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H = \alpha \, \Delta T \, H_0}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

Given $\alpha = 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$, $\Delta T = 0.15 \ \text{K}$, $H_0 = 1000 \ \text{m}$:

$$
\Delta H = (1.7 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.15 \times 1000 = 2.55 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H \approx 25.5 \ \text{mm}}
$$

**Conclusion:** A modest 0.15 °C warming of the upper 1000 m of the global ocean produces a thermosteric rise of about 25.5 mm — comparable to the *entire* observed 20th-century steric contribution.

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**GIA Mechanism:**
During the Last Glacial Maximum (~20,000 years ago), massive ice sheets depressed the Earth's lithosphere and displaced viscous mantle material away from beneath them. As the climate warmed, the ice sheets melted and the lithosphere began to rebound viscoelastically over millennial timescales. This vertical land motion is called **Glacial Isostatic Adjustment**.

**Mathematical Description:**
The observed RSL at any tide gauge is:

$$
RSL(t) = GMSL(t) + V_{land}(t)
$$

where $V_{land}(t)$ is the vertical land velocity. For a rebounding region (e.g., Scandinavia, Hudson Bay), $V_{land} > 0$ (uplift), so even with positive $GMSL(t)$ the RSL may *decrease*:

$$
\frac{d(RSL)}{dt} = \underbrace{+3.3 \ \text{mm/yr}}_{\text{GMSL rise}} - \underbrace{9 \ \text{mm/yr}}_{\text{Land uplift in Scandinavia}} = -5.7 \ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Peripheral subsidence:** The displaced mantle material flows back, causing a broad *forebulge collapse* in zones peripheral to the former ice sheets (e.g., the US East Coast), which **amplifies** the GMSL rise locally. Hence, the US East Coast experiences RSL rise of 3–4 mm/yr — *higher* than the global mean.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently assume that RSL is uniform globally. **Always state** that RSL = GMSL + Land Motion, and give the sign of land motion for the region under discussion. Marks are lost when the formula is omitted.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

> **(a) With neat block diagrams, explain the four principal components contributing to global mean sea level rise. State one measurement technique for each component. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) Compute the sea level rise caused by complete melting of (i) the Greenland Ice Sheet and (ii) the Antarctic Ice Sheet. Use $A_o = 3.618 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^2$, $\rho_w = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{ice} = 917 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution to B(a):**

The four principal components of GMSL rise and their measurement techniques are:

| # | Component | Mechanism | Measurement Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thermosteric** | Warming → expansion | **Argo float network** (autonomous profiling floats measuring T, S vs. depth) |
| 2 | **Halosteric** | Salinity change → density change | Argo floats + salinity sensors |
| 3 | **Mass (Ice sheets & glaciers)** | Meltwater addition | **GRACE-FO satellite gravimetry** (measures time-varying gravity field) |
| 4 | **Dynamic** | Wind & circulation-driven redistribution | **Satellite altimetry** (Jason-3, Sentinel-6) for sea-surface height |

**Block Diagram Summary:**

$$
GMSL = \underbrace{\int \alpha \, dT \, dz}_{\text{Steric (Argo)}} + \underbrace{\frac{\Delta M_{ice}}{\rho_w A_o}}_{\text{Mass (GRACE-FO)}} + \underbrace{\Delta \eta_{dyn}}_{\text{Dynamic (Altimetry)}}
$$

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert ice volume to liquid water volume:**

For an ice mass of volume $V_{ice}$ and density $\rho_{ice}$:

$$
V_{water} = \frac{\rho_{ice}}{\rho_w} V_{ice} = \frac{917}{1025} V_{ice} \approx 0.8946 \, V_{ice}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the resulting sea level rise:**

$$
\Delta H = \frac{V_{water}}{A_o} = \frac{0.8946 \, V_{ice}}{3.618 \times 10^{14}}
$$

**(i) Greenland Ice Sheet ($V_{ice} = 2.85 \times 10^{15} \ \text{m}^3$):**

$$
\Delta H_{GIS} = \frac{0.8946 \times 2.85 \times 10^{15}}{3.618 \times 10^{14}}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{GIS} = \frac{2.5496 \times 10^{15}}{3.618 \times 10^{14}} \approx 7.05 \ \text{m}
$$

**(ii) Antarctic Ice Sheet ($V_{ice} = 2.59 \times 10^{16} \ \text{m}^3$):**

$$
\Delta H_{AIS} = \frac{0.8946 \times 2.59 \times 10^{16}}{3.618 \times 10^{14}}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{AIS} = \frac{2.3170 \times 10^{16}}{3.618 \times 10^{14}} \approx 64.04 \ \text{m}
$$

**Total potential rise from complete melt of both ice sheets:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta H_{total} \approx 71.1 \ \text{m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **multiply by the density ratio** $\rho_{ice}/\rho_w$ and simply use $V_{ice}/A_o$. This **over-estimates** the rise by a factor of 1.12. **Lose 1 mark.**
>
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall #2:** Using the wrong ocean area (e.g., $3.6 \times 10^{8}$ km² confused with km² vs m²) is a **fatal error**. Always check that the unit is m².

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wide

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns Identified by KTU Board Examiners (2020–2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing GMSL with RSL:** Always clarify the reference frame. A 2-mark deduction applies if the student mixes them up.
> 2. **Skipping the density ratio** in meltwater problems (see Pitfall #1 above).
> 3. **Using $g$ in formulas where it is not required** (e.g., in volume-to-rise conversion). Marks deducted for irrelevant terms.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the assumption $\alpha \Delta T \ll 1$** in the linearized steric derivation. Examiners specifically test whether the student understands the small-argument approximation.
> 5. **Not specifying the time period** of any sea level rate (e.g., 1993–2023 satellite altimetry vs 1900–2000 tide gauge). Lose 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the final answer of a numerical problem. KTU mandates SI units.
> 7. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows** or direction of flow. Use directed arrows in flow-charts.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Revision for the KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Eustatic = global water volume change; Isostatic = local land motion.**
- **GMSL = Steric + Mass + Dynamic components.**
- **RSL (tide gauge) = GMSL + Vertical Land Motion.**
- The current rate of GMSL rise is **~3.3 – 3.4 mm/yr**, more than twice the 20th-century average.
- **Steric formula (linearized):** $\Delta H = \alpha \Delta T H$, with $\alpha \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$ for upper ocean.
- **Mass conversion:** $\Delta H = \dfrac{\rho_{ice}}{\rho_w} \dfrac{V_{ice}}{A_o}$; **always include the density ratio**.
- **Sea level equivalent constants:**
  - $A_o = 3.618 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^2$
  - $\rho_w / \rho_{ice} \approx 1.118$
  - 1 mm SLR ≈ $3.7 \times 10^{11}$ tonnes of water added.
- **Full melt of Greenland ≈ 7 m; Full melt of Antarctica ≈ 58 m; total ≈ 65–71 m** (depending on bedrock feedback).
- **Thermosteric contribution:** ~1.5 mm/yr (~45 % of total).
- **Mass contribution:** ~1.8 mm/yr (Greenland + Antarctica + glaciers + land-water storage).
- **Halosteric effect** is smaller than thermosteric at the global scale but important regionally.
- **GIA** causes **falling** RSL in Scandinavia and Canada (rebound) and **amplified** RSL rise on the US East Coast (peripheral subsidence).
- **Measurement techniques:**
  - **Tide gauges** (PSMSL network) → historical RSL
  - **Satellite altimetry** (TOPEX/Poseidon, Jason-1/2/3, Sentinel-6) → GMSL since 1993
  - **GPS stations** → land vertical motion
  - **GRACE / GRACE-FO** → mass redistribution (ice, groundwater)
  - **Argo floats** → steric profile
- **Pollution consequences of SLR:** saltwater intrusion into coastal aquifers, re-mobilization of buried solid waste and heavy metals, loss of wetlands (which act as natural pollutant filters), and accelerated coastal erosion.
- **IPCC AR6 projections for 2100 (SSP2-4.5):** ~0.4 – 0.7 m of GMSL rise above 1995–2014 baseline.
- **Sign convention in KTU answers:** $\Delta H > 0$ → sea level rise, $\Delta H < 0$ → sea level fall.
- **Linear superposition is valid** for sea level components to first order (small perturbations).
- **Key exam tricks:** Watch for unit confusion (m vs. mm), density ratio omissions, and untagged time periods in numerical answers.
- **Always state assumptions explicitly:** isostatic equilibrium, hydrostatic pressure, small $\alpha \Delta T$, uniform meltwater distribution.
- **Coastal cities at risk in India (pollution-relevance):** Kochi, Mangalore, Mumbai, Chennai, Sundarbans, Kuttanad — all are low-lying deltaic / estuarine zones with active pollutant discharges.
- **Engineering application — seawall freeboard design:** Crest elevation = design high-tide level + storm surge + projected SLR (50–100-year horizon) + safety factor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Emissivity and Green Shipping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carbon Emissivity and Green Shipping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Carbon Emissivity (Maritime Context)

**Carbon Emissivity (CE)** in the maritime sector is defined as the **mass of carbon-based greenhouse gases (GHGs), expressed in grams of CO₂-equivalent (gCO₂eq), released per unit of useful transport work performed by a vessel**, typically measured in **grams of CO₂ per tonne-nautical mile (gCO₂ / t·nm)** or **grams of CO₂ per tonne-kilometre (gCO₂ / t·km)**.

The reference regulatory framework is the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) MARPOL Annex VI**, which regulates air pollution from ships and was amended by the **IMO Initial IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships (MEPC 304(72), 2018)** and the **2023 IMO GHG Strategy (MEPC 377(80))**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The student must distinguish between the **three operational pillars** of maritime decarbonisation:
> 1. **Energy Efficiency** (technical measure: EEDI / EEXI)
> 2. **Operational Carbon Intensity** (operational measure: CII / SEEMP)
> 3. **Alternative Fuels & Propulsion** (technological measure: LNG, methanol, ammonia, H₂, wind, electric, nuclear)

### 1.2 Definition of Green Shipping

**Green Shipping** is the integrated design, operation, and policy framework that aims to **reduce the environmental footprint of maritime transport** by minimising greenhouse gas emissions, air pollutants (SOx, NOx, PM), noise, and water pollution, while maintaining or improving economic efficiency and safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Regulatory Benchmarks (KTU High-Yield Definitions)**
> - **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** → applies to **newbuild** ships (Regulation 22, MARPOL Annex VI).
> - **EEXI (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index)** → applies to **existing** ships from 2023.
> - **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** → annual operational rating (A to E) introduced 2023.
> - **SEEMP (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan)** → mandatory operational plan.
> - **DCSA (Decarbonisation Pathway)** → industry-aligned roadmap.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Backpack of Carbon"

Imagine every ship as a **traveller carrying a backpack of invisible CO₂ molecules**. The heavier the cargo (fuel) and the longer the journey, the heavier the backpack becomes.

- A **bulk carrier** carrying iron ore from Brazil to China is like a traveller walking a long distance with a heavy load — the *emissions per tonne-km* is moderate, but the **total emissions are huge**.
- A **container ship** is the long-haul trucker of the seas — the biggest single contributor (~25–30% of global shipping CO₂).
- A **cruise ship** is like a luxury tour bus — it burns enormous fuel per passenger-day for comfort.

**Green Shipping** = making the backpack lighter (cleaner fuels), teaching the traveller to walk smarter (route optimisation, slow steaming), and replacing the shoes with electric/hydrogen equivalents (alternative propulsion).

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Bolded)

| Constant / Metric | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference speed for EEDI** | **Vref (75% of MCR) at 75% of design draught** | IMO Res. MEPC.364(79) |
| **Density of HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil)** | **≈ 991 kg/m³ at 15 °C** | ISO 8217 |
| **CO₂ Mass Emission Factor — HFO** | **3.114 t CO₂ / t fuel** | IMO MEPC.364(79) Annex VI |
| **CO₂ Mass Emission Factor — Diesel/Gas Oil** | **3.206 t CO₂ / t fuel** | IMO MEPC.364(79) |
| **CO₂ Mass Emission Factor — LNG** | **2.750 t CO₂ / t fuel** | IMO MEPC.364(79) |
| **CO₂ Mass Emission Factor — Methanol** | **1.375 t CO₂ / t fuel** | IMO MEPC.364(79) |
| **CO₂ Mass Emission Factor — Ammonia** | **0.000 t CO₂ / t fuel** (carbon-free) | IMO MEPC.364(79) |
| **Global Warming Potential of CH₄ (100-yr)** | **28–34 (AR5) / 27.9 (AR6)** | IPCC |
| **Target: 2030 (revised 2023)** | **≥ 20% reduction (≥ 30% striving)** | MEPC 377(80) |
| **Target: 2040 (revised 2023)** | **≥ 70% reduction striving for 80%** | MEPC 377(80) |
| **Target: 2050 (net-zero)** | **Net-zero GHG emissions** | MEPC 377(80) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Decarbonisation Hierarchy (Why & How)

**Layer 1 — Energy Efficiency (Design & Hull)**
- **Why?** The cheapest tonne of CO₂ is the one never produced. Improving hydrodynamics and propulsion efficiency reduces fuel burn directly.
- **How?**
  - Hull form optimisation (slenderness ratio, bulbous bow, stern optimisation).
  - Air lubrication systems (micro-bubble injection reduces frictional resistance by 5–10%).
  - Waste Heat Recovery Systems (WHRS) on auxiliary engines.
  - Propeller Boss Cap Fins (PBCF) and contra-rotating propellers.
  - Low-friction antifouling coatings (silicone-based, ~3–8% fuel saving).

**Layer 2 — Operational Measures (Behavioural)**
- **Why?** A given ship can emit 10–30% less CO₂ with the same machinery by smart operations.
- **How?**
  - **Slow Steaming** (reducing from 24 to 18 knots cuts fuel by ~40%).
  - **Weather Routing** (avoiding head seas saves 2–6%).
  - **Just-In-Time (JIT) Port Arrival** (eliminates anchorage idling).
  - **Trim & Draft Optimisation** (Optimum Trim, ISO 19018).
  - **Voyage Planning** under SEEMP Part III.

**Layer 3 — Alternative Fuels & New Technologies**
- **Why?** Even a perfectly efficient conventional ship running on HFO emits ~3.1 t CO₂/t fuel. Carbon-zero targets require **molecular decarbonisation** — changing the fuel.
- **How?**
  - **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas)** — 20–25% CO₂ reduction; challenge: methane slip.
  - **Methanol (CH₃OH)** — drop-in fuel; green methanol from renewable H₂ + captured CO₂.
  - **Ammonia (NH₃)** — zero-carbon combustion, but toxic; needs careful handling.
  - **Hydrogen (H₂)** — zero-carbon; low volumetric density.
  - **Biofuels** (FAME, BTL) — carbon-neutral in life cycle.
  - **Wind-Assisted Propulsion** (rotor sails, kites — 5–30% savings).
  - **Nuclear** (e.g., nuclear-powered cargo vessels — historical concept).
  - **Onboard Carbon Capture & Storage (OCCS)** — post-combustion amine scrubbing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Fuel-Preparedness" Spectrum (KTU Definition)**
> IMO classifies future fuels by *Tier* of readiness: **Tier 1 (LNG, biofuels — available now)**, **Tier 2 (Methanol, LPG — developing)**, **Tier 3 (Ammonia, Hydrogen — long-term)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation ID | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E1** | $\text{EEDI} = \dfrac{\Big( \prod_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot \big(\sum_{i=1}^{nME} P_{ME(i)} \cdot C_{FME(i)} \cdot SFC_{ME(i)}\big) + \big(\sum_{i=1}^{nPTI} P_{PTI(i)} \cdot C_{FAE(i)} \cdot SFC_{AE(i)}\big) + \big(\sum_{i=1}^{nAE} P_{AE(i)} \cdot C_{FAE(i)} \cdot SFC_{AE(i)}\big) \Big) \cdot f_{i} \cdot f_{c} \cdot f_{l} \cdot V_{ref}}{f_w \cdot Capacity \cdot V_{ref}}$ | Energy Efficiency Design Index (newbuild) | gCO₂ / t·nm |
| **E2** | $\text{EEXI} = \dfrac{\text{Available EEDI value for the ship}}{\text{Required EEDI value for the ship}}$ | Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (existing) | ratio (≤ 1) |
| **E3** | $\text{CII} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{Fj} \cdot m_{fuel,j}}{DWT \cdot \text{Distance}}$ | Carbon Intensity Indicator (annual) | gCO₂ / t·nm |
| **E4** | $\text{Required CII} = (1 - Z/100) \cdot \text{CII}_{ref}$ | Annual reduction trajectory | gCO₂ / t·nm |
| **E5** | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \cdot C_F$ | Mass of CO₂ per fuel burnt | tonnes |
| **E6** | $\text{Carbon Emissivity Index (CEI)} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{W_{cargo} \cdot L_{voyage}}$ | Specific CO₂ per transport work | gCO₂ / t·nm |
| **E7** | $\text{AER} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{DWT \cdot \text{Distance}}$ | Annual Efficiency Ratio (operational metric) | gCO₂ / t·nm |
| **E8** | $C_{F,\text{blend}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_{F,i}$ | Weighted CO₂ factor for blended fuels | t CO₂ / t fuel |
| **E9** | $\Delta m_{CO_2} = m_{CO_2,\text{before}} - m_{CO_2,\text{after}}$ | Absolute CO₂ reduction | tonnes |
| **E10** | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{prop}}{m_{fuel} \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | Propulsive efficiency (engine → thrust) | dimensionless |

> **Notation key (used in formulas above):**
> - $f_j$ = correction factor for ship type $j$
> - $P_{ME(i)}$ = power of main engine $i$ (kW)
> - $C_{FME(i)}$ = CO₂ emission factor of main engine fuel (g CO₂ / g fuel)
> - $SFC_{ME(i)}$ = specific fuel consumption of main engine (g / kWh)
> - $f_i, f_c, f_l$ = capacity, cubic, technical/limited correction factors
> - $V_{ref}$ = reference speed (knots)
> - $f_w$ = weighting factor (load factor at sea)
> - $C_{Fj}$ = CO₂ conversion factor for fuel $j$
> - $m_{fuel,j}$ = mass of fuel $j$ consumed (tonnes)
> - $DWT$ = deadweight tonnage (tonnes)
> - $Z$ = annual reduction factor (% per year, e.g., 5% for 2023–2026; 7% for 2027–2030)
> - $CII_{ref}$ = reference CII baseline value (gCO₂ / t·nm)
> - $W_{cargo}$ = cargo weight carried (tonnes)
> - $L_{voyage}$ = voyage distance (nautical miles)
> - $w_i$ = mass fraction of fuel $i$ in blend
> - $LHV$ = lower heating value of fuel (kJ / kg)
> - $P_{prop}$ = useful propulsive power (kW)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards** use EEDI to benchmark hull-engine-propeller integration. The 2025 phase requires ~30% improvement over the 2013 baseline.
- **Port State Control** verifies SEEMP Part III and CII rating — failing ships face detention.
- **Charterers (cargo owners)** use CII rating for ESG reporting under **Poseidon Principles** (launched 2019, currently ~30 banks representing >$200 Bn shipping finance).
- **Class societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) issue *Green Certificates* based on emission performance.
- **Stock exchanges** (e.g., Hong Kong, Oslo) now require shipping ESG disclosure tied to CII.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EEDI Formula (Regulation 22 / Resolution MEPC.364(79))

**Step 1 — Establish the numerator: total CO₂ emissions from all energy consumers.**

A ship's energy consumers during its reference voyage are categorised into:
- Main engines (ME) — propulsion
- Auxiliary engines (AE) — hotel load
- Shaft generators / Power Take-In (PTI)
- Boilers (sometimes included)

For each consumer $i$, the mass of CO₂ produced per second is:

$$m_{CO_2,i} = P_i \cdot SFC_i \cdot C_{F,i}$$

where:
- $P_i$ = rated power output (kW)
- $SFC_i$ = specific fuel consumption (g fuel / kWh)
- $C_{F,i}$ = CO₂ mass per unit fuel (g CO₂ / g fuel)

**Step 2 — Sum over all consumers and apply correction factors.**

The numerator becomes:

$$\text{Numerator} = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{nME} P_{ME(i)} \cdot C_{F,ME(i)} \cdot SFC_{ME(i)} + \sum_{i=1}^{nPTI} P_{PTI(i)} \cdot C_{F,AE(i)} \cdot SFC_{AE(i)} + \sum_{i=1}^{nAE} P_{AE(i)} \cdot C_{F,AE(i)} \cdot SFC_{AE(i)} \right] \cdot f_j \cdot f_i \cdot f_c \cdot f_l$$

**Explanation of factors:**
- $f_j$ = ship-type correction factor (e.g., 1.0 for tankers, 1.04 for bulk carriers).
- $f_i$ = capacity correction (for ships below standard capacity).
- $f_c$ = cubic correction (e.g., ro-ro, ro-pax).
- $f_l$ = limited-load correction (mechanical / hybrid propulsion cases).

**Step 3 — Denominator: useful transport work.**

Useful transport work is mass × distance:

$$\text{Denominator} = f_w \cdot Capacity \cdot V_{ref}$$

where $Capacity$ is **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)** for tankers/bulker/general cargo, or **GT** (Gross Tonnage) for passenger/cruise ships, and $V_{ref}$ is the reference speed in knots.

**Step 4 — Final form.**

Combining numerator and denominator, the EEDI (in g CO₂ / t·nm) is:

$$\boxed{\text{EEDI} = \frac{\Big( \sum_{ME} P_{ME} C_{F,ME} SFC_{ME} + \sum_{PTI} P_{PTI} C_{F,AE} SFC_{AE} + \sum_{AE} P_{AE} C_{F,AE} SFC_{AE} \Big) \cdot f_j \cdot f_i \cdot f_c \cdot f_l}{f_w \cdot Capacity}}$$

(Note: $V_{ref}$ cancels out in the standard form because numerator already contains fuel burn *per unit time* and denominator contains *per unit time* implicitly through power-time integration — both sides are power-based.)

### 3.2 Worked Example — CII Calculation for a Panamax Bulk Carrier

**Given data for Vessel "MARINEPOL-A" (2023):**
- Type: Panamax Bulk Carrier
- DWT = 82,000 t
- Annual fuel consumption:
  - HFO: 4,200 tonnes
  - MGO (auxiliary + port): 200 tonnes
- Annual distance steamed: 75,000 nautical miles

**Step 1 — Compute CO₂ emissions from each fuel stream.**

For HFO ($C_{F,HFO}$ = 3.114):

$$m_{CO_2,HFO} = 4{,}200 \times 3.114 = 13{,}078.8 \text{ t CO}_2$$

For MGO ($C_{F,MGO}$ = 3.206):

$$m_{CO_2,MGO} = 200 \times 3.206 = 641.2 \text{ t CO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Total CO₂.**

$$m_{CO_2,\text{total}} = 13{,}078.8 + 641.2 = 13{,}720.0 \text{ t CO}_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply CII formula (AER type for bulk carrier).**

$$\text{CII} = \frac{m_{CO_2,\text{total}}}{DWT \times \text{Distance}} = \frac{13{,}720{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{82{,}000 \times 75{,}000 \text{ t·nm}}$$

$$\text{CII} = \frac{13{,}720{,}000{,}000}{6{,}150{,}000{,}000} = 2.2306 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ / t·nm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with required CII.**

For 2023, the IMO baseline reference CII for a Panamax bulker is approximately **5.93 g CO₂ / t·nm**, with annual reduction factor $Z = 5\%$:

$$\text{CII}_{\text{required}} = (1 - 0.05) \times 5.93 = 5.6335 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ / t·nm}$$

**Step 5 — Rating determination.**

$$\text{Attained CII} = 2.2306 \ll \text{CII}_{\text{required}} = 5.6335$$

Ratio = $2.2306 / 5.6335 = 0.396$ → Way below 0.86 → **Rating A** (best operational performance).

> [!NOTE]
> **CII Rating Bands (KTU 2024 Important):**
> - **A**: Ratio $\leq 0.86$ — Major superior
> - **B**: $0.86 <$ Ratio $\leq 0.94$
> - **C**: $0.94 <$ Ratio $\leq 1.06$ — Required
> - **D**: $1.06 <$ Ratio $\leq 1.19$ — Below required
> - **E**: Ratio $> 1.19$ — Poor; **3 consecutive E ratings → Corrective Action Plan**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Green Fuel Comparison

| Fuel | CO₂ Reduction (well-to-wake, vs HFO) | Maturity (IMO Tier) | Key Challenge | Vapour Pressure | Methane Slip | Bunkering Cost (USD/tonne, 2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HFO (Baseline)** | 0% | Tier 0 (legacy) | Sulfur; CO₂ | low | none | ~$550 |
| **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas)** | 20–25% | Tier 1 (proven) | Methane slip; cryogenic storage | low (cryo) | yes (CH₄ GHG) | ~$700 |
| **Methanol (CH₃OH)** | 5–15% (grey) / 90–95% (green) | Tier 2 | Toxicity; energy density | medium | no | ~$400 (grey) / ~$1,200 (green) |
| **Ammonia (NH₃)** | 0% (grey) / 90%+ (green) | Tier 3 | Toxicity; NOx | high (toxic) | no | ~$700 (grey) / ~$1,500 (green) |
| **Hydrogen (H₂, liquid)** | 90%+ (green H₂) | Tier 3 | Storage volume; boil-off | very high (cryo) | no | ~$2,500–6,000 |
| **Biodiesel (FAME)** | 30–80% (blend, life-cycle) | Tier 1 | Cold flow; oxidation | low | no | ~$900–1,400 |
| **e-Fuels (e-Methanol, e-Ammonia)** | 90%+ | Tier 2/3 | Green H₂ cost; PtX plants | medium | no | ~$1,500–2,500 |

> **Sources**: DNV Maritime CO₂ Reduction Outlook 2024; Lloyd's Register Fuel Ready; MAN Energy Solutions 2024 Whitepaper; ABS Sustainability Whitepaper.

### 3.4 Why Slow Steaming Cuts Fuel More Than Linearly (Detailed Derivation)

A ship's fuel consumption follows the **cubic law** of resistance:

$$R = R_f + R_{wave} + R_{eddy} \approx k_1 V^2 + k_2 V^n \quad (n \approx 4 \text{ to } 6)$$

The effective power required at speed $V$ is:

$$P = R \cdot V = k_1 V^3 + k_2 V^{n+1}$$

Reducing speed from $V_1$ to $V_2$ changes power as:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^3 \quad \text{(approx., frictional-dominated regime)}$$

If a ship reduces speed by 25%:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.75)^3 = 0.4219$$

→ Fuel reduction ≈ **57.8%**, demonstrating the powerful effect of slow steaming. This is why **slow steaming is the single most effective "no-cost" decarbonisation lever**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The IMO Maritime Decarbonisation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Ship Energy System] --> nodeB[Fuel Selection Stage]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Conventional HFO/MGO]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Transitional LNG]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Zero Carbon NH3/H2]

    nodeA --> nodeC[Energy Efficiency Stage]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Hull Optimisation]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Propulsion Efficiency]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Auxiliary Systems]

    nodeA --> nodeD[Operational Stage]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Slow Steaming]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Weather Routing]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[JIT Port Arrival]

    nodeB1 --> nodeE[Emissions Output CO2 SOx NOx PM]
    nodeB2 --> nodeE
    nodeB3 --> nodeE
    nodeC1 --> nodeE
    nodeC2 --> nodeE
    nodeC3 --> nodeE
    nodeD1 --> nodeE
    nodeD2 --> nodeE
    nodeD3 --> nodeE

    nodeE --> nodeF[Regulatory Layer]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[MARPOL Annex VI]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[EEDI EEXI CII SEEMP]

    nodeF --> nodeG[Monitoring and Certification]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[DCS Data Collection]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Annual Rating A to E]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[Poseidon Principles ESG]

    nodeG2 --> nodeH{Performance Classification}
    nodeH -->|A and B| nodeH1[Green Certified]
    nodeH -->|C| nodeH2[Compliant Baseline]
    nodeH -->|D and E| nodeH3[Corrective Action Plan]

    nodeH3 --> nodeI[Ship Improvement Cycle]
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Green Shipping Technology Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[Vessel Hull and Form] --> nodeZ[Overall Carbon Emissivity Reduction]
    nodeA2[Air Lubrication System] --> nodeZ
    nodeA3[Low Friction Coating] --> nodeZ
    nodeA4[Bulbous Bow and Stern] --> nodeZ

    nodeB1[WHR Waste Heat Recovery] --> nodeZ
    nodeB2[Shaft Generator] --> nodeZ
    nodeB3[Hybrid Battery Pack] --> nodeZ

    nodeC1[Wind Rotor Sails] --> nodeZ
    nodeC2[Kite Towed Sail] --> nodeZ
    nodeC3[Solar PV Panels] --> nodeZ

    nodeD1[LNG Dual Fuel Engine] --> nodeZ
    nodeD2[Methanol Engine] --> nodeZ
    nodeD3[Ammonia Engine] --> nodeZ
    nodeD4[Fuel Cell H2] --> nodeZ
    nodeD5[Biofuel B20 B30] --> nodeZ

    nodeE1[OCCS Onboard Carbon Capture] --> nodeZ
    nodeE2[Shore Power Cold Ironing] --> nodeZ

    nodeF1[Route Optimisation AI] --> nodeZ
    nodeF2[JIT Port Arrival] --> nodeZ
    nodeF3[Trim and Draft Optimisation] --> nodeZ
```

### 4.3 Schematic: CII Rating Decision Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start of Year] --> collect[Collect DCS Fuel and Distance Data]
    collect --> calc[Compute Attained CII gCO2 per t nm]
    calc --> ref[Read Required CII from Baseline Z factor]
    ref --> ratio[Compute Ratio = Attained divided by Required]
    ratio -->|less than 0.86| ratingA[Rating A]
    ratio -->|0.86 to 0.94| ratingB[Rating B]
    ratio -->|0.94 to 1.06| ratingC[Rating C]
    ratio -->|1.06 to 1.19| ratingD[Rating D]
    ratio -->|greater than 1.19| ratingE[Rating E]

    ratingE --> check{Three Consecutive E}
    check -->|Yes| cap[Issue Corrective Action Plan SEEMP Part III Update]
    check -->|No| continue[Continue Monitoring]
    cap --> continue

    ratingD --> check2{D Five Year Plan}
    check2 -->|Yes| cap2[SEEMP Enhancement]
    check2 -->|No| continue

    ratingA --> cert[Issue Statement of Compliance]
    ratingB --> cert
    ratingC --> cert
    ratingD --> cert2[Statement of Compliance with Plan]
    ratingE --> cert2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in KTU Context:**
> - **Block 4.1** represents the *closed-loop* IMO strategy: emissions trigger regulation, regulation triggers monitoring, monitoring feeds improvement.
> - **Block 4.2** is the *technology stack* — the 5 categories students should remember: Hull, Energy Recovery, Wind/External, Fuel Choice, Post-Treatment.
> - **Block 4.3** is the *operational evaluation pipeline* — a process-flow mapping DCS data to a CII rating.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**
*Define Carbon Emissivity in the maritime context. State any two standard units used to express it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Carbon Emissivity is the mass of carbon-based greenhouse gases (CO₂ equivalent) released per unit of useful transport work performed by a vessel, as defined under IMO MARPOL Annex VI. It quantifies the *efficiency* of converting fuel energy into transport.
- **Units (1 mark):** Two standard units are (i) **g CO₂ / tonne-nautical mile (gCO₂ / t·nm)** — used in EEDI/EEXI/CII, and (ii) **g CO₂ / tonne-km (gCO₂ / t·km)** — used in the Poseidon Principles and EU ETS.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Module 1]**
*List the four operational indicators (indices/plans) introduced by IMO under MARPOL Annex VI to control shipping emissions. State the year of introduction of the latest one.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** — applies to newbuilds, introduced 2013.
2. **SEEMP (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan)** — Part I/II from 2013, Part III added 2021.
3. **EEXI (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index)** — introduced **2023** (latest technical index).
4. **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** — introduced **2023** (latest operational indicator with rating A–E).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **three-layer framework for maritime decarbonisation** as adopted by the IMO. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Kamsarmax bulk carrier has the following parameters:
- Main engine: $P_{ME}$ = 9,500 kW; SFC = 175 g/kWh; fuel = HFO ($C_F$ = 3.114)
- Auxiliaries: $P_{AE}$ = 800 kW; SFC = 215 g/kWh; fuel = MGO ($C_F$ = 3.206)
- DWT = 82,000 t; $V_{ref}$ = 14 knots; $f_w$ = 0.8

Calculate the **EEDI** and state whether the vessel complies with the IMO 2023 phase requirement of approximately **3.6 g CO₂ / t·nm** for this class (assume $f_j$ = $f_i$ = $f_c$ = $f_l$ = 1.0). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Three-Layer Framework (7 marks):**
- **Layer 1 — Energy Efficiency (Technical)** (2 marks): Hull optimisation, air lubrication, propeller improvements, low-friction coating. Reduces *inherent* emission per nautical mile.
- **Layer 2 — Operational Measures** (2 marks): Slow steaming, weather routing, JIT, trim optimisation, SEEMP Part III. Reduces *emission per voyage* without hardware change.
- **Layer 3 — Alternative Fuels & New Technologies** (3 marks): LNG, methanol, ammonia, H₂, biofuels, wind-assist, OCCS, nuclear. Replaces *molecular carbon* in fuel.

The block diagram should show these three layers as parallel inputs to a final "Reduced Carbon Emissivity" output, governed by IMO regulation.

**(b) EEDI Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Numerator components.

Main engine CO₂ emission rate:
$$P_{ME} \cdot C_{F,HFO} \cdot SFC_{ME} = 9{,}500 \times 3.114 \times 175 = 5{,}177{,}025 \text{ g/kWh·kW·unit}$$

To convert to g/kWh (since $C_F$ is t/t and SFC is g/kWh, the product is dimensionless per kWh input — careful dimensional handling is required):

Standard EEDI numerator (per hour, all in g CO₂ per hour):

Main engine: $9{,}500 \text{ kW} \times 175 \text{ g/kWh} \times 3.114 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/g fuel}$

= $9{,}500 \times 175 \times 3.114$ g CO₂/hour

$$= 5{,}177{,}025 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/h} = 5.177 \text{ t CO}_2\text{/h}$$

Auxiliaries: $800 \times 215 \times 3.206$

$$= 551{,}432 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/h} = 0.5514 \text{ t CO}_2\text{/h}$$

Step 2 — Total numerator per hour.

$$5.177 + 0.5514 = 5.7284 \text{ t CO}_2\text{/h}$$

Step 3 — Apply all correction factors (= 1 here).

Numerator remains: $5.7284$ t CO₂/h.

Step 4 — Denominator (transport work per hour).

$$\text{Denominator} = f_w \times DWT = 0.8 \times 82{,}000 = 65{,}600 \text{ t}$$

The "per hour" unit implicitly cancels, as EEDI is g CO₂ / t·nm:

$$\text{EEDI} = \frac{5.7284 \text{ t CO}_2\text{/h}}{65{,}600 \text{ t}} \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ t}}$$

Per nautical mile (using $V_{ref}$ = 14 kn → 1 hour = 14 nm):

$$\text{EEDI} = \frac{5.7284 \times 10^6 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/h}}{65{,}600 \text{ t} \times 14 \text{ nm/h}} = \frac{5.7284 \times 10^6}{918{,}400}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EEDI} = 6.237 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ / t·nm}}$$

Step 5 — Compliance check.

- Required (2023 phase for Kamsarmax bulk carrier ~ 82,000 DWT, taken as ~3.6 g CO₂ / t·nm in the question).
- Attained = 6.237 g CO₂ / t·nm.
- **Attained > Required → Vessel does NOT comply.** (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** The vessel fails the 2023 EEDI requirement; corrective measures (slow steaming, hull cleaning, or retrofitting energy-saving devices) are needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (EEDI Calculation):**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Mixing up units. $C_F$ is in **g CO₂ per g fuel** (dimensionless ratio). Do NOT multiply by 1,000,000 again. Read question carefully: IMO $C_F$ is in *t CO₂ / t fuel* = *g CO₂ / g fuel*.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to include both ME **and** AE. Lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting $V_{ref}$ in denominator → results off by an order of magnitude. Always recheck dimensional analysis.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Saying "ship complies" without checking the attained/required ratio direction.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** framework in detail. Define the **attained CII**, **required CII**, and the **five rating bands (A to E)**. State the consequence of getting an E rating for three consecutive years. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container ship uses the following fuels in a year:
- HFO: 5,800 tonnes
- MGO: 350 tonnes
- LNG: 1,200 tonnes

Annual distance steamed = 90,000 nm. DWT = 60,000 t.

Compute the **attained CII** and rate the vessel (assume CII reference baseline = 7.5 g CO₂ / t·nm, $Z$ = 5% for the year). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) CII Framework Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** CII is an *operational* metric that measures how efficiently a ship transports cargo in terms of CO₂ emitted per transport work, evaluated **annually** using DCS (Data Collection System) fuel consumption data and distance steamed.
- **Attained CII (2 marks):** Computed from the actual fuel consumption of the ship for the year:
  $$\text{CII}_{\text{attained}} = \frac{\sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot m_{fuel,j}}{DWT \cdot \text{Distance}}$$
- **Required CII (1 mark):** A declining value starting from a 2019 baseline reduced by factor $Z$% per year; for 2023, $Z$ = 5%; for 2027 onward, $Z$ = 7%.
- **Rating Bands (1 mark):** A (≤ 0.86), B (0.86–0.94), C (0.94–1.06), D (1.06–1.19), E (> 1.19).
- **Consequence of 3 × E (1 mark):** The ship must develop and implement a **Corrective Action Plan** (CAP) and a **SEEMP Part III update**; persistent poor rating leads to port state control intervention and reputational/financial penalty under Poseidon Principles.

**(b) CII Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute CO₂ from each fuel.

$$m_{CO_2,HFO} = 5{,}800 \times 3.114 = 18{,}061.2 \text{ t}$$

$$m_{CO_2,MGO} = 350 \times 3.206 = 1{,}122.1 \text{ t}$$

$$m_{CO_2,LNG} = 1{,}200 \times 2.750 = 3{,}300.0 \text{ t}$$

Step 2 — Total CO₂.

$$m_{CO_2,\text{total}} = 18{,}061.2 + 1{,}122.1 + 3{,}300.0 = 22{,}483.3 \text{ t CO}_2$$

Step 3 — Convert to grams.

$$m_{CO_2,\text{total}} = 22{,}483.3 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = 2.24833 \times 10^{10} \text{ g}$$

Step 4 — Transport work.

$$DWT \times \text{Distance} = 60{,}000 \times 90{,}000 = 5.4 \times 10^9 \text{ t·nm}$$

Step 5 — Attained CII.

$$\text{CII}_{\text{attained}} = \frac{2.24833 \times 10^{10}}{5.4 \times 10^9} = 4.1636 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ / t·nm}$$

Step 6 — Required CII.

$$\text{CII}_{\text{required}} = (1 - 0.05) \times 7.5 = 7.125 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ / t·nm}$$

Step 7 — Ratio.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{4.1636}{7.125} = 0.5844$$

Step 8 — Rating.

$0.5844 < 0.86$ → **Rating A (Major Superior)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (CII Calculation):**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing $C_F$ for LNG. Many students use 3.114 (HFO) for LNG — it is actually **2.750**, giving a major error. Lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to convert tonnes → grams. The unit of CII is *grams*, not tonnes. Off by factor $10^6$.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Applying the wrong reduction factor. $Z$ changes over the years (5% until 2026, 7% from 2027).
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Mixing up DWT and GT. Bulk/tanker/container → **DWT**; passenger/cruise → **GT**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Carbon Emissivity and Green Shipping**

- [x] **Carbon Emissivity** is *g CO₂ per transport work (g CO₂ / t·nm)*, not total emissions.
- [x] **MARPOL Annex VI** is the parent regulation. Amendments create EEDI, EEXI, CII, SEEMP.
- [x] **EEDI** = technical, newbuild. **EEXI** = technical, existing. **CII** = operational, annual. **SEEMP** = plan/management.
- [x] **IMO GHG Strategy 2023**: 20–30% reduction by 2030, 70–80% by 2040, **net-zero by or around 2050**.
- [x] **CII Rating**: A (≤0.86), B, C, D, E (>1.19). Three E's in a row → **Corrective Action Plan**.
- [x] **CO₂ Conversion Factors (MUST memorize)**: HFO 3.114, MGO/Diesel 3.206, LNG 2.750, Methanol 1.375, Ammonia 0.0.
- [x] **LNG reduces CO₂ 20–25%** but suffers **methane slip** (CH₄ is 28× more potent than CO₂ in 100-yr GWP).
- [x] **Ammonia** is the only *molecularly* carbon-zero maritime fuel at scale (toxicity challenge).
- [x] **Hydrogen (H₂)** is zero-carbon at point of use; challenge is production (green vs grey) and storage density.
- [x] **Slow Steaming** follows a *cubic law* — 25% speed reduction = ~58% fuel reduction.
- [x] **Air Lubrication** = micro-bubbles injected under hull, reducing friction by 5–10%.
- [x] **Poseidon Principles** tie shipping-loan interest rates to CII rating (ESG-linked finance).
- [x] **OCCS (Onboard Carbon Capture & Storage)** is the post-combustion analogue of land-based CCS.
- [x] **EEXI formula** is ratio: Attained / Required (must be ≤ 1).
- [x] **CII formula** is: (Sum of $C_F \times$ fuel) / (DWT × Distance).
- [x] **Green corridors** (e.g., Shanghai-LA, Rotterdam-NYC) are pilot routes for zero-emission shipping.
- [x] **Wind-assisted propulsion** (rotor sails, kites) can save 5–30% on suitable routes.
- [x] **Cold ironing** (shore power) eliminates auxiliary engine emissions during port stays.
- [x] **Methanol and ammonia** are the *most likely* transitional and long-term fuels, respectively, per MAN Energy Solutions and DNV.
- [x] **DCSA (Digital Container Shipping Association)** standardises data exchange for emissions reporting.
- [x] **EU ETS extension to shipping (2024)** requires shipowners to surrender CO₂ allowances for intra-EU and 50% of international voyages.
- [x] **Biofuels (FAME, BTL)** are *drop-in* but face feedstock limits; *e-fuels* (e-methanol, e-ammonia) are the *synthetic* future.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biodiversity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biodiversity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Biodiversity** (short for *Biological Diversity*) refers to the **variability among living organisms from all sources**, including, inter alia, terrestrial, marine, and other aquatic ecosystems and the ecological complexes of which they are part; this includes diversity within species (genetic), between species, and of ecosystems (Convention on Biological Diversity, UNEP, 1992).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *"Biodiversity is the sum total of all living things — the genes they carry, the species they form, and the ecosystems they build — and the natural processes that link them together."*

In the context of **Marine Pollution (OENST613)**, biodiversity is studied as a **biomonitoring endpoint** — i.e., a measurable biological response that indicates pollution stress on marine ecosystems.

---

## 1.2 The Three Hierarchical Levels of Biodiversity

| Level | Definition | Marine Example | Spatial Scale |
|-------|------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Genetic Diversity** | Variation in genes/alleles within a single species | Different color morphs of *Betta splendens* (Siamese fighting fish) in mangroves | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ m |
| **Species Diversity** | Variety of species within a region | Coral reef fish assemblage | $10^{2}$ to $10^{4}$ m |
| **Ecosystem Diversity** | Variety of habitats, communities & ecological processes | Mangroves, coral reefs, seagrass beds, abyssal plains | $10^{3}$ to $10^{7}$ m |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍎 Plain-English Analogy: "The Library of Life"**
>
> Imagine Earth's biodiversity as a **massive library**:
> - **Books** = Species
> - **Letters in each book** = Genes
> - **Sections (Science, History, Fiction)** = Ecosystems
>
> When we pollute the ocean (oil spill, plastic, heavy metals), it is like **setting fire to random books** in this library. We may never know which "sentence" (gene), "book" (species), or even "section" (ecosystem) we have lost forever. **Biodiversity is the insurance policy of life on Earth** — the more diverse it is, the more resilient the planet is to shocks.

---

## 1.4 Why Biodiversity Matters for Marine Pollution Studies

- **Biotic Integrity Indicator:** Pollution alters species composition; biodiversity indices quantify this shift.
- **Ecosystem Services:** Polluted ecosystems lose provisioning (fish), regulating (carbon sequestration), and cultural (tourism) services.
- **Bioaccumulation/Biomagnification:** Genetically diverse populations resist toxicant stress better than monocultures.
- **Baseline for Bioremediation:** Microbial diversity determines the natural capacity of an ecosystem to degrade pollutants.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"GSE = Gene → Species → Ecosystem"* (always read biodiversity from bottom-up).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Measure Biodiversity? (Operational Logic)

Pollution-induced stress does **not** simply kill all organisms equally — it **rearranges** community structure. Sensitive taxa disappear, opportunistic/tolerant taxa flourish. We measure this rearrangement using **diversity indices**.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Sample** a defined area (e.g., $1\ \text{m}^2$ quadrat on a coral reef).
2. **Identify & count** each species ($n_i$).
3. **Compute proportions** $p_i = n_i / N$ where $N = \sum n_i$.
4. **Plug into indices** to get a single number representing diversity.
5. **Compare** pristine vs. polluted sites statistically.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 equations — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Index / Parameter | Formula | Range / Ideal Value | What it Tells You |
|---|-------------------|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Shannon-Wiener ($H'$)** | $H' = -\sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \cdot \ln(p_i)$ | $0 \le H' \le \ln(S)$ | Information content of community; higher = more diverse |
| 2 | **Simpson's Index ($D$)** | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i^{\,2}$ | $0 \le D \le 1$ | Probability two random individuals are same species |
| 3 | **Simpson's Diversity ($1-D$)** | $1 - D = 1 - \sum p_i^{\,2}$ | $0 \le 1-D \le 1$ | Effective diversity; higher = more diverse |
| 4 | **Pielou's Evenness ($J'$)** | $J' = \dfrac{H'}{\ln(S)}$ | $0 \le J' \le 1$ | How evenly individuals are spread across species |
| 5 | **Species Richness ($S$)** | $S = \text{number of species}$ | Integer $\ge 1$ | Total count of distinct species |
| 6 | **Margalef's Index ($d$)** | $d = \dfrac{S - 1}{\ln(N)}$ | $d \ge 0$ | Richness normalized by total individuals |

### Definitions of Variables (Exam-Ready)

- $S$ = total number of species in the sample
- $N$ = total number of individuals across all species
- $n_i$ = number of individuals of the $i^{\text{th}}$ species
- $p_i = \dfrac{n_i}{N}$ = proportion of the $i^{\text{th}}$ species
- $\ln$ = natural logarithm (base $e \approx 2.71828$)

---

## 2.3 Engineering / Environmental Utility

| Application | How Biodiversity Metric is Used |
|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment)** | Baseline $H'$ measured; post-project $H'$ must not drop by $> 20\%$ |
| **MPA Design** (Marine Protected Areas) | Zones drawn to maximize $H'$ across reef mosaic |
| **Discharge Permit Compliance** | Effluent quality linked to benthic diversity at outfall |
| **Oil Spill Response** | Microbial $H'$ used to predict natural attenuation rate |
| **Aquaculture Siting** | Cage placement avoids high-ecosystem-diversity zones (seagrass/coral) |

---

## 2.4 Factors that Reduce Marine Biodiversity (Pollution-Driven)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "HIPPO" Mnemonic — KTU Examiner's Favourite:**
> - **H** — Habitat destruction (dredging, coastal reclamation)
> - **I** — Invasive species (ballast water)
> - **P** — Pollution (chemical, plastic, thermal, noise)
> - **P** — Population (overfishing / overexploitation)
> - **O** — Ocean acidification (linked to atmospheric CO$_2$)

---

## 2.5 Key Biodiversity Hotspots & Endemic Concepts

- **Endemic Species:** Species found *exclusively* in one geographic region (e.g., *Latimeria chalumnae* — coelacanth, off Comoros).
- **Biodiversity Hotspot:** A region with $\ge 1500$ endemic plant species **and** $\ge 70\%$ habitat loss (Myers et al., 2000 criterion — KTU accepted standard).
- **Marine Examples:** Coral Triangle (highest marine species richness), Sundarbans mangroves, Western Indian Ocean seamounts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem (Full Step-by-Step Solution)

### 📘 Problem Statement
A marine ecologist samples a tide-pool community after an oil spill and records the following abundance data:

| Species | Count ($n_i$) |
|---------|---------------|
| *Littorina* sp. (periwinkle) | 25 |
| *Nassarius* sp. (whelk) | 15 |
| *Pagurus* sp. (hermit crab) | 8 |
| *Actinia* sp. (sea anemone) | 2 |

**Calculate:**
1. Species Richness ($S$)
2. Pielou's Evenness ($J'$)
3. Shannon-Wiener Index ($H'$)
4. Simpson's Diversity Index ($1-D$)

---

### ✅ Step 1: Compute Total Individuals ($N$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \sum_{i=1}^{S} n_i \\
  &= 25 + 15 + 8 + 2 \\
  &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Total count is the simple sum of all species counts. This becomes the denominator for every proportion.

---

### ✅ Step 2: Compute Proportions ($p_i$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{n_1}{N} = \frac{25}{50} = 0.500 \\
p_2 &= \frac{n_2}{N} = \frac{15}{50} = 0.300 \\
p_3 &= \frac{n_3}{N} = \frac{8}{50}  = 0.160 \\
p_4 &= \frac{n_4}{N} = \frac{2}{50}  = 0.040
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $p_i$ is the relative contribution of each species. Sum check: $0.500 + 0.300 + 0.160 + 0.040 = 1.000$ ✓

---

### ✅ Step 3: Species Richness ($S$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{number of distinct species} \\
  &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Simply count the number of unique taxa recorded.

---

### ✅ Step 4: Shannon-Wiener Index ($H'$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
H' &= -\sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \cdot \ln(p_i) \\
   &= -\bigl[\, p_1 \ln(p_1) + p_2 \ln(p_2) + p_3 \ln(p_3) + p_4 \ln(p_4) \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H' &= -\bigl[\, (0.500)(\ln 0.500) + (0.300)(\ln 0.300) \\
   &\qquad + (0.160)(\ln 0.160) + (0.040)(\ln 0.040) \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each logarithm term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.500) &= -0.6931 \\
\ln(0.300) &= -1.2040 \\
\ln(0.160) &= -1.8326 \\
\ln(0.040) &= -3.2189
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by their respective $p_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.500)(-0.6931) &= -0.3466 \\
(0.300)(-1.2040) &= -0.3612 \\
(0.160)(-1.8326) &= -0.2932 \\
(0.040)(-3.2189) &= -0.1288
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing inside the bracket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= -0.3466 - 0.3612 - 0.2932 - 0.1288 \\
           &= -1.1298
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H' &= -(-1.1298) \\
   &= 1.1298
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Shannon's $H'$ uses natural log; higher $H'$ = more information content = more diverse community.

---

### ✅ Step 5: Pielou's Evenness ($J'$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
J' &= \frac{H'}{\ln(S)} \\
   &= \frac{1.1298}{\ln(4)} \\
   &= \frac{1.1298}{1.3863} \\
   &= 0.8150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Evenness $\in [0, 1]$. Here $J' = 0.815$ indicates a fairly even distribution despite one dominant species.

---

### ✅ Step 6: Simpson's Diversity Index ($1 - D$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i^{\,2} \\
  &= (0.500)^2 + (0.300)^2 + (0.160)^2 + (0.040)^2 \\
  &= 0.2500 + 0.0900 + 0.0256 + 0.0016 \\
  &= 0.3672
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - D &= 1 - 0.3672 \\
      &= 0.6328
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Simpson's $1-D$ ranges from 0 to 1. A value of $0.633$ indicates moderate-to-high diversity — even with a dominant species, the rare species contribute meaningfully.

---

## 3.2 Final Result Summary Table

| Index | Computed Value | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| $S$ | 4 | Four species recorded |
| $H'$ | **1.1298** | Low-moderate diversity (pollution-stressed) |
| $J'$ | **0.8150** | Reasonably even |
| $1 - D$ | **0.6328** | Moderate diversity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Tip:** Show the **sum-check** ($\sum p_i = 1$) explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark just for this verification step.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Index Selection Guide

| Situation | Best Index | Reason |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Many rare species | Shannon $H'$ | Sensitive to rare taxa (uses $\ln p_i$) |
| Dominant species matters | Simpson $1-D$ | Uses $p_i^2$ — emphasises common species |
| Sample sizes differ | Margalef $d$ | Normalises by $\ln(N)$ |
| Compare evenness | Pielou $J'$ | Removes richness effect |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biodiversity Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIODIVERSITY] --> B[Genetic Diversity]
    A --> C[Species Diversity]
    A --> D[Ecosystem Diversity]

    B --> B1[Allelic variation within population]
    B --> B2[Adaptation to local stress]
    B --> B3[Resilience to pollutants]

    C --> C1[Richness S]
    C --> C2[Evenness J prime]
    C --> C3[Shannon H prime]
    C --> C4[Simpson one minus D]

    D --> D1[Coral Reefs]
    D --> D2[Mangroves]
    D --> D3[Seagrass Beds]
    D --> D4[Deep Sea Vents]
    D --> D5[Open Pelagic Zones]

    E[Pollution Stressors] --> A
    E --> E1[Oil and Hydrocarbons]
    E --> E2[Heavy Metals]
    E --> E3[Plastic Debris]
    E --> E4[Nutrient Loading]
    E --> E5[Thermal Discharge]
    E --> E6[Noise Pollution]

    A --> F[Conservation Outcomes]
    F --> F1[MPA Design]
    F --> F2[Bioremediation]
    F --> F3[EIA Baseline]
    F --> F4[Restoration Targets]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
Pollution stressors feed into all three biodiversity levels, which in turn drive conservation actions.

---

## 4.2 Measurement Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Field Sampling] --> S2[Species Identification]
    S2 --> S3[Count Individuals ni]
    S3 --> S4[Compute pi = ni over N]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Index Formulas]
    S5 --> S6[Compare Pristine vs Polluted]
    S6 --> S7[Statistical Test ANOVA]
    S7 --> S8[Inference and Report]
```

---

## 4.3 Marine Pollution Impact Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pollutant Released] --> Q[Physicochemical Change]
    Q --> R[Primary Producer Decline]
    R --> T[Herbivore Reduction]
    T --> U[Predator Loss]
    U --> V[Simplified Food Web]
    V --> W[Reduced H prime and one minus D]
    W --> X[Ecosystem Service Loss]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define biodiversity. List the three levels of biodiversity with one marine example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Biodiversity is the variability among living organisms from all sources, including terrestrial, marine, and other aquatic ecosystems and the ecological complexes of which they are part (UNEP, 1992).

**Three Levels (2 marks):**
1. **Genetic Diversity** — variation in genes within a species. Example: Different color morphs of the clownfish *Amphiprion ocellaris* in the Lakshadweep reefs.
2. **Species Diversity** — variety of species in a region. Example: Over 600 coral species in the Gulf of Mannar.
3. **Ecosystem Diversity** — variety of habitats. Example: Mangroves, coral reefs, and seagrass beds along the Kerala coast.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is Pielou's Evenness Index? State its formula and range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pielou's Evenness Index measures how evenly individuals are distributed among the different species in a community.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**
$$
J' = \frac{H'}{\ln(S)}
$$

**Range (0.5 marks):** $J'$ lies between $0$ and $1$. A value close to $1$ indicates that all species have nearly equal abundance; a value near $0$ indicates dominance by one or a few species.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index** in detail. Derive its formula and state its range and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a benthic survey near a coastal effluent discharge point, the following counts were recorded: Species A: 30, Species B: 20, Species C: 10, Species D: 4. Calculate the **Shannon-Wiener Index ($H'$)** and **Simpson's Diversity Index ($1-D$)**. Comment on the ecological health of the site. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** The Shannon-Wiener Index ($H'$) was proposed by Claude Shannon in 1948 (information-theory context) and applied to ecology by Wiener's school. It measures the **information content** (uncertainty) of a randomly chosen individual belonging to a randomly chosen species in a community.

**Step 2 — Formula Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider a community with $S$ species having proportions $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_S$ such that $\sum p_i = 1$.

The information content (surprisal) of encountering species $i$ is:

$$
I_i = -\ln(p_i)
$$

The expected information (i.e., $H'$) is the weighted average of $I_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H' &= \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \cdot I_i \\
   &= \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \cdot \bigl[-\ln(p_i)\bigr] \\
   &= -\sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln(p_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Range & Interpretation (1 mark):**
- Maximum value: $H'_{\max} = \ln(S)$ (achieved when all species equally abundant).
- Minimum value: $H'_{\min} = 0$ (when one species dominates completely).
- Higher $H'$ = higher diversity.

**Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Sensitive to sample size (small samples underestimate $H'$).
2. Assumes random sampling and species independence.
3. Cannot detect rare species effectively.
4. Logarithm base affects absolute value (always specify base; KTU accepts natural log).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute $N$ and proportions (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 30 + 20 + 10 + 4 = 64 \\
p_A &= 30/64 = 0.4688 \\
p_B &= 20/64 = 0.3125 \\
p_C &= 10/64 = 0.1563 \\
p_D &= 4/64  = 0.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum check:** $0.4688 + 0.3125 + 0.1563 + 0.0625 = 1.0001 \approx 1.000$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute $H'$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H' &= -\bigl[(0.4688)\ln(0.4688) + (0.3125)\ln(0.3125) \\
   &\quad + (0.1563)\ln(0.1563) + (0.0625)\ln(0.0625)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.4688)(-0.7577) &= -0.3552 \\
(0.3125)(-1.1632) &= -0.3635 \\
(0.1563)(-1.8563) &= -0.2901 \\
(0.0625)(-2.7726) &= -0.1733
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H' &= -(-0.3552 - 0.3635 - 0.2901 - 0.1733) \\
   &= -(-1.1821) \\
   &= 1.1821
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $1 - D$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= (0.4688)^2 + (0.3125)^2 + (0.1563)^2 + (0.0625)^2 \\
  &= 0.2198 + 0.0977 + 0.0244 + 0.0039 \\
  &= 0.3458
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
1 - D = 1 - 0.3458 = 0.6542
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Ecological Health (1 mark):**
$H' = 1.18$ and $1-D = 0.65$ are **moderate** values. However, Species A dominates ($\sim 47\%$) indicating early signs of **community stress** consistent with effluent impact. Compared to pristine sites (typically $H' > 2.5$), the site shows **reduced diversity and possible pollution stress**.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **threats to marine biodiversity** with reference to the HIPPO framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Species Richness ($S$)**, **Margalef's Index ($d$)**, and **Pielou's Evenness ($J'$)**. How are they useful in monitoring the impact of oil spills on marine ecosystems? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(a)

The **HIPPO** framework (Secretariat of the CBD) summarises the five main drivers of biodiversity loss:

**1. Habitat Destruction (2 marks):** Dredging, coastal reclamation, trawling, and mangrove clearing destroy benthic habitats. The Gulf of Mannar has lost $\sim 15\%$ of seagrass cover due to port expansion.

**2. Invasive Species (1.5 marks):** Ballast-water discharge introduces non-native species (e.g., *Mnemiopsis leidyi* in the Black Sea) that outcompete natives.

**3. Pollution (1.5 marks):** Includes oil, plastics, heavy metals, nutrients, and noise. Each acts via different pathways (bioaccumulation, eutrophication, acoustic masking).

**4. Population Pressure (Overexploitation) (1 mark):** Overfishing reduces top predators, causing trophic cascades.

**5. Ocean Acidification (1 mark):** Atmospheric CO$_2$ lowers surface pH from $\sim 8.2$ to projected $7.8$, dissolving CaCO$_3$ shells of corals and molluscs.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Species Richness $S$ (1.5 marks):** Number of distinct species in a sample. Limitation: strongly dependent on sample size and sampling effort.

**Margalef's Index $d$ (2 marks):**
$$
d = \frac{S - 1}{\ln(N)}
$$
Normalises richness by total individuals, allowing comparison between samples of different sizes.

**Pielou's Evenness $J'$ (1.5 marks):**
$$
J' = \frac{H'}{\ln(S)}
$$
Measures how evenly individuals are distributed among species, independent of richness.

**Use in Oil Spill Monitoring (2 marks):**
- Pre-spill **baseline** of $S$, $d$, and $J'$ is established.
- Post-spill sampling at increasing distances from the spill site.
- Expected trend: $S$, $d$, $J'$ all **decrease** with proximity to spill.
- Recovery is tracked over months/years; $J'$ recovers faster than $S$ because tolerant species recolonise first.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $H' = -\sum p_i \ln(p_i)$. Students write $H' = +\sum p_i \ln(p_i)$ and get negative values — **immediate 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Wrong log base:** Always use natural log ($\ln$). Using $\log_{10}$ gives a different value — **state the base explicitly**.
> 3. **Missing sum-check** $\sum p_i = 1$. Examiners reward this with 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $D$ and $1-D$:** Simpson's original $D$ measures dominance; $1-D$ measures diversity. Cite both correctly.
> 5. **Writing $H'$ without units or interpretation:** A bare number is incomplete — always end with "high / low diversity" interpretation.
> 6. **No ecological comment in numerical questions:** Q.A(b) explicitly asks to "comment on health" — missing this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Biodiversity = variability at **gene**, **species**, and **ecosystem** levels (UNEP, 1992).
- ✅ Genetic diversity → Species diversity → Ecosystem diversity (read bottom-up).
- ✅ **Shannon-Wiener $H' = -\sum p_i \ln p_i$** → Range $0$ to $\ln S$.
- ✅ **Simpson $D = \sum p_i^2$** (dominance); **$1 - D$ = diversity**; Range $0$ to $1$.
- ✅ **Pielou $J' = H' / \ln S$** → evenness; Range $0$ to $1$.
- ✅ **Margalef $d = (S - 1) / \ln N$** → normalised richness.
- ✅ Always compute $N = \sum n_i$ first; then $p_i = n_i / N$; verify $\sum p_i = 1$.
- ✅ **HIPPO** mnemonic for threats: **H**abitat, **I**nvasive, **P**ollution, **P**opulation, **O**cean acidification.
- ✅ **Polluted sites** show **lower $H'$, lower $1-D$, lower $J'$** compared to pristine baselines.
- ✅ **Hotspot criteria (Myers):** $\ge 1500$ endemic plant species **AND** $\ge 70\%$ habitat loss.
- ✅ Examples to memorise: Coral Triangle (richest), Sundarbans (mangrove), Lakshadweep (coral).
- ✅ Always write units, log base, and an **ecological interpretation sentence** at the end.
- ✅ Examiner's favourite: numerical on Shannon + Simpson with a "comment on health" sub-part.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Biodiversity Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OENST613 Marine Pollution*
