topics.title: Prevention of Air and Water Pollution from Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prevention of Air and Water Pollution from Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prevention of Air and Water Pollution from Ships** refers to the consolidated set of internationally binding engineering, operational, and regulatory measures — codified primarily under the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)** of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — that govern the discharge of harmful substances into the marine atmosphere and water column by vessels of any flag State engaged in international voyages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the NEP-2020 Outcome-Based framework, this topic maps directly to **CO-2: Identify major sources of marine pollution and the regulatory mechanisms to control them**. Students must demonstrate knowledge of MARPOL Annexes I–VI, the **EEDI/EEXI/CII** energy efficiency framework, **Emission Control Areas (ECA)**, **SOx scrubbers**, **SCR**, **Ballast Water Management (BWM)** and **sewage/garbage handling**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating City" Picture

Imagine a **mid-sized coastal city of 50,000 inhabitants** suddenly pushed out to sea. Every day, this city:

- Burns **heavy fuel oil** thicker than cold molasses for electricity and transport → spewing **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, **nitrogen oxides (NOx)** and **black carbon**.
- Flushes toilets, showers, and kitchens directly overboard.
- Disposes of plastics, food waste, and machinery oil into the harbour.
- Pumps water in at one port (to stay stable) and squirts it out at another port — carrying **invasive species** like miniature stowaways.

That city is a **single container ship**. Prevention of pollution from ships is therefore the equivalent of installing **sewage treatment plants, scrubbers on chimneys, segregated waste-collection, and water-filtration systems** on this floating city — except the rules are global and enforced by a single United Nations body (IMO) through **MARPOL**.

## 1.3 Why It Matters — The Numbers

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Global Maritime Pollution Statistics (IMO/UNEP data)**
> - International shipping contributes approximately **2.89 % of global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions** (IMO Fourth GHG Study 2020).
> - If unregulated, shipping could represent **17 % of global CO₂ by 2050**.
> - Sulphur emissions from ships previously exceeded those from all global road vehicles combined.
> - **SOx Scrubbers (EGCS)** and **SCR units** are now retrofitted on **> 4,000 vessels** worldwide.

## 1.4 The MARPOL 73/78 Framework — At a Glance

MARPOL is the principal international convention covering prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes. It is a combination of two treaties adopted in **1973 and 1978**, and has been updated by numerous protocols and amendments.

| Annex | Pollutant Stream | Scope (KTU-Mandated Coverage) |
|------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Annex I** | Oil & Oily Water | Discharge limits, ODM, SBT, COW |
| **Annex II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | Categorization rules |
| **Annex III** | Packaged Harmful Substances | Marking & labelling |
| **Annex IV** | Sewage | Holding tanks, MSD |
| **Annex V** | Garbage | Zero plastic discharge |
| **Annex VI** | Air Pollution | SOx, NOx, GHG, EEDI, EEXI, CII |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:** Annex I (oil), Annex IV (sewage), Annex V (garbage) and Annex VI (air) carry the highest marks weightage. Annex II and III are descriptive only.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Six Layers of Pollution Control

Pollution from a ship arises from two channels:
1. **Operational (routine)** — every voyage produces emissions and waste.
2. **Accidental** — collisions, groundings, oil spills.

The KTU syllabus focuses on the **operational channel**, addressed through five hierarchical layers:

**Layer 1 — Source Reduction (Design Phase)**
- Cleaner fuel selection (LNG, methanol, ammonia, biofuels)
- Energy-efficient hull design, propeller optimization, air-lubrication
- Adoption of **EEDI**-compliant engines for new ships

**Layer 2 — Process Modification (Equipment)**
- **EGCS / Scrubbers** to remove SOx from exhaust
- **SCR / SNCR** units to convert NOx to N₂ and H₂O
- **BWMS** to treat ballast water (UV, electrolysis, filtration)

**Layer 3 — End-of-Pipe Treatment**
- Oily Water Separators (OWS) with **15 ppm** discharge limit
- Sewage Treatment Plants (STP)
- Incinerators for garbage

**Layer 4 — Containment**
- Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT), Double Hulls, Cofferdams
- Holding tanks for sewage and grey water in ECA zones

**Layer 5 — Behavioural / Procedural Controls**
- **SEEMP** (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan)
- **CII** rating trajectory, port-state control inspections
- Garbage Management Plan, Sewage Management Plan

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Energy Efficiency Indices

The IMO uses three flagship indices for air-pollution prevention from ships. Each follows a **"Emissions per Unit of Useful Transport Work"** philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Speed $V_{ref}$** is the ship's speed in calm water at **85 % of MCR** (Maximum Continuous Rating) at full draft. It is **NOT** the service speed.

### 2.2.1 Attained EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index) — *New Ships only*

$$
\text{EEDI}_{attained} \;=\; \frac{\left(\prod_{j=1}^{n} f_{j}\right) \cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^{nMe} P_{ME,i} \cdot C_{F,i} \cdot SFC_{ME,i}\right) + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{nPTI} P_{PTI,i} \cdot C_{F,i} \cdot SFC_{PTI,i}\right) - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{neff} f_{eff,i} \cdot P_{eff,i} \cdot C_{F,i} \cdot SFC_{eff,i}\right)}{f_{w} \cdot Capacity \cdot V_{ref}}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $P_{ME,i}$ | Power of main engine $i$ | kW |
| $P_{PTI,i}$ | Power of auxiliary propulsion (shaft generator) | kW |
| $P_{eff,i}$ | Power of innovative efficiency technology (negative credit) | kW |
| $C_{F,i}$ | Carbon emission factor of fuel $i$ (g CO₂ / g fuel) | dimensionless × 10⁶ |
| $SFC_{i}$ | Specific Fuel Consumption | g / kWh |
| $f_{j}$ | Correction factor (weather, ice-class, etc.) | dimensionless |
| $f_{w}$ | Capacity correction factor at non-default loading | dimensionless |
| $Capacity$ | Deadweight (DWT) or TEU or GT as per ship type | t / TEU |
| $V_{ref}$ | Reference service speed | knots |

The **Required EEDI** is:

$$
\text{EEDI}_{required} \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{X}{100}\right) \cdot \text{Reference Line Value}
$$

**Phase reductions (X)** for bulk carriers, tankers, container ships, gas carriers, general cargo:

| Phase | Year | Reduction (%) |
|-------|------|---------------|
| 1 | 2013 – 2014 | 0 |
| 2 | 2015 – 2019 | 10 |
| 3 | 2020 – 2024 | 20 |
| 4 | 2025 – 2032 | 30 |

### 2.2.2 Attained EEXI (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index) — *Existing Ships, applicable from 1 Jan 2023*

$$
\text{EEXI}_{attained} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Total CO}_2 \text{ emissions at } 83\%\text{MCR}}{\text{Capacity} \cdot V_{ref}}
$$

It is essentially the **same formulation as EEDI** but evaluated for vessels built before 1 Jan 2013, forcing them to retrofit or derate engines to comply.

### 2.2.3 CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator) — *Operational, applicable 2023*

$$
\text{CII}_{required} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{j} F_{j} \cdot C_{F,j}}{m_{\text{cargo}} \cdot V_{\text{actual}} \cdot t_{\text{voyage}}}
$$

The ship receives a grade **A → E**, with grades **D for 3 consecutive years** or a single **E** triggering mandatory **SEEMP corrective action**.

## 2.3 Sulphur Cap Equations (Annex VI, Chapter 3)

For fuel oil used or carried for use on board, the sulphur content by mass must satisfy:

$$
S_{\%} \;\leq\; S_{\text{cap}}
$$

| Zone | $S_{\text{cap}}$ (effective since) |
|------|------------------------------------|
| Global (outside ECA) | 0.50 % m/m (1 Jan 2020) |
| ECA (Baltic, North Sea, North America, US Caribbean) | 0.10 % m/m (1 Jan 2015) |
| ECA — new stricter limit (proposed) | 0.10 % m/m (universal ECA by 2025–27 discussion) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence via Scrubber:** A ship fitted with a **closed-loop or open-loop EGCS** can continue using HSFO (Heavy Fuel Oil with up to 3.5 % S) provided SOx emissions in the wash-water plume do not exceed what would result from burning 0.10 % S fuel.

## 2.4 NOx Tier Limits (Annex VI, Chapter 4)

$$
\text{NOx} \;=\; a \cdot n^{b} \quad \text{g / kWh}
$$

where $n$ = engine rated speed (rpm).

| Tier | $\text{NO}_x$ limit (g/kWh) for $n \leq 130$ | Year of Application |
|------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **I** | 17.0 | 2000 |
| **II** | 14.4 | 2011 |
| **III** | 3.4 | 2016 (in NECA), 2021+ globally under review |

## 2.5 Ballast Water Management Compliance

$$
\text{D-2 Standard: Organism concentration} \;\leq\; \text{IMO set limits}
$$

| Organism Class | Discharge Limit |
|----------------|------------------|
| Organisms $\geq$ 50 µm | $< 10$ viable organisms / m³ |
| Organisms 10–50 µm | $< 10$ viable organisms / ml |
| Indicator microbes (Vibrio cholerae, E. coli, intestinal Enterococci) | $< 1$ CFU / 100 ml (or specified CFU thresholds) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These indices are not merely academic — they directly drive:
- **Hull-form optimisation** (slender hulls, bulbous bows)
- **Waste-heat recovery systems**
- **Fuel-flex engines** capable of running LNG, methanol, ammonia
- **Slow-steaming** operational policies
- **Shore power (cold-ironing)** at berth
- **Wind-assisted propulsion** (rotor sails, kites)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — EEDI Numerical for a KTU-Style Problem

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]** A 75,000 DWT bulk carrier has a single main engine with the following data:
> - $P_{ME} = 18{,}500$ kW
> - $SFC = 168$ g/kWh
> - Fuel: HFO with $C_F = 3.114$ (g CO₂ / g fuel)
> - Shaft generator $P_{PTI} = 600$ kW, $SFC_{PTI} = 200$ g/kWh, same fuel
> - $V_{ref} = 14$ knots
> - No innovative efficiency technology ($P_{eff} = 0$)
> - All $f_j = 1.0$, $f_w = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Identify numerator terms.**

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; \prod_{j} f_{j}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{nMe} P_{ME,i}\cdot C_{F,i}\cdot SFC_{ME,i} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{nPTI} P_{PTI,i}\cdot C_{F,i}\cdot SFC_{PTI,i} \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{neff}\!\!\ldots
$$

**Step 2 — Compute main engine contribution.**

$$
P_{ME}\cdot C_F \cdot SFC_{ME} \;=\; 18{,}500 \times 3.114 \times 168
$$

$$
= 18{,}500 \times 523.152 \;=\; 9{,}678{,}312 \;\text{g CO}_2\text{/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute shaft generator contribution.**

$$
P_{PTI}\cdot C_F \cdot SFC_{PTI} \;=\; 600 \times 3.114 \times 200
$$

$$
= 600 \times 622.8 \;=\; 373{,}680 \;\text{g CO}_2\text{/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract innovation term (zero here).**

$$
\text{Numerator total} \;=\; 9{,}678{,}312 + 373{,}680 \;=\; 10{,}051{,}992 \;\text{g CO}_2\text{/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator (transport work × speed factor).**

$$
f_w \cdot Capacity \cdot V_{ref} \;=\; 1.0 \times 75{,}000 \times 14 \;=\; 1{,}050{,}000 \;\text{DWT·knots}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide numerator by denominator to obtain EEDI.**

$$
\text{EEDI}_{attained} \;=\; \frac{10{,}051{,}992}{1{,}050{,}000} \;\approx\; 9.57 \;\text{g CO}_2 / (\text{DWT·knot})
$$

**Step 7 — Compare to Required EEDI for Phase 2 bulk carrier (75 k DWT).**

Assume Reference Line Value $\approx 18.9$ g CO₂/(DWT·n) and reduction $X = 10$ %:

$$
\text{EEDI}_{required} \;=\; (1 - 0.10)\times 18.9 \;=\; 17.01
$$

**Step 8 — Compliance check.**

$$
9.57 \;\ll\; 17.01 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{SHIP COMPLIES with Phase 2 by a wide margin}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — SOx Scrubber Mass Balance

A 50 MW two-stroke main engine burns fuel oil at $S_{\%} = 2.5$ % m/m.

**Step 1 — Fuel consumption estimate.**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} \;\approx\; SFC \times P_{ME} \;\approx\; 170 \times 50{,}000 \;=\; 8{,}500{,}000 \;\text{g/h} \;=\; 8.5 \;\text{t/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Sulphur flow into combustion chamber.**

$$
\dot{m}_S \;=\; \dot{m}_{fuel}\times S_{\%} \;=\; 8.5\times 0.025 \;=\; 0.2125 \;\text{t S/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert S → SO₂ (molar mass ratio 64/32 = 2).**

$$
\dot{m}_{SO_2} \;=\; 2\times \dot{m}_S \;=\; 0.425 \;\text{t SO}_2\text{/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Scrubber removal efficiency required to meet 0.10 % S equivalent.**

Without scrubber, equivalent emission from 2.5 % S fuel:

$$
\dot{m}_{SO_2,0.1\%} \;=\; \dot{m}_{SO_2}\times \frac{0.10}{2.5} \;=\; 0.017\;\text{t/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Required scrubber efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{scrubber} \;\geq\; \frac{\dot{m}_{SO_2,actual} - \dot{m}_{SO_2,0.1\%}}{\dot{m}_{SO_2,actual}} \;\geq\; \frac{0.425 - 0.017}{0.425} \;\approx\; 96.0\,\%
$$

> This is why **all commercially certified scrubbers guarantee $\geq 97$ % SOx removal**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic for Pollution-Prevention Technology

| Problem | Engineered Solution | Underlying Principle |
|---------|--------------------|----------------------|
| SOx emission | EGCS Scrubber / Switch to VLSFO / LNG | Wet scrubbing, fuel-switching, fuel substitution |
| NOx emission | SCR (with urea), EGR, water injection | Selective catalytic reduction, exhaust cooling |
| GHG (CO₂) | EEDI hull optimisation, slow-steaming, wind-assist | Energy efficiency at design and operational level |
| Oil discharge | OWS 15 ppm + SBT + double hull | Gravity separation, containment |
| Sewage | STP + MSD + holding tank in ECA | Biological / chemical disinfection |
| Garbage | Incinerator + Garbage Record Book + port reception | Segregation, controlled disposal |
| Ballast water invasive species | BWMS (UV + electrochlorination) | UV sterilisation + hypochlorite generation |

## 3.4 Worked Derivation #3 — CII Sample Calculation

A containership $Capacity = 120{,}000$ DWT, distance sailed 50,000 nm in a year, total fuel consumed = 32,000 t of HFO.

**Step 1 — Total CO₂ emitted.**

$$
\text{CO}_2 \;=\; 32{,}000 \times 3.114 \;=\; 99{,}648\;\text{t CO}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Transport work.**

$$
W_{trans} \;=\; 120{,}000 \times 50{,}000 \;=\; 6.0\times 10^9\;\text{DWT·nm}
$$

**Step 3 — CII (attained).**

$$
\text{CII} \;=\; \frac{99{,}648\times 10^6\;\text{g}}{6.0\times 10^9\;\text{DWT·nm}} \;\approx\; 16.6\;\text{g CO}_2\text{/DWT·nm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with required CII (let's say 19 g). Since 16.6 < 19, ship earns a good (A or B) rating.**

## 3.5 Common Engineering Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfalls — Air Pollution Module**
> 1. Mixing **EEDI** (design) with **CII** (operational). They are not interchangeable.
> 2. Confusing **ECA** with **ECA port-of-call** — Baltic, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA.
> 3. Forgetting that the **0.10 %** ECA limit applies to *fuel used*, not necessarily emissions.
> 4. Treating **EGR** and **SCR** as equivalent — EGR reduces peak combustion temp (thermal NOx); SCR catalytically converts NO → NO₂ → N₂.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** State the global and ECA sulphur cap limits under MARPOL Annex VI and name the technology that allows continued use of high-sulphur fuel oil.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under MARPOL Annex VI (Chapter 3, Regulation 14):
- **Global sulphur cap (outside ECA)**: **0.50 % m/m** effective 1 Jan 2020.
- **ECA sulphur cap** (Baltic, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean): **0.10 % m/m** effective 1 Jan 2015.
- **Technology**: **Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS) / SOx Scrubber** — open-loop, closed-loop or hybrid — removes SOx from exhaust gas, allowing continued use of HSFO with up to 3.5 % sulphur, provided the equivalent SOx emission rate matches the 0.10 % fuel standard.

*(1 mark each for the two sulphur limits + 1 mark for scrubber technology.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between EEDI and CII in one sentence each. State the regulatory chapter of MARPOL Annex VI under which they fall.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** applies to **new ships** at the **design stage** and measures *CO₂ emissions per unit transport work at 85 % MCR*. Falls under **Chapter 4, Regulation 22** of MARPOL Annex VI.
- **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** applies to **all ships of ≥ 5,000 GT** on an **annual operational basis** and rates them **A to E**. Falls under **Chapter 4, Regulation 28**.

*(2 marks differentiation + 1 mark chapter reference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** Discuss in detail the **MARPOL Annex VI** framework for the **prevention of air pollution from ships**, covering SOx, NOx, GHG, and the energy efficiency indices. Include technological and operational solutions.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Model Answer:**

**1. MARPOL Annex VI — Overview (2 marks)**
- Adopted 1997, entered into force 2005. Six chapters.
- Applies to all vessels > 400 GT on international voyages; smaller vessels may also be governed by flag-State rules.

**2. SOx Regulations — Regulation 14 (2 marks)**
- Global sulphur cap: 0.50 % m/m from 2020.
- ECA limit: 0.10 % m/m.
- Compliance methods: low-sulphur fuel OR scrubber OR LNG.

**3. NOx Regulations — Regulation 13 (2 marks)**
- Tier I, II, III limits based on engine rated speed $n$ (rpm).
- Tier III mandatory in ECA for engines installed after 1 Jan 2016.
- Technologies: SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction), EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation), direct water injection.

**4. Particulate Matter and Black Carbon (1 mark)**
- Diesel particulate filters under consideration; 0.5 % S fuel also reduces PM.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Model Answer:**

**1. GHG — Regulation 23 + Chapters 4 amendments (2 marks)**
- EEDI mandatory for new ships.
- EEXI mandatory for existing ships (from 1 Jan 2023).
- CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator) operational rating (A–E).

**2. Energy Efficiency Technologies (2 marks)**
- Hull coating, air-lubrication, propeller boss cap fin, waste-heat recovery.
- LNG, methanol, ammonia as alternative fuels.
- Wind-assisted propulsion (rotor sails, kites).

**3. SEEMP (1 mark)**
- Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan — Part I (monitoring), Part II (targeted measures), Part III (CII corrective plan).

**4. Port-State Control & Inspections (1 mark)**
- IAPP Certificate (International Air Pollution Prevention).
- Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN) must record sulphur content.

**5. IMO 2023 Strategy (1 mark)**
- Indicative checkpoints: 20 % GHG reduction by 2030; net-zero by or around 2050.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions (Q-A)**
> 1. Confusing **ECA zones** (4 zones) and their individual entry-into-force dates.
> 2. Stating SOx limit as **3.5 %** (the historical cap) without specifying it was changed in 2020.
> 3. Omitting the **IAPP Certificate** and **BDN** — examiners deduct at least 1 mark for this.
> 4. Mixing **Tier II and Tier III applicability** with respect to ECA vs. global.

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Explain the **MARPOL Annex I (Oil), Annex IV (Sewage) and Annex V (Garbage)** requirements for the prevention of water pollution from ships, with diagrams and discharge limitations.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Annex I (Oil)**
**Model Answer:**

**1. Discharge Limits — Regulation 15 (2 marks)**
- Oil content in effluent from machinery space bilges must not exceed **15 ppm** (measured by ODME — Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System).
- Discharge prohibited within 50 nm from nearest land unless using a functioning OWS + ODME.

**2. Constructional Standards — Regulation 19 (2 marks)**
- Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT), Double Hull, Double Bottom for tankers.
- Oil tanker size thresholds (5,000 DWT for new builds).
- Slop tank capacity ≥ 3 % of cargo capacity.

**3. SOPEP — Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (1 mark)**
- Mandatory for all oil tankers and ships > 400 GT.
- Must be approved by flag administration.

**4. IOPP Certificate (1 mark)**
- International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate issued after survey.

**5. Example: ODME logic (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Discharge permitted only if:} \quad [Oil] < 15\;\text{ppm},\quad \text{Instantaneous rate} \le 60\;\text{L/nm}
$$

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Annex IV (Sewage) + Annex V (Garbage)**
**Model Answer:**

**Annex IV — Sewage (3 marks)**
- Definition: sewage = drainage from toilets/urinals; wastes from medical spaces; grey water from showers/sinks if mixed.
- **Discharge rules** (Regulation 11):
  - ≥ 12 nm from nearest land: discharge permissible if approved STP, with comminuted and disinfected effluent.
  - ≥ 3 nm: comminuted + disinfected only.
  - Within 3 nm: holding tank mandatory; use port reception facility.
- **Comminuter & Disinfector** — or full **STP (Sewage Treatment Plant)**.
- **ISPP Certificate** issued.

**Annex V — Garbage (4 marks)**
- Adopted 1973; MARPOL 73/78 Annex V in force 1988; major amendment in **2011** — **all plastics strictly prohibited for discharge anywhere**.
- Garbage categories and discharge distances:

| Garbage Type | Outside Special Area | Inside Special Area |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Food waste (comminuted) | ≥ 3 nm | ≥ 12 nm |
| Food waste (not comminuted) | ≥ 12 nm | Discharge prohibited |
| Plastics (all kinds) | Discharge prohibited | Discharge prohibited |
| Domestic waste, paper, rags | ≥ 12 nm | Discharge prohibited |
| Cooking oil, cargo residues | ≥ 12 nm | Discharge prohibited |

- **Garbage Management Plan** mandatory for all ships ≥ 100 GT.
- **Garbage Record Book** mandatory for ships ≥ 400 GT and every ship certified to carry ≥ 15 persons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions (Q-B)**
> 1. Forgetting to specify that **plastics are absolutely banned** from discharge under Annex V.
> 2. Confusing **Annex IV sewage** with **Annex V garbage** definitions (e.g., placing kitchen waste under Annex IV).
> 3. Forgetting the **15 ppm oil-in-water** limit and the **50 nm rule** in Annex I.
> 4. Skipping the **ISPP Certificate** for Annex IV.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rapid-Recall Checklist**

- **MARPOL 73/78** = umbrella treaty; 6 Annexes for operational pollution.
- **Annex VI Air Pollution** → SOx (Reg 14), NOx (Reg 13), GHG (Regs 22-28), EEDI/EEXI/CII, IAPP Certificate, Bunker Delivery Notes.
- **Sulphur Cap**: Global **0.50 %** (since 2020); ECA **0.10 %**.
- **NOx Tiers**: I → 17 g/kWh; II → 14.4 g/kWh; III → 3.4 g/kWh (in NECA, $n \le$ 130 rpm).
- **EEDI**: design-stage metric for **new ships only**; reduction phases 0, 10, 20, 30 % from 2013 to 2032.
- **EEXI**: existing-ship retrofit metric from **1 Jan 2023**.
- **CII**: operational, **A–E** rating, mandatory SEEMP Part III corrective plan if grade D (3 years) or E.
- **Scrubbers**: ≥ 97 % SOx removal efficiency; open-loop, closed-loop, hybrid.
- **SCR**: 80-95 % NOx reduction; urea (AdBlue) injection; works well in combination with EGR.
- **Ballast Water**: D-2 biological limits; BWM Convention in force since **8 Sept 2017**; BWMS (UV, electrochlorination).
- **Annex I Oil**: **15 ppm** discharge limit; SBT, double hull; ODME; IOPP Certificate.
- **Annex IV Sewage**: discharge ≥ 12 nm with STP; ≥ 3 nm if comminuted + disinfected; ISPP Certificate.
- **Annex V Garbage**: **plastics banned everywhere**; Garbage Management Plan (≥ 100 GT); Garbage Record Book (≥ 400 GT or ≥ 15 persons).
- **SEEMP**: 3-part plan (monitoring, measures, correction).
- **IAPP** Certificate is to air pollution what **IOPP** is to oil — keep them distinct.
- **IMO 2023 Strategy** targets: net-zero GHG by ~2050; 20 % reduction by 2030.

> End of Module 2 Topic Notes — *Prevention of Air and Water Pollution from Ships*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emissions from Ships Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Engine Emissions** are the gaseous, particulate, and thermal by-products released into the marine atmosphere as a direct consequence of the combustion of heavy fuel oil (HFO), marine gas oil (MGO), or alternative fuels (LNG, biofuels, methanol, hydrogen) in the **main propulsion engine**, **auxiliary diesel generators**, and **boilers** of a sea-going vessel.

These emissions constitute one of the largest regulated pollution streams in the marine environment, governed principally by **MARPOL Annex VI** of the International Maritime Organization (IMO). The regulated species are classified into:

| Class | Examples | Primary Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Acidifying Gases** | SO₂, NOₓ | Combustion of sulfur in fuel / high-T N₂ + O₂ reaction |
| **Greenhouse Gases (GHG)** | CO₂, CH₄, N₂O | Stoichiometric & incomplete combustion |
| **Particulate Matter (PM)** | Black Carbon (BC), Organic Carbon (OC), sulfates | Incomplete combustion, lube-oil ash |
| **Air Toxics / VOC** | CO, unburnt HC, formaldehyde | Local fuel-rich zones in cylinder |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Power Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a large container vessel as a **city-sized power plant floating on the ocean**. A single Panamax bulker can burn up to **~120 tonnes of heavy fuel oil per day** at design service speed. Just like a land-based thermal power plant, the flue gases (exhaust stack) carry away pollution, but unlike a land plant which is bound to one country’s emission rules, a ship moves between jurisdictions — making **global harmonized regulation (MARPOL Annex VI)** essential.

## 1.3 Why It Matters — Key Air-Pollution Statistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **Fourth IMO GHG Study (2020)**, international shipping accounted for approximately **~2.89% of global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions in 2018**, comparable to the entire annual emissions of a large industrialized nation like Germany. Without intervention, this share could rise to **~10–17% of global CO₂** by 2050 (baseline business-as-usual).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC)** of a modern 2-stroke MAN B&W main engine: **~165 g/kWh**
- **Lower calorific value (LCV) of HFO**: **~40.2 MJ/kg**
- **Mass fraction of carbon in HFO**: **~0.85 (85%)**
- **IMO Reference Carbon Factor**: **3.114 t CO₂ / t fuel** (based on 0.85 carbon fraction and 44/12 molar mass ratio)
- **Molecular mass ratio used by IMO**: $M_{CO_2}/M_C = 44/12 \approx 3.667$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry & Thermodynamics Behind the Pollutants

### 2.1.1 Sulfur Oxides (SOₓ)
Sulfur enters the combustion chamber organically bound in the fuel. It is fully oxidized to **SO₂** with a fraction further converted to **SO₃**:

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad (95\text{–}98\%)$$

$$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3 \quad (1\text{–}2\%, \text{catalysed by Pt/V catalysts})$$

The mass-balance relationship between **fuel sulfur content (FSC)** and **SO₂ emission rate** is the **single most heavily tested derivation** in this module.

### 2.1.2 Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)
NOₓ is predominantly **thermal NOₓ** formed via the **Zeldovich mechanism**:

$$O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$$
$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$$
$$N + OH \rightarrow NO + H$$

Activation energy ≈ **318 kJ/mol** — only achievable when **peak cylinder temperature > 1700 K** and **O₂ excess > 1%** is present.

### 2.1.3 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
CO₂ emission is fundamentally a **stoichiometric problem** dictated by fuel chemistry. The IMO simplified equation is:

$$m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_f \times 3.667$$

where $C_f$ is the **carbon factor** (mass fraction of carbon in fuel).

### 2.1.4 Particulate Matter (PM) & Black Carbon (BC)
PM forms via three routes:
1. **Incomplete combustion** of HC → soot (BC)
2. **Sulfate aerosols** from SO₃ → fine-mode PM₂.₅
3. **Lube-oil ash** containing Ca, V, Ni, S

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every equation** you are expected to know for ESE / internal assessments on this topic. Memorize the units as carefully as the symbols.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SO₂ emission rate per tonne fuel | $m_{SO_2} = m_{fuel} \times FSC \times 2.0$ | FSC in mass fraction; 64/32 = 2.0 stoichiometric factor |
| 2 | CO₂ per tonne fuel (IMO) | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_f \times 3.667$ | $C_f$ = carbon fraction (~0.85 HFO) |
| 3 | CO₂ per kg fuel (general) | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times \frac{44}{12} \times w_C$ | $w_C$ = mass fraction of carbon |
| 4 | Specific fuel oil consumption | $SFOC = \frac{m_{fuel}}{P_{shaft} \times t}$ | g/kWh |
| 5 | Brake-specific emission | $g_{NO_x} = \frac{m_{NO_x}}{P_{brake} \times t}$ | g/kWh |
| 6 | Scrubber SO₂ removal efficiency | $\eta_{SO_2} = \frac{C_{in}-C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Open/Closed/Hybrid loop |
| 7 | EEDI formula (simplified) | $EEDI = \frac{CO_2}{DWT \times V_{ref}}$ | g CO₂ / (tonne·nm) |
| 8 | Zeldovich rate constant | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius form |
| 9 | Air-Fuel ratio (stoich for HFO) | $AFR_{stoich} = \frac{1}{0.232}\left(\frac{C}{12}+\frac{H}{4}-\frac{O}{32}\right)$ | Mass basis |
| 10 | Brake power from SFOC | $P_B = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{SFOC \times 10^{-6}}$ | kW (SFOC in g/kWh) |

> **Pitfall:** Question 1 in the table uses the **mass ratio** 2.0 (i.e. 64/32), **NOT** 2.5. Students who confuse $SO_2$ with $SO_3$ lose marks.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas drive the design decisions in:
- **Scrubber sizing** — washing tower height computed from inlet/outlet SO₂ ratio
- **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** compliance — required ratio vs. attained ratio determines vessel rating (A–E)
- **EEXI (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index)** — limits installed power via shaft-power limiting devices
- **SOₓ Scrubber washwater discharge** — linked to **MARPOL Annex VI Reg. 4** and **Annex V**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — SO₂ Emission from Fuel Sulfur Content

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation is KTU-favourite:** It directly links a measurable fuel property (FSC, expressed as % m/m) to actual mass of pollutant discharged, which is exactly how port-state-control inspectors calculate compliance.

### Statement
For each kilogram of fuel oil containing a mass fraction $S$ of sulfur, derive the mass of $SO_2$ produced assuming complete combustion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the underlying reaction.**

$$S_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow SO_{2(g)}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate molar masses (using IUPAC standard atomic weights).**

$$M_S = 32.06 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_{SO_2} = 32.06 + 2(16.00) = 64.06 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass-balance starting from 1 kg of fuel.**

Mass of sulfur in 1 kg of fuel:
$$m_S = S \times 1 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert sulfur mass to moles.**

$$n_S = \frac{S}{32.06} \text{ mol per kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometry gives 1 mol S → 1 mol SO₂.**

$$n_{SO_2} = n_S = \frac{S}{32.06}$$

**Step 6 — Convert SO₂ moles back to mass.**

$$m_{SO_2} = n_{SO_2} \times 64.06 = \frac{S}{32.06} \times 64.06 = S \times \frac{64.06}{32.06}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the stoichiometric factor.**

$$\frac{64.06}{32.06} = 1.998 \approx 2.0$$

**Step 8 — Final closed-form equation.**

$$\boxed{\,m_{SO_2} = 2.0 \times S \times m_{fuel}\,}$$

For a fuel with **FSC = 3.5% (0.035 m/m)** and **m_fuel = 1 tonne**:

$$m_{SO_2} = 2.0 \times 0.035 \times 1000 = 70 \text{ kg SO}_2 \text{ per tonne fuel}$$

This explains why a 3.5% sulfur HFO produces **70 kg SO₂/tonne**, whereas an IMO 2020-compliant 0.5% fuel produces only **10 kg SO₂/tonne** — a **6× reduction**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — CO₂ Emission from Combustion (IMO Convention)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Combustion reaction for a generic hydrocarbon $C_xH_yS_zO_w$:**

$$C_xH_yS_zO_w + aO_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + zSO_2$$

**Step 2 — Mass-balance carbon atoms:**

Every **1 mol of C** in the fuel produces **1 mol of CO₂**. Therefore:

$$n_{CO_2} = n_C = \frac{m_{fuel} \times w_C}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass:**

$$m_{CO_2} = n_{CO_2} \times 44 = \frac{m_{fuel} \times w_C \times 44}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Define the IMO Carbon Factor:**

$$C_f = \frac{44}{12} \times w_C = 3.667 \times w_C$$

For HFO where $w_C \approx 0.85$:

$$C_f = 3.667 \times 0.85 = 3.114 \text{ t CO}_2 \text{ per t fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression.**

$$\boxed{\,m_{CO_2} = 3.114 \times m_{fuel}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Required Scrubber Removal Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Use-case:** A ship currently burns **3.5% S HFO**. To comply with the **0.1% S ECA limit**, the Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS) must remove a specific percentage of SO₂. This derivation produces the **design efficiency** for scrubber vendors.

**Step 1 — Without scrubber, SO₂ mass ratio equals:**

$$\left(\frac{SO_2}{fuel}\right)_{3.5\%} = 2 \times 0.035 = 0.070$$

**Step 2 — Compliant ratio (target ≤ 0.1% S equivalence):**

$$\left(\frac{SO_2}{fuel}\right)_{target} = 2 \times 0.001 = 0.002$$

**Step 3 — Required removal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{SO_2} = \frac{0.070 - 0.002}{0.070} \times 100\% = \frac{0.068}{0.070} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{SO_2} = 97.14\%\,}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The EGCS must achieve **at least 97.14% SO₂ reduction** — a value that **open-loop seawater scrubbers** can typically attain at **S/E ratios of ~45–50 kg seawater / kg exhaust**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — EEDI Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 12,000 TEU container vessel has reference service speed $V_{ref}=22$ kn, capacity $DWT=120{,}000$ t, fuel consumption at reference condition $m_f = 215$ t/day on HFO ($C_f = 3.114$). Compute the EEDI.

**Step 1 — Daily CO₂ emission.**

$$m_{CO_2} = 215 \times 3.114 = 669.51 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 2 — Power-to-mass per nautical mile.**

$$EEDI = \frac{669.51}{120{,}000 \times 22} \text{ t CO}_2/(\text{t}\cdot\text{nm})$$

$$\boxed{\,EEDI = 2.536 \times 10^{-4} \text{ t CO}_2/(\text{t}\cdot\text{nm}) = 253.6 \text{ g CO}_2/(\text{t}\cdot\text{nm})\,}$$

**Step 3 — Phase-2 required EEDI (2025–2029) for this size class is ~30% reduction from baseline = ~177 g/t·nm.**

→ The ship is **non-compliant** and requires power limitation or engine derating.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Compliance Matrix (Tier Standards for NOₓ)

| Engine Speed (n, rpm) | **Tier I** (2000) | **Tier II** (2011) | **Tier III** (2016+, in NECAs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| n < 130 | 17.0 g/kWh | 14.4 g/kWh | 3.4 g/kWh |
| 130 ≤ n < 2000 | 45 × n⁻⁰·² | 44 × n⁻⁰·²³ | 9 × n⁻⁰·² |
| n ≥ 2000 | 9.8 g/kWh | 7.7 g/kWh | 2.0 g/kWh |

> **Examiner trap:** Many students write "Tier III applies globally." It does **NOT** — Tier III applies only when the ship operates in a **NECA (Nitrogen Oxide Emission Control Area)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Flow: From Fuel to Atmospheric Emission

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heavy Fuel Oil Storage Tank] --> B[Fuel Treatment Separator]
    B --> C[Main 2-Stroke Diesel Engine]
    B --> D[Auxiliary Diesel Generators]
    B --> E[Auxiliary Boiler]
    C --> F[Turbocharger]
    D --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    E --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    F --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    G --> H{EGCS Scrubber?}
    H -->|Yes| I[SO2 Removed via Seawater Scrubbing]
    H -->|No| J[SOx Released to Stack]
    H -->|Yes| K[NOx Released to Stack]
    J --> L[Atmosphere / Plume Dispersion]
    I --> L[Atmosphere / Plume Dispersion]
    K --> L[Atmosphere / Plume Dispersion]
    M[SCR / EGR Unit] -->|Reduces NOx| C
    N[Fuel Sulfur Cap Compliance Check] --> B
    O[Continuous Emission Monitoring System CEMS] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** The diagram shows that pollution control can be done at **three intervention points**:
1. **At source** — fuel treatment (low-S fuel), engine design (Tier III, SCR/EGR)
2. **In stream** — EGCS scrubber for SO₂, SCR catalyst for NOₓ
3. **At stack** — CEMS for monitoring & verification

---

## 4.2 Regulatory Architecture — MARPOL Annex VI Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[IMO MARPOL Annex VI Regulations for Air Pollution]
    Root --> R1[Reg 12 - SOx and Particulate Matter]
    Root --> R2[Reg 13 - NOx Tier Standards]
    Root --> R3[Reg 14 - Sulfur Cap of Fuel Oil]
    Root --> R4[Reg 22A - EEXI Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index]
    Root --> R5[Reg 23 - CII Carbon Intensity Indicator Rating]
    Root --> R6[Reg 24 - Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan SEEMP]
    R1 --> ECAs1[Sox Emission Control Areas]
    R2 --> NECAs[Nox Emission Control Areas]
    ECAs1 --> Baltic[Baltic Sea]
    ECAs1 --> NSea[North Sea]
    ECAs1 --> NAmECA[North American ECA]
    ECAs1 --> USCarib[US Caribbean Sea ECA]
    ECAs1 --> MedECA[Mediterranean Sea ECA 2025]
    NECAs --> Baltic2[Baltic Sea]
    NECAs --> NAmNECA[North American NECA]
```

---

## 4.3 NOₓ Reduction Technology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to reduce NOx?] --> OperateArea{Operating in NECA?}
    OperateArea -->|No| Tier2[Comply with Tier II: 7.7 to 14.4 g per kWh]
    OperateArea -->|Yes| Tier3[Must achieve Tier III: 2.0 to 3.4 g per kWh]
    Tier3 --> TechChoice{Retrofit or Newbuild?}
    TechChoice -->|Newbuild| NewEngine[Select Tier III Engine Long Stroke, Low Pressure SCR]
    TechChoice -->|Retrofit| OldEng{Engine Tier I or II?}
    OldEng -->|Tier II| ModEng[Engine Tuning: Miller Cycle, Reduced Pilot Injection]
    ModEng -->|Still Not Compliant| AddSCR[Install Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]
    OldEng -->|Tier I| EngineMod[Major Engine Mod or Fuel Switch LNG]
```

---

## 4.4 SO₂ Compliance Pathway — Fuel Switch vs. Scrubber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Ship Owner Compliance Decision] --> Q1{Operational Area Mix?}
    Q1 -->|Mostly Global Routes| FuelSwitch[Switch to 0.5 percent S VLSFO or MGO]
    Q1 -->|Frequent ECA Voyages| Q2{Capital vs Operating Cost Tradeoff?}
    Q2 -->|Low Capital| BunkerSwitch[Switch to 0.1 percent S Fuel in ECA]
    Q2 -->|High Capital High Op Efficiency| InstallScrub[Install Hybrid EGCS Scrubber]
    InstallScrub --> Washwater[Manage Washwater Discharge per MEPC Resolution 340 77]
    FuelSwitch --> Compliant[Compliant Annex VI Reg 14]
    BunkerSwitch --> Compliant
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Sulfur Emission Control Area" (SOₓ-ECA) under MARPOL Annex VI. List any **three** currently designated SOₓ-ECAs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **SOₓ Emission Control Area** is a sea region designated by the IMO where stricter controls on sulfur oxides emissions from ships are enforced, requiring either:
> (a) use of low-sulfur fuel oil (≤ **0.10% m/m** FSC), OR
> (b) equivalent SO₂ reduction via an approved **Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS / scrubber)**.

The currently designated SOₓ-ECAs are:
1. **Baltic Sea** (in force since 2006)
2. **North Sea** (in force since 2007)
3. **North American ECA** (in force since 2012)
4. **United States Caribbean Sea ECA** (2014)
5. **Mediterranean Sea ECA** (effective **1 May 2025**)

*(Any three for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is **Black Carbon (BC)**? Why is it considered the second-most important climate-forcing agent from shipping after CO₂?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Black Carbon (BC)** is the **light-absorbing carbonaceous component of particulate matter (PM)** emitted from incomplete combustion of hydrocarbon fuels in marine diesel engines. It consists of sub-micron spherules of nearly pure elemental carbon (~95–98% C) with traces of H, S and N.
>
> BC is climate-significant because:
> 1. Its **mass absorption cross-section** is among the highest of any atmospheric aerosol (~7.5 m²/g at 550 nm).
> 2. When deposited on snow/ice, it reduces **albedo**, accelerating cryosphere melt — a **positive feedback loop**.
> 3. Its **lifetime in the atmosphere** is short (days to weeks), so reducing BC delivers **immediate near-term climate benefit**, unlike CO₂ which has multi-century residence time.
> 4. BC also acts as a carrier for **toxic PAHs and heavy metals** (V, Ni), posing human health risk at port cities.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to Dec 2024 paper pattern]**

**(a)** A bulk carrier operates on **HFO with FSC = 2.8% m/m** and burns **95 tonnes/day** on the main engine plus **8 tonnes/day** on auxiliary engines.
1. Calculate the **daily SO₂ emission in kg/day**.
2. The vessel re-routes via the **Baltic Sea ECA**. If it switches to **0.1% S MGO**, calculate the **% reduction in SO₂ emission** achieved per day.
3. If the owner instead installs a scrubber of efficiency **97%**, compute the **SO₂ emitted per day** and state whether compliance is achieved. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neatly labelled block diagram, explain the working principle of an **Open-Loop Seawater Scrubber** for SO₂ removal. List any **four** limitations of the open-loop system. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Step 1 — SO₂ from main engine (95 t/day, 2.8% S).**

$$m_{SO_2,main} = 2.0 \times 0.028 \times 95 \times 1000 = 5320 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — SO₂ from auxiliaries (8 t/day, 2.8% S).**

$$m_{SO_2,aux} = 2.0 \times 0.028 \times 8 \times 1000 = 448 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Total SO₂ (pre-ECA).**

$$m_{SO_2,total} = 5320 + 448 = 5768 \text{ kg/day} \approx 5.77 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 4 — Total SO₂ after switching all engines to 0.1% MGO.**

$$m_{SO_2,MGO} = 2.0 \times 0.001 \times 103 \times 1000 = 206 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction.**

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{5768 - 206}{5768} \times 100\% = 96.43\%$$

**Step 6 — Scrubber option (97% efficiency on HFO).**

$$m_{SO_2,scrubber} = 5768 \times (1 - 0.97) = 173.04 \text{ kg/day}$$

Compare to compliant level (using 0.1% S, 103 t/day): 206 kg/day.

Since **173.04 < 206**, **compliance achieved** ✓ (with a small safety margin).

---

#### Model Solution for A(b)

**Working Principle of Open-Loop Seawater Scrubber:**

1. **Intake** — Seawater is drawn from the engine cooling system or sea chest.
2. **Scrubbing tower entry** — Hot exhaust gas (180–400°C) enters the **bottom** of a vertical packed tower/counter-current spray column.
3. **Counter-current contact** — Seawater sprayed from top absorbs SO₂ via:
$$\begin{aligned}
SO_2 + H_2O &\rightleftharpoons H_2SO_3 \\
H_2SO_3 &\rightleftharpoons H^+ + HSO_3^- \\
HSO_3^- &\rightleftharpoons H^+ + SO_3^{2-}
\end{aligned}$$
4. **Acidic washwater collection** — the now acidic water (pH ~2–3) flows to a **holding tank** and is neutralised if required.
5. **Clean exhaust discharge** — Purified gas (T ~ 50°C, SO₂ < target) is released via stack.

**Four Limitations of Open-Loop Systems:**
1. **Sensitive water-quality:** Only usable in seas with **pH > 6.5, alkalinity > 2 mmol/L**.
2. **Restricted in harbours:** Many ports (e.g., California, Germany) **ban discharge of scrubber washwater** → requires closed-loop hybrid mode.
3. **Scaling & fouling:** Re-mineralised water forms **calcium-sulfate scale** in internals.
4. **Plume visibility:** Adds **slight plume opacity** (sulfate aerosols) — public-perception issues.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[Modeled on KTU Jul 2023 pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Zeldovich mechanism** for thermal NOₓ formation. Explain **four engine design / operational strategies** by which shipbuilders comply with **IMO Tier III** NOₓ standards. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A ship has reference speed **20 knots**, **DWT = 80,000 t**, fuel consumption **175 t/day** on HFO.
1. Compute its **attained EEDI**.
2. If the Phase-2 required EEDI for this size is **16.7 g/t·nm**, determine whether the ship is compliant.
3. Suggest **two** practical engineering retrofits to achieve compliance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for B(a)

**Zeldovich Mechanism:**
Thermal NOₓ is formed via the extended Zeldovich chain:

$$\begin{aligned}
O + N_2 &\rightarrow NO + N \quad\quad\quad \text{(rate-limiting)} \\
N + O_2 &\rightarrow NO + O \\
N + OH &\rightarrow NO + H \quad \text{(extended)}
\end{aligned}$$

The **first step** requires breaking the strong N≡N triple bond (**945 kJ/mol**) and is only kinetically significant above **1700 K**, where Arrhenius rate constants become appreciable.

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1[O][N_2] + k_2[N][O_2] + k_3[N][OH]$$

where $k_i = A_i \exp(-E_a^i / RT)$, with $E_a^1 \approx 318$ kJ/mol.

**Four Strategies to Achieve Tier III NOₓ Compliance:**

1. **Selective Catalical Reduction (SCR):** Inject aqueous urea (AdBlue) → NH₃ → reduces NO to N₂ on a catalyst (V₂O₅/TiO₂). Up to **95% NOₓ reduction**.
2. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Re-route 25–40% cooled exhaust back to scavenge air, lowering peak combustion temperature below 1700 K.
3. **Miller Cycle / Long-Stroke Engine:** Increase stroke-to-bore ratio, achieve late-closure intake → lower effective compression ratio → lower peak T.
4. **Two-Stage Turbocharging / Common-Rail Fuel Injection:** Optimise injection timing, multiple pulses → suppress pre-mixed combustion peak.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b)

**Step 1 — Daily CO₂.**

$$m_{CO_2} = 175 \times 3.114 = 544.95 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 2 — Attained EEDI.**

$$EEDI_{attained} = \frac{544.95}{80{,}000 \times 20} \text{ t CO}_2 /(\text{t}\cdot\text{nm})$$

$$\boxed{\,EEDI_{attained} = 3.406 \times 10^{-4} \text{ t/(t·nm)} = 340.6 \text{ g/(t·nm)}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check.**

Required = **16.7 g/(t·nm)**. Attained = **340.6 g/(t·nm)**.
Required >> Attained? **No — required is much lower than attained.**

$$\text{Non-compliance margin} = 340.6 - 16.7 = 323.9 \text{ g/(t·nm) — ship is grossly non-compliant}$$

*(Note for students: the required EEDI of ~16.7 g/t·nm is **typical for very large container vessels post-2025 reduction phases**. For 80,000 DWT bulkers the actual required value will be larger — recheck IMO Resolution MEPC.364(79) reference line; here we test arithmetic clarity.)*

**Step 4 — Two Engineering Retrofits:**
1. **Shaft Power Limitation (ShaPoLi):** Mechanically or electronically cap maximum continuous rating (MCR) → directly lowers SFOC-load alignment and hence EEDI.
2. **Air Lubrication System / Hull Cleaning:** Reduces frictional resistance by 5–10% → lowers required propulsive power → lower fuel consumption → lower CO₂ → improved EEDI.
*(Alternatives: WAPS (Wind-Assisted Propulsion), Flettner rotors, fuel switch to LNG, hull-form optimization, propeller duct retrofit.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Most Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the molecular mass of SO₂ (64, not 80).** Use 64/32 = 2.0, not 64/32 ≈ 2.0 with extra decimals lost.
> 2. **Mixing up EEDI vs. EEXI vs. CII.** EEDI applies to **newbuilds**, EEXI applies to **existing ships**, CII is a **continuous operational rating** (A–E).
> 3. **Treating Tier III as global.** It is **NECA-only**.
> 4. **Quoting IMO 2020 sulfur cap as 0.1%** instead of **0.5%** globally. (0.1% is the **ECA** cap.)
> 5. **Carbon factor confusion:** Some students multiply by **3.114** (which already includes $w_C=0.85$), and then again by 0.85, **double-counting**.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ Ship engine emissions include **SOₓ, NOₓ, CO₂, CO, PM/BC, VOC**, governed by **MARPOL Annex VI**.
- ✅ The **SO₂ emission formula** is **$m_{SO_2} = 2.0 \times FSC \times m_{fuel}$** — derive, memorize and re-derive.
- ✅ The **IMO CO₂ conversion factor** is **3.114 t CO₂/t fuel** for HFO ($C_f = 3.667 w_C$).
- ✅ **Global sulfur cap (2020): 0.5% m/m; ECA cap: 0.10% m/m.**
- ✅ **NOₓ Tiers:** I (2000) → II (2011) → III (2016, NECAs only).
- ✅ Tier III: **2.0 g/kWh (≥2000 rpm), 3.4 g/kWh (<130 rpm)**, and $9 \cdot n^{-0.2}$ in between.
- ✅ **EEXI** = single-attestation design index; **CII** = annual operational rating; **EEDI** = newbuild index.
- ✅ **Scrubbers** must achieve ~**97.14% SO₂ removal** to bridge 3.5% → 0.1% S.
- ✅ **Thermal NOₓ** follows the **Zeldovich mechanism**, requires **T > 1700 K**.
- ✅ Emission-control hardware: **SCR** (NOₓ), **EGR** (NOₓ), **EGCS/scrubber** (SO₂), **CEMS** (monitoring).
- ✅ Designated SOₓ-ECAs: **Baltic, North Sea, N. American, US Caribbean, Mediterranean (2025)**.
- ✅ Designated NECAs: **Baltic, North American** (only two currently).
- ✅ Black carbon = **light-absorbing sub-micron soot**; high climate-warming efficiency; short atmospheric lifetime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Oil Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Oil Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Oil Quality (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Fuel oil quality* refers to the **complete set of physical, chemical, and thermal properties** that determine the suitability of a marine fuel oil for safe combustion, efficient energy conversion, and compliance with environmental emission regulations aboard ocean-going vessels. It is governed primarily by the **ISO 8217:2017** international standard (and its annual amendments) and constrained by **MARPOL Annex VI** of the International Maritime Organization (IMO), which caps sulfur content and other pollutants.

In marine engineering parlance, fuel oil quality is not just "how well it burns" — it is a *multi-parameter fingerprint* covering **~20 measurable characteristics** that together predict how a fuel will behave during storage, handling, pumping, atomization, combustion, and post-combustion exhaust release.

### The Seven Pillars of Marine Fuel Oil Quality

| # | Quality Parameter | Practical Significance |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Density (API / SG)** | Determines energy per unit volume; affects tank calibration |
| 2 | **Kinematic Viscosity** | Governs atomization quality and pumpability |
| 3 | **Sulfur Content (% m/m)** | Drives SOₓ emissions & ECA compliance |
| 4 | **Flash Point (°C)** | Safety against accidental ignition |
| 5 | **Pour Point (°C)** | Lowest storage/flow temperature |
| 6 | **Net Specific Energy (MJ/kg)** | Energy released per kilogram of fuel |
| 7 | **Water & Sediment (% V/V)** | Quality degradation and sludge formation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love linking a *single fuel property* to its *environmental consequence*. Always remember — *sulfur → SO₂/SO₃ → acid rain & respiratory illness*; *high asphaltenes → incombustibles → particulate matter (PM) → air pollution*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Mental Model

Imagine you walk into a café and order coffee. The barista asks:
- *Strong or mild?* → That's **calorific value** (energy density).
- *Hot or warm?* → That's **flash point / pour point** (thermal behavior).
- *With sugar or without?* → That's **sulfur content** (impurity loading).
- *Thin filter coffee or thick decoction?* → That's **viscosity**.
- *Pure or diluted with water?* → That's **water & sediment content**.

If you get *thick, sugary, watered-down cold coffee*, the cup is unusable. Similarly, if your marine fuel oil has **high viscosity, high sulfur, high water, and a low flash point**, the engine suffers — and so does the marine environment. **Fuel oil quality is essentially the "taste test" of marine bunkers**, standardized so every ship engineer worldwide can predict performance.

## 1.3 Why Fuel Oil Quality Matters in Marine Pollution

A low-quality fuel produces:

- **Air Pollution:** SOₓ, NOₓ, CO₂, PM (particulate matter), black carbon.
- **Water Pollution:** Bilge sludge, leaked HFO, oily water from purifier discharge.
- **Operational Pollution:** Engine failure leading to emergency bunker spills.
- **Economic Pollution:** Excess fuel consumption due to poor calorific value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL Annex VI Cap (Global):** **0.50% m/m sulfur** since 2020.
> **ECA Cap (Baltic, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean):** **0.10% m/m sulfur**.

These regulatory limits are the *entire reason* "fuel oil quality" is a pollution module topic — not just an engineering convenience.

---

## 1.4 Heavy Fuel Oil vs. Marine Diesel Oil — A Quick Distinction

$$\boxed{\text{HFO} = \text{Residual fuel (heavy)}\;\;\big|\;\;\text{MDO/MGO} = \text{Distillate fuel (light)}}$$

**HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil):** A residual product of crude oil refining — the *thick tar-like* leftover after gasoline, diesel, and kerosene have been distilled out. Examples: **IFO 380, IFO 180, RMG 380** (ISO 8217:2017 grades).

**MDO/MGO (Marine Diesel Oil / Marine Gas Oil):** A *distillate blend* — cleaner, lighter, more expensive. Examples: **DMA, DMB, DMZ** grades under ISO 8217.

> [!NOTE]
> The **number** in *IFO 380* refers to the **maximum kinematic viscosity in cSt at 50°C** — i.e., IFO 380 must have a viscosity ≤ **380 cSt at 50 °C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Density & Specific Gravity — Why Energy per Liter Matters

Density ($\rho$) is the **mass per unit volume** of fuel oil, typically measured in **kg/m³** at **15°C** (per ISO 8217 reference temperature). Specific Gravity (SG) is the ratio of fuel density to water density at the same temperature.

**Why it matters:** A denser fuel packs more mass (and hence more energy) into a tank of fixed volume. However, if the density is *too high*, atomization suffers because heavier molecules don't vaporize easily in the combustion chamber.

### Density → API Gravity Conversion

The American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity is the industry-preferred way to express fuel "lightness":

$$\text{API Gravity} = \frac{141.5}{\text{SG at } 60°\text{F}} - 131.5$$

> Lower API = heavier fuel (e.g., HFO ≈ 10–20° API). Higher API = lighter fuel (e.g., diesel ≈ 35–45° API).

## 2.2 Viscosity — The Atomization Gatekeeper

**Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)** measures the fuel's resistance to flow under gravity, expressed in **mm²/s (centistokes, cSt)** at a reference temperature (usually **50°C** for residual fuels, **40°C** for distillates).

**Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** measures resistance under applied shear stress, in **Pa·s** or **cP (centipoise)**.

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$$

where $\rho$ is the density at the same temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why viscosity governs pollution:** If viscosity is too **high**, fuel droplets from the injector are large → incomplete combustion → **black smoke, PM, unburnt hydrocarbons (UHC), and carbon deposits** on cylinder liners and turbocharger blades. If too **low**, lubrication of fuel injection pumps drops → wear and possible pump seizure.

## 2.3 Sulfur Content — The Pollution Kingpin

Sulfur is the **single most regulated parameter** in marine fuel quality. It is measured as a percentage by mass (% m/m).

**The pollution chain is brutally simple:**

$$\text{Fuel S} \xrightarrow{\text{Combustion}} \text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{O}_2 / \text{UV}} \text{SO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\;(\text{acid rain})$$

Sulfur also poisons **catalytic converters** and **scrubbers**, and forms **sulfate particulate matter (PM₂.₅)** — a respiratory hazard.

## 2.4 Flash Point & Fire Safety

The **flash point** is the lowest temperature at which fuel vapors can momentarily ignite when exposed to an open flame. ISO 8217 mandates a **minimum flash point of 60°C** for safety during storage, transfer, and handling.

> [!NOTE]
> Flash point is *not* the same as auto-ignition temperature. **Flash point ≈ 60–80°C** for HFO; **auto-ignition ≈ 250–400°C**.

## 2.5 Pour Point, Cloud Point & Cold Flow

- **Pour Point (PP):** Lowest temperature at which the fuel still flows. Critical for **winter navigation** in polar or northern waters.
- **Cloud Point (CP):** Temperature at which wax crystals first appear (fuel looks cloudy).
- **Cold Filter Plugging Point (CFPP):** Lowest temperature at which fuel passes a standardized filter.

$$\text{CP} > \text{CFPP} > \text{PP (lower temperature)} \quad \text{typically}$$

## 2.6 Calorific Value — The Energy Bank

The **Net Specific Energy (NCV)** or **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of marine residual fuel ranges typically from **~39 to 42 MJ/kg**. Distillates (MGO/MDO) are slightly higher at **~42.5–43 MJ/kg**.

A useful empirical correlation (API Technical Data Book):

$$\text{NCV} \;[\text{MJ/kg}] \approx 46.423 - 9.155 \cdot \text{SG} - 0.0815 \cdot \text{Sulfur\%} - 0.259 \cdot \text{Water\%}$$

## 2.7 ISO 8217:2017 — The Industry Bible

ISO 8217 categorizes marine fuels into **two families**:

| Family | Description | Examples |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Distillate (DF)** | Cleaner, low-residue | DMA, DMZ, DMB |
| **Residual (RF)** | Heavy, viscous, residual-grade | RMA 10, RMB 30, RMD 80, RME 180, RMG 380, RMK 700 |

Each grade has its **own table of limits** for viscosity, density, sulfur, flash point, pour point, ash, water, etc.

## 2.8 🚀 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these — they appear in 7-mark and 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Practical Use |
|---|-------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | $\mu$ = Pa·s, $\nu$ = m²/s, $\rho$ = kg/m³ | Dynamic viscosity from kinematic |
| 2 | $\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5$ | SG at 60°F | Density → API gravity |
| 3 | $\text{NCV} \approx 46.423 - 9.155\cdot\text{SG} - 0.0815\cdot\text{S}\% - 0.259\cdot\text{W}\%$ | MJ/kg | Empirical calorific value |
| 4 | $\text{SFOC} = \frac{m_f}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Specific fuel oil consumption |
| 5 | $\eta_B = \frac{P_{brake}}{m_f \cdot \text{NCV}}$ | dimensionless | Brake thermal efficiency |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = m_f \cdot \text{S\%} \cdot 2.0$ | g/s | SO₂ mass emission rate |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = m_f \cdot 3.114$ | (per g fuel) | CO₂ emission factor |
| 8 | $\text{SG} = \frac{\rho_{\text{fuel}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | at 15°C | Specific gravity definition |
| 9 | Reynolds No. (fuel pipe): $Re = \frac{vD}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 10 | $P_{brake} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N \cdot T}{60}$ | W or kW | Brake power from torque |

> **Anti-pitfall:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ inside tables, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ — never raw `|x|` (breaks markdown).

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Bunker Quantity Calculations (BQS):** Density × volume × temperature correction → tons of fuel received.
2. **Scrubber Sizing:** SO₂ mass flow dictates NaOH or seawater scrubber capacity.
3. **Purifier Calibration:** Density difference between oil and water/sludge drives separation efficiency.
4. **Engine Tuning:** Viscosity at the injector (post-heater) sets the injection pressure profile.
5. **Emission Monitoring (MRV):** IMO DCS uses $\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = \text{fuel} \cdot \text{EF}$ where EF = emission factor (3.114 for HFO, 3.206 for MGO/Diesel).

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Note to Students:** Every line below is worked out step by step. KTU board examiners **reward clarity of each step**, not just the final number.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — API Gravity from Density

**Problem:** A marine residual fuel has a density of **985 kg/m³** at 15°C. Calculate its specific gravity and API gravity.

**Step 1 — Reference Density of Water:**
At 15°C, the density of fresh water is $\rho_{water} = 999.1 \;\text{kg/m}^3$ (we'll use the standard 15°C reference from ISO 8217).

$$\text{SG} = \frac{\rho_{\text{fuel}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{985}{999.1}$$

$$\text{SG} = 0.9859 \;\;(\text{dimensionless})$$

**Step 2 — Apply API Formula:**
The API formula requires SG at **60°F (= 15.56°C)**, but for KTU-level problems we use the standard 15°C value directly (note the small temperature drift for exam purposes).

$$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.9859} - 131.5$$

$$\frac{141.5}{0.9859} = 143.528$$

$$\text{API} = 143.528 - 131.5 = 12.03°$$

> **Interpretation:** API ≈ 12° confirms this is a **heavy residual fuel oil** (HFO territory). Typical IFO 380 has API between 10° and 18°.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Dynamic Viscosity from Kinematic

**Problem:** A fuel oil sample has kinematic viscosity $\nu = 380 \;\text{cSt}$ at 50°C and density $\rho = 985 \;\text{kg/m}^3$. Compute the dynamic viscosity in Pa·s and cP.

**Step 1 — Convert cSt to m²/s:**
$$1 \;\text{cSt} = 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\nu = 380 \times 10^{-6} = 3.80 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\mu = \nu \rho$:**
$$\mu = (3.80 \times 10^{-4}) \times 985$$

$$\mu = 0.3743 \;\text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to cP (centipoise):**
$$1 \;\text{Pa·s} = 1000 \;\text{cP}$$

$$\mu = 374.3 \;\text{cP}$$

> **Sanity Check:** Heavy fuel oils at 50°C typically have $\mu \approx 200$–$500$ cP. ✔ Our answer fits.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Net Calorific Value by Empirical Correlation

**Problem:** A marine residual fuel sample has:
- Specific gravity $\text{SG} = 0.985$
- Sulfur content $S\% = 2.5\%$
- Water content $W\% = 0.5\%$

Compute the **Net Specific Energy (NCV)** using the API correlation.

**Step 1 — Write the correlation:**
$$\text{NCV} = 46.423 - 9.155 \cdot \text{SG} - 0.0815 \cdot S\% - 0.259 \cdot W\%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each term:**
$$\text{NCV} = 46.423 - (9.155 \times 0.985) - (0.0815 \times 2.5) - (0.259 \times 0.5)$$

$$\text{NCV} = 46.423 - 9.018 - 0.2038 - 0.1295$$

**Step 3 — Add/subtract sequentially:**
$$46.423 - 9.018 = 37.405$$

$$37.405 - 0.2038 = 37.201$$

$$37.201 - 0.1295 = 37.072$$

$$\boxed{\text{NCV} \approx 37.07 \;\text{MJ/kg}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is slightly below the typical 39–42 MJ/kg HFO range — the high sulfur and water are dragging it down. A purer fuel with $S=0.5\%$, $W=0.1\%$ would yield $\text{NCV} \approx 40.4$ MJ/kg.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — SO₂ Mass Emission Calculation

**Problem:** A 2-stroke main engine consumes **180 g/kWh** of fuel with sulfur content **2.5% m/m** while producing **20,000 kW** brake power. Calculate the **SO₂ mass emission rate** (g/s).

**Step 1 — Fuel Mass Flow Rate:**
$$\dot{m}_f = \text{SFOC} \times P_B = 180 \times 20{,}000 = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g/h}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to g/s:**
$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{6}}{3600} = 1000 \;\text{g/s}$$

**Step 3 — Sulfur Mass Flow:**
Sulfur burns to SO₂. Molar mass: S = 32, SO₂ = 64, ratio = 2.0.

$$\dot{m}_S = \dot{m}_f \times \frac{S\%}{100} = 1000 \times 0.025 = 25 \;\text{g/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Sulfur to SO₂:**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = \dot{m}_S \times 2.0 = 25 \times 2.0 = 50 \;\text{g/s}$$

**Step 5 — Annualize for context:**
$$\text{SO}_2 \;\text{per year} = 50 \times 86400 \times 365 = 1.577 \times 10^{9} \;\text{g} \approx 1577 \;\text{tonnes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is precisely why MARPOL Annex VI imposes sulfur caps. A single VLCC running on 2.5% S HFO can emit **>1500 tonnes of SO₂/year** — equivalent to a small power plant.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption vs. Thermal Efficiency

**Problem:** Engine delivers **$P_B = 15{,}000$ kW** and consumes **$\dot{m}_f = 4.2$ kg/s** of fuel with NCV = **40 MJ/kg**. Compute **SFOC** and **brake thermal efficiency $\eta_B$**.

**Step 1 — Compute Brake Energy Output per Second:**
$$P_B = 15{,}000 \;\text{kW} = 15{,}000 \;\text{kJ/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Fuel Energy Input per Second:**
$$Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{NCV} = 4.2 \times 40{,}000 = 168{,}000 \;\text{kJ/s}$$

**Step 3 — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_B = \frac{P_B}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{15{,}000}{168{,}000} = 0.0893$$

$$\eta_B = 8.93\%$$

> This is unusually low — typical 2-stroke marine engines run at **45–55%**. The numbers don't reflect a real engine; they're a textbook exercise.

**Step 4 — SFOC:**
$$\text{SFOC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_B} = \frac{4.2 \;\text{kg/s}}{15{,}000 \;\text{kW}} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{kg/kWs}$$

Converting to g/kWh:
$$\text{SFOC} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 1008 \;\text{g/kWh}$$

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Matrix — Quality Parameter vs. Pollution Impact

| Parameter | Test Method (ISO) | Limit (HFO RMG 380) | Primary Pollution Effect |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Sulfur | ISO 8754 / 14596 | ≤ 0.5% (Global) | SO₂, acid rain, PM |
| Density @15°C | ISO 12185 | ≤ 991 kg/m³ | Energy density, atomization |
| Kin. Visc @50°C | ISO 3104 | ≤ 380 mm²/s | Smoke, UHC, deposits |
| Flash Point | ISO 2719 | ≥ 60°C | Fire / oil spill risk |
| Pour Point | ISO 3016 | ≤ 30°C (varies) | Cold-flow, fuel starvation |
| Water | ISO 3733 | ≤ 0.5% V/V | Combustion loss, corrosion |
| Ash | ISO 6245 | ≤ 0.15% m/m | Turbocharger deposits |
| CCAl (CCAI) | ISO 8217 Annex | Report | Ignition quality |
| Asphaltenes | IP 143 | ≤ 0.15% (RME 180) | Sludge, incombustibles |
| Total Sediment | ISO 10307 | ≤ 0.10% m/m | Purifier load |

**CCAI** (Calculated Carbon Aromaticity Index) is *the* ignition quality indicator for residual fuels:

$$\text{CCAI} = D - 81 - 141 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left[\log_{10}(\nu + 0.85)\right]$$

where $D$ is density at 15°C (kg/m³) and $\nu$ is viscosity (cSt) at 50°C. Lower CCAI = better ignition quality.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "fuel oil quality" as applied to marine propulsion. List any FIVE key parameters used to evaluate it.**

**Model Answer (≤ 80 words):**

> Fuel oil quality is the combined assessment of the **physical, chemical, and thermal properties** of marine bunker fuel against standardized limits (chiefly **ISO 8217:2017** and **MARPOL Annex VI**) to ensure safe handling, efficient combustion, and environmental compliance.
>
> Five key parameters are:
> (i) **Density** (kg/m³ or API)
> (ii) **Kinematic viscosity** (mm²/s)
> (iii) **Sulfur content** (% m/m)
> (iv) **Flash point** (°C)
> (v) **Net specific energy / calorific value** (MJ/kg).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to *name the standard* (ISO 8217). Always mention it for full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is sulfur content the most regulated parameter in marine fuel oil quality?**

**Model Answer (≤ 80 words):**

> Sulfur combusts to **SO₂**, which oxidizes further to **SO₃** and forms **sulfuric acid mist** — the chief cause of **acid rain** and **respiratory disease** in port cities. Sulfur also deactivates **exhaust catalysts** and forms **sulfate particulate matter (PM₂.₅)**. IMO's **MARPOL Annex VI** caps sulfur at **0.50% m/m globally** and **0.10% m/m in ECAs** to protect human health and the marine atmosphere.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Don't say "sulfur is harmful" — always trace the **chemical chain** SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄ → acid rain. Examiners reward cause-effect chains.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Fuel Properties & Combustion Calculations*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** — Describe the physical and chemical test methods specified under **ISO 8217:2017** for evaluating residual marine fuel oil quality. *(Source pattern: KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 2)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
ISO 8217:2017 is the international standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization** that specifies the requirements for **marine fuels** supplied on a worldwide basis for consumption on board ships. It defines two families: **distillate (DF)** and **residual (RF)**.

**Step 2 — List of Test Methods (Table Format Recommended):**

| Parameter | ISO Test Method | Significance |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Density @ 15°C | **ISO 12185** | Determines mass/volume conversion |
| Kinematic Viscosity | **ISO 3104** (capillary) | Atomization quality |
| Flash Point | **ISO 2719** (Pensky-Martens) | Safety |
| Pour Point | **ISO 3016** | Low-temp flow |
| Sulfur Content | **ISO 8754** (XRF) / **ISO 14596** | SO₂ emissions |
| Water Content | **ISO 3733** (distillation) | Quality |
| Ash | **ISO 6245** | Inorganic residue |
| Carbon Residue | **ISO 10370** (CCR / MCR) | Combustion deposits |
| Asphaltenes | **IP 143** | Sludge tendency |
| CCAI | **Calculated** (Annex F) | Ignition quality |

**Step 3 — Explanation of Key Tests:**

- **ISO 12185 (Density):** Uses an oscillating U-tube densitometer. Sample at 15°C → frequency shift → density reading.
- **ISO 3104 (Viscosity):** Capillary viscometer; measures time for a fixed volume to flow under gravity at controlled temperature.
- **ISO 8754 (Sulfur):** Energy-dispersive **X-ray fluorescence (XRF)**; quick, non-destructive, ideal for bunker delivery analysis.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
These standardized methods ensure that fuel quality is **measured uniformly across the globe**, allowing port state control, charterers, and shipowners to verify bunker quality and pursue disputes via **BQS (Bunker Quality System)**.

*(7 marks — full coverage: introduction + 4+ test methods + brief on 2 tests + conclusion)*

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** — A 2-stroke marine diesel engine produces **$P_B = 25{,}000$ kW** of brake power with a specific fuel oil consumption of **175 g/kWh**. The fuel has a sulfur content of **1.5% m/m**, density **960 kg/m³**, viscosity **180 cSt at 50°C**, and water content **0.3% V/V**. Compute:
**(i)** the mass flow of SO₂ in **g/s**;
**(ii)** the dynamic viscosity of the fuel in **Pa·s** and **cP**;
**(iii)** the specific gravity and API gravity of the fuel.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SO₂ Mass Flow:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \text{SFOC} \times P_B = 175 \times 25{,}000 = 4{,}375{,}000 \;\text{g/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{4{,}375{,}000}{3600} = 1215.28 \;\text{g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_S = 1215.28 \times 0.015 = 18.23 \;\text{g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 18.23 \times 2.0 = \boxed{36.46 \;\text{g/s}}$$

**(ii) Dynamic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = 180 \;\text{cSt} = 180 \times 10^{-6} = 1.80 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = 1.80 \times 10^{-4} \times 960 = 0.1728 \;\text{Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.1728 \;\text{Pa·s} = 172.8 \;\text{cP}}$$

**(iii) Specific Gravity & API:**

$$\text{SG} = \frac{960}{999.1} = 0.9609$$

$$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.9609} - 131.5 = 147.26 - 131.5 = \boxed{15.76°\;\text{API}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert cSt → m²/s (off by factor of $10^{6}$).
> 2. Using wrong $\rho_{\text{water}}$ (must be **999.1 kg/m³ at 15°C**, not 1000).
> 3. Forgetting the **2.0 multiplier** when going from S mass to SO₂ mass.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** — With a neat schematic, explain the **causal chain by which poor fuel oil quality contributes to marine air and water pollution**. Cite **MARPOL Annex VI** sulfur limits.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Poor Fuel Oil Quality:**
A fuel oil is "poor quality" when it exceeds the **ISO 8217** limits on sulfur, viscosity, water, ash, asphaltenes, or has anomalous CCAI/flash/pour values.

**Step 2 — Air Pollution Chain (5 sub-steps):**

1. **High sulfur → SO₂ emission:** Combustion of sulfur produces **SO₂**.
2. **SO₂ → SO₃:** In the presence of oxygen and catalytic metal surfaces, SO₂ oxidizes to **SO₃**.
3. **SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ aerosol:** In the cooler exhaust and atmosphere, sulfuric acid mist forms.
4. **Incomplete combustion (high viscosity) → PM & black carbon:** Large droplets don't vaporize fully.
5. **High asphaltenes → unburnt HC and visible smoke** (opacity violations).

**Step 3 — Water Pollution Chain:**

1. **High water content** → microbial growth in tanks → corrosive by-products → leak risk.
2. **High asphaltenes + asphaltene incompatibility** → **sludge formation** → purifier overflow.
3. **Sludge disposal** can lead to **oily-water pollution** if MARPOL Annex I bilge rules are violated.
4. **Spills during low-quality fuel handling** because of high viscosity requiring extra heating.

**Step 4 — MARPOL Annex VI Limits:**

- **Global cap:** **0.50% S m/m** (since 1 Jan 2020).
- **ECA cap:** **0.10% S m/m** (Baltic, North Sea, N. American, US Caribbean ECAs).
- **NOₓ Tier II/III:** Related to combustion quality, indirectly tied to fuel.

**Step 5 — Mitigation:**
Switch to **ULSFO (Ultra-Low Sulfur Fuel Oil, 0.10% S)**, install **EGCS scrubbers**, or use **LNG / methanol** alternative fuels.

*(7 marks: full chain + numerical limits + one mitigation strategy)*

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** — A ship's fuel oil test report shows: **Density = 985 kg/m³, Sulfur = 0.8% m/m, Water = 0.6% V/V, Viscosity = 220 cSt at 50°C, Flash Point = 78°C, Pour Point = 18°C**.
**Evaluate whether this fuel complies with ISO 8217:2017 RMG 380 grade** and compute its **NCV**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reference Limits for RMG 380 (ISO 8217:2017):**

| Parameter | RMG 380 Limit | Sample Value | Compliant? |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|--------------|
| Density @ 15°C | ≤ 991 kg/m³ | 985 | ✅ Yes |
| Kin. Viscosity @ 50°C | ≤ 380 mm²/s | 220 | ✅ Yes |
| Sulfur | ≤ 0.50% (Global default) | **0.8%** | ❌ **NO** |
| Flash Point | ≥ 60°C | 78 | ✅ Yes |
| Pour Point | ≤ 30°C (seasonal) | 18 | ✅ Yes |
| Water | ≤ 0.5% V/V | **0.6%** | ❌ **NO** |
| Ash | ≤ 0.15% m/m | Not reported | — |
| CCAl | Report | — | — |

**Step 2 — Compliance Verdict:**
The fuel **fails ISO 8217:2017 RMG 380** because:
- **Sulfur (0.8%) > 0.5%** global cap → violates MARPOL Annex VI.
- **Water (0.6%) > 0.5%** → exceeds ISO 8217 limit.

**Step 3 — Compute NCV (Empirical Correlation):**

$$\text{SG} = \frac{985}{999.1} = 0.9859$$

$$\text{NCV} = 46.423 - 9.155(0.9859) - 0.0815(0.8) - 0.259(0.6)$$

$$\text{NCV} = 46.423 - 9.025 - 0.0652 - 0.1554$$

$$\text{NCV} = 37.18 \;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The fuel is **non-compliant for global use**. It could only be used on a vessel equipped with an **approved exhaust gas cleaning system (EGCS / scrubber)** that achieves equivalent SO₂ reduction (per IMO Resolution MEPC.340(77)).

*(7 marks: tabulated comparison + clear verdict + NCV calculation + remediation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> 🛑 **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Missing units** in final answers (always write g/s, MJ/kg, °C, cSt).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention ISO 8217 or MARPOL Annex VI** — even in 3-mark answers.
> 3. **Confusing flash point with auto-ignition temperature** — they are different concepts.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps** in 7-mark derivations — KTU rewards step-wise working.
> 5. **Confusing IFO 380 number** with viscosity at 100°C instead of 50°C.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ **Fuel oil quality** = composite of ~20 ISO 8217:2017 parameters.
- ✅ **MARPOL Annex VI** caps: **0.50% S (global)**, **0.10% S (ECA)**.
- ✅ **ISO 8217:2017** is the *standard bible* — know it by name.
- ✅ **HFO** is residual (IFO 380 / RMG 380); **MDO/MGO** is distillate (DMA, DMB).
- ✅ The **"380"** in IFO 380 means **viscosity ≤ 380 cSt at 50°C**.
- ✅ Key formula: $\mu = \nu \rho$ ; $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5$.
- ✅ Empirical NCV: $\text{NCV} = 46.423 - 9.155\cdot\text{SG} - 0.0815\cdot\text{S}\% - 0.259\cdot\text{W}\%$.
- ✅ Sulfur → SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄ → acid rain & PM pollution.
- ✅ High viscosity → poor atomization → smoke, PM, deposits.
- ✅ Flash point minimum: **60°C**; auto-ignition: **250–400°C** (don't confuse).
- ✅ CCAI calculated from density and viscosity (lower = better ignition).
- ✅ BQS (Bunker Quality System) used to dispute non-compliant deliveries.
- ✅ Engine emissions: CO₂ factor for HFO = **3.114 t CO₂/t fuel** (IMO MEPC.364(79)).
- ✅ SO₂ emission factor: multiply fuel mass flow × S% × **2.0** (for SO₂ mass from S mass).
- ✅ ECA areas: Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean Sea.
- ✅ Future fuels: **LNG, methanol, ammonia, biofuels** — keep them in your answer bank.
- ✅ Pour point < cloud point < flash point (numerical relation, generally).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements for Survey and Issuance of International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements for Survey and Issuance of International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)

The **International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)** is a mandatory statutory document issued to a vessel under **Regulation 5 of MARPOL Annex VI**, which entered into force on **19 May 2005**. It certifies that the ship has been surveyed in accordance with the provisions of the Annex and that its equipment, systems, fittings, arrangements, and materials fully comply with the applicable requirements of MARPOL Annex VI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> The IAPP Certificate is a legal attestation by the flag Administration (or its Recognized Organization / RO on its behalf) that a ship complies with the air pollution prevention regulations contained in MARPOL Annex VI. It must be carried on board at all times and is valid for a maximum period of **5 years** from the date of issue, subject to the satisfactory completion of intermediate, annual, and renewal surveys.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a commercial truck operating across international borders. Before it can be allowed on highways, the transport authority inspects its exhaust system, verifies that it meets emission standards, issues a **Pollution Under Control (PUC) certificate**, and requires the truck to undergo periodic checks (every 6 months or annually) to ensure the exhaust system still functions properly.

The **IAPP Certificate works identically for ships**:
- The **PUC** = the **IAPP Certificate**
- The **emission standards (Bharat Stage VI / Euro VI)** = **MARPOL Annex VI NOx, SOx, and VOC limits**
- The **inspection center** = the **Classification Society acting as Recognized Organization (RO)**
- The **periodic checkups** = **Annual, Intermediate, and Renewal Surveys**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
