- This gives oil-content ratios in recovered fluid of 70–95%, far exceeding weir/suction skimmers.

**Two Limitations:**
1. Sensitive to **wave action**; disc entry/exit causes oil loss at high Hs.
2. **Disc fouling** by debris, tar, or ice reduces oleophilic efficiency and requires frequent cleaning.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $N = 12$, $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $N_{rev} = 25 \text{ rpm}$, $h_{sub} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, $\eta_{film} = 0.80$, $k_{eff} = 0.15$.

Using the disc-skimmer equation derived in Section 3.2:

$$Q_{oil} = \frac{N \cdot \pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot h_{sub} \cdot N_{rev} \cdot \eta_{film}}{120} \times k_{eff}$$

Step-by-step:

$$N \cdot \pi^2 = 12 \times 9.8696 = 118.435$$

$$D^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$$

$$h_{sub} \cdot N_{rev} \cdot \eta_{film} = 0.08 \times 25 \times 0.80 = 1.6$$

Numerator:

$$118.435 \times 0.25 \times 1.6 = 47.374$$

Divide by 120:

$$\frac{47.374}{120} = 0.3948 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Apply correction factor:

$$Q_{oil} = 0.3948 \times 0.15 = 0.0592 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to m³/hr:

$$Q_{oil} = 0.0592 \times 3600 \approx 213.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{Q_{oil} \approx 213 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Many students forget to convert mm to m before plugging into the formula. Marks lost: 1–2 per sub-question.
> 2. **Skipping the correction factor $k_{eff}$** in disc skimmer derivations: without this, the answer overshoots by ~6×, and full marks are denied.
> 3. **Confusing weir with suction skimmer:** Weir uses gravity, suction uses vacuum. Do not interchange their governing equations.
> 4. **Forgetting to state oil viscosity assumption** in numericals: Always write "Assuming $\mu < 1000$ cP (light/medium oil)" before quoting a recovery rate.
> 5. **Wrong classification order:** KTU expects the five types in the order — Weir, Suction, Belt, Brush, Disc. Random order = partial deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Skimming Systems**

- ✅ A **skimmer** is a *recovery* device; it always works *with* a containment boom.
- ✅ **Five major types:** Weir, Suction, Belt/Rope-mop, Brush, Disc/Drum.
- ✅ **Weir & Suction** → gravity/vacuum based → work for *light oil, calm seas*.
- ✅ **Belt, Brush, Disc, Drum** → *oleophilic* based → work for *heavy oil, moderate seas*.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta_{oil} = Q_{oil}/(Q_{oil}+Q_{water}) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Critical oil film thickness:** $t_{min} \approx 1$ mm (below this, skimming is impractical).
- ✅ **Oleophilic materials:** polypropylene, polyethylene; oil contact angle < 30°.
- ✅ **Wave limit:** most skimmers cannot operate at $H_s > 2 \text{ m}$.
- ✅ **Recovery rate equation (weir):** $Q_{oil} = W \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2g(t-t_{crit})}$.
- ✅ **Recovery rate equation (disc):** $Q_{oil} = \frac{N \pi^2 D^2 h_{sub} N_{rev} \eta_{film}}{120} \times k_{eff}$.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** 1 m³ ≈ 6.29 bbl (for unit-conversion MCQs).
- ✅ **Kerala-context:** Cochin Port, Cochin Shipyard, and Indian Coast Guard District HQ-4 (Kochi) are the regional KTU-favourite deployment references.
- ✅ **Syllabus tag:** Topic appears in **Module 3** under *Mechanical Recovery Methods* and links directly to **MARPOL Annex I** regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Storage System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oil Storage System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Oil Storage System** in the marine context refers to the engineered network of tanks, compartments, piping, valves, pumping units, and monitoring instrumentation used to receive, segregate, transfer, store, and dispense petroleum-based fluids (crude oil, heavy fuel oil, marine diesel oil, lubricating oil, sludge, and bilge residues) aboard sea-going vessels and at onshore terminals. The system is governed primarily by the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)** Annex I regulations and the **International Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** Convention Chapter II-2.

The onboard oil storage system encompasses:

- **Cargo oil tanks** (for tankers carrying oil as cargo)
- **Bunker/storage tanks** (fuel oil reserve for ship propulsion)
- **Settling and service tanks** (for fuel treatment)
- **Sludge tanks** (for oil residue collection)
- **Bilge tanks** (for oily water collection)
- **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT)** (mandated to avoid ballast contamination)
- **Double Hull and Double Bottom structures** (mandated by MARPOL after Exxon Valdez, 1989)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Any discussion of marine oil storage systems must be contextualized within the regulatory framework of MARPOL Annex I, particularly **Regulations 13F (double hull)**, **14 (oil filtration)**, and **18 (IOPPC – International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a giant kitchen shelf with carefully labelled containers. Just as you keep rice, lentils, sugar, and spices in **separate airtight containers** to prevent cross-contamination and spoilage, a ship's oil storage system keeps **different grades of oil, water, and waste in segregated compartments**. The "shelf" itself is engineered with double walls — so even if the inner container leaks (a hull breach), the outer wall acts as a **safety catcher**, much like a child's sippy-cup with an outer shell preventing spills from reaching the floor (the ocean).

Another simple analogy: think of oil storage as **a hospital's blood bank**. Blood must be stored in specialized bags at controlled temperatures, segregated by blood group, with strict inventories and overflow backup units. Similarly, marine oil tanks are classified, monitored, and equipped with safety/overflow tanks (overflow or cofferdams).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **API Gravity (American Petroleum Institute):** Measures oil density relative to water.
- **Specific Gravity of typical crude oil:** ranges from **0.85 to 0.95** (lighter than water, hence floats → forms slicks).
- **Flash Point of Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO):** ≥ **60 °C** (MARPOL limit for carriage as fuel).
- **Kinematic Viscosity of HFO at 50 °C:** up to **380 mm²/s** (cSt).
- **Standard Oil Tanker Capacity Units:**
  - **1 DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)** ≈ **1000 kg** of carrying capacity
  - **1 barrel** = **0.159 m³** = **42 US gallons**
- **Standard Settling Tank Retention Time:** minimum **24 hours** for HFO bunker service.
- **Standard Double-Hull Ballast Tank Spacing:** minimum **2 m** (MARPOL Reg. 13F for tankers ≥ 5000 DWT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Pearl:** The relationship between oil density and its behaviour at sea (whether it floats, disperses, or sinks) directly dictates the storage tank engineering — lighter oils need sealed vent systems, heavier oils need heating coils.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A marine oil storage system must perform **six concurrent functions**:

1. **Receiving** – Loading oil from shore terminal via manifold and pipes.
2. **Segregating** – Keeping incompatible grades in separate tanks (cargo segregation; segregated ballast).
3. **Treating** – Settling (gravity separation), heating (to reduce viscosity), centrifuges (purifiers/clarifiers).
4. **Storing** – Long-term retention without contamination or quality loss.
5. **Transferring** – Pumping oil between tanks and to consumers (engines, boilers).
6. **Monitoring** – Level gauging, temperature, pressure, vapour return, leak detection.

### 2.1.1 Tank Types by Functional Role

| Tank Type | Primary Function | Typical Volume Range |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Cargo Tank | Carry crude/products as freight | 10⁴ – 10⁵ m³ (VLCC) |
| Bunker Tank | Reserve fuel for main/aux engines | 100 – 2000 m³ |
| Settling Tank | Gravity separation of water from HFO | 5 – 50 m³ |
| Service Tank | Day-tank feeding engines directly | 2 – 20 m³ |
| Sludge Tank | Collect oil residue from purifiers | 2 – 30 m³ |
| Bilge Tank | Collect oily water from engine room | 1 – 15 m³ |
| Cofferdam | Empty isolating space between tanks | Structural only |

### 2.1.2 Tank Geometry and Structural Logic

Marine tanks are primarily **prismatic** (rectangular cross-section) to maximize hull space utilization. Three classical configurations exist:

- **Integral Tanks** – Use the hull structure itself as walls (lighter, cheaper, but higher pollution risk).
- **Independent Tanks** – Self-supporting tanks with Type A (gravity-only stress analysis, prismatic), Type B (model-tested, e.g., IMO Type B LNG reference), Type C (pressure vessel, cylindrical/spherical).
- **Double-Wall Tanks** – Inner tank + outer hull barrier (double hull).

## 2.2 Pollution Prevention Architecture

The pollution-prevention logic of an oil storage system follows the **MARPOL "Defence-in-Depth"** philosophy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL Defence-in-Depth Layers (in order of priority):**
> 1. **Eliminate** – Use Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT) so cargo/ballast never mix.
> 2. **Reduce** – Crude Oil Washing (COW) to strip residues with the cargo itself.
> 3. **Treat** – Oily Water Separator (OWS) – discharge limit **15 ppm**.
> 4. **Contain** – Sludge tanks, pump-room drainage, spill trays.
> 5. **Recover** – SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan).

## 2.3 Heating & Viscosity Management

Heavy fuel oils are stored at ambient/elevated temperature to maintain pumpability. Heating is achieved via **steam coils** embedded in the tank or external **heat exchangers**.

> The viscosity-pumpability criterion dictates that fuel must reach ≤ **180 mm²/s (cSt)** at the burner tip.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\rho_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + ^\circ API}$ | API to density conversion (at 15 °C) | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $V_{tank} = L \times B \times H \times \eta_{fill}$ | Rectangular tank volume (η_fill = 0.95 typical) | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{cyl} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$ | Cylindrical tank volume | m³ |
| 4 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Discharge rate through orifice (Torricelli-based) | m³/s |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at tank bottom | Pa |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity (μ dynamic, ρ density) | m²/s |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for pipe flow | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{vessel} = \rho g h_{max}$ | Max pressure on tank bottom plate | Pa |
| 9 | $M_{oil} = V_{tank} \cdot \rho_{oil}$ | Oil mass in storage | kg |
| 10 | $T_{settle} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{in}}$ | Hydraulic retention time | s |
| 11 | $P_{MARPOL} \leq 15$ ppm | OWS discharge oil concentration | mg/L |
| 12 | $h_{min,DH} = 2.0$ m | Double hull minimum spacing (tankers ≥ 5000 DWT) | m |
| 13 | $t_{COW} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{crude,jet}}$ | Crude oil washing time per tank | s |
| 14 | $h_{ull,gap} = \dfrac{B}{20}$ | Double bottom height (B = ship breadth) | m |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tanker designers** use these formulas to size double hulls against grounding scenarios.
- **Port authorities** apply tank-volume calculations to plan bunker loading rates.
- **Class societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) verify tank designs using API and hydrostatic formulas.
- **Coastal regulators** enforce MARPOL discharge limits, where the 15 ppm rule is enforced through OWS sizing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rectangular Tank Volume & Fill Efficiency

The fundamental volume of a prismatic (rectangular) oil tank is simply **length × breadth × depth**. However, in marine practice, tanks are never filled to 100 % — an **ullage** (free space) of **5 %** is mandatory to accommodate thermal expansion and prevent overflow.

**Given:**
- Tank internal length, $L = 12$ m
- Internal breadth, $B = 8$ m
- Internal depth, $H = 6$ m
- Fill efficiency, $\eta_{fill} = 0.95$

**To Find:** Effective oil storage volume $V_{tank}$.

**Step 1:** Compute geometric volume of the cuboid.

$$V_{geo} = L \times B \times H$$

$$V_{geo} = 12 \times 8 \times 6 = 576 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Apply fill efficiency to obtain effective storage volume.

$$V_{tank} = V_{geo} \times \eta_{fill}$$

$$V_{tank} = 576 \times 0.95 = 547.2 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Express in barrels for industrial communication.

$$V_{tank, bbl} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{0.159}$$

$$V_{tank, bbl} = \dfrac{547.2}{0.159} \approx 3441.5 \ \text{bbl}$$

**Interpretation:** A modest engine-room bunker tank of 12 m × 8 m × 6 m can hold approximately **547 m³** (≈ 3442 barrels) of HFO at 95 % fill — sufficient to power a mid-sized bulk carrier for several days at sea.

## 3.2 Derivation: API Gravity to Density Conversion

**Given:** Crude oil sample with API gravity = **22 °API** at 15 °C.

**Step 1:** Apply the API definition.

$$\rho_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + ^\circ API} \times \rho_{water}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and compute numerically.

$$\rho_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + 22} \times 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{153.5} \times 1000$$

$$\rho_{oil} \approx 921.8 \ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Compare with water to confirm floating behaviour.

$$\rho_{oil} \approx 921.8 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \lt \rho_{water} = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Conclusion:** This oil floats on seawater — a critical observation because floating oil forms slicks that contaminate shorelines (e.g., the **Prestige spill, Spain 2002**, where heavy fuel spread over 1000 km of coastline precisely because it floated).

## 3.3 Derivation: Settling Tank Retention Time

**Given:**
- Settling tank volume, $V_{tank} = 20$ m³
- Inflow rate from bunker transfer pump, $Q_{in} = 1$ m³/h

**Step 1:** Apply the retention time formula.

$$T_{settle} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and compute.

$$T_{settle} = \dfrac{20}{1} = 20 \ \text{h}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with MARPOL/bunker standard.

$$T_{settle} = 20 \ \text{h} \lt T_{req} = 24 \ \text{h}$$

**Conclusion:** The current settling tank is **undersized**. To meet the 24 h settling criterion, the tank must be enlarged:

$$V_{tank, req} = Q_{in} \times T_{req} = 1 \times 24 = 24 \ \text{m}^3$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Double Hull Compliance Calculation

**Given:** A product tanker of breadth $B = 32$ m.

**Step 1:** Apply the double-bottom height formula.

$$h_{ull, gap} = \dfrac{B}{20}$$

$$h_{ull, gap} = \dfrac{32}{20} = 1.6 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with MARPOL minimum of **2.0 m**.

$$1.6 \ \text{m} \lt 2.0 \ \text{m} \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Non-compliant}$$

**Step 3:** Required widening.

$$\Delta h = 2.0 - 1.6 = 0.4 \ \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel's double-bottom gap is below the MARPOL Annex I Reg. 13F minimum of 2 m for tankers ≥ 5000 DWT; the gap must be increased by 0.4 m to meet regulatory compliance — otherwise, the vessel cannot be issued the **IOPP Certificate**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Storage System Selection

| Criterion | Integral Tank | Independent Type A | Independent Type C | Double Hull |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Highest |
| Pollution Risk | High | Medium | Low | Lowest |
| Volume Efficiency | High (98 %) | Medium | Low (85 %) | Medium |
| Suitable Cargo | Fuel oil only | Chemicals | LNG/LPG | Crude oil |
| Inspection | Difficult | Moderate | Easy | Moderate |
| MARPOL Compliance | Limited | Conditional | Full | Full |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Marine Oil Storage System Architecture

Below is a block-level functional architecture of the marine oil storage system, illustrating all major subsystems and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Shore Terminal Manifold"] --> node2["Ship Manifold and ESD System"]
    node2 --> node3["Cargo Tank Block A"]
    node2 --> node4["Cargo Tank Block B"]
    node2 --> node5["Cargo Tank Block C"]
    node3 --> node6["Inert Gas Generator"]
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7["Vapour Return Line"]
    node7 --> node1
    node3 --> node8["Crude Oil Washing Pump"]
    node4 --> node8
    node5 --> node8
    node8 --> node3
    node8 --> node4
    node8 --> node5
    node9["HFO Bunker Storage Tank"] --> node10["Settling Tank"]
    node10 --> node11["Service Tank"]
    node11 --> node12["Main Engine"]
    node11 --> node13["Aux Boiler"]
    node10 --> node14["Centrifuge Purifier"]
    node14 --> node10
    node14 --> node15["Sludge Tank"]
    node15 --> node16["Incinerator or Shore Discharge"]
    node17["Engine Room Bilge"] --> node18["Oily Water Separator OWS"]
    node18 --> node19["Bilge Holding Tank"]
    node19 --> node20["15 ppm Monitor and Overboard"]
    node19 --> node21["Sludge Tank"]
    node3 --> node22["Segregated Ballast Tank SBT"]
    node4 --> node22
    node22 --> node23["Dedicated Ballast Pump"]
    node23 --> node24["Overboard Discharge"]
    node3 --> node25["Double Hull Space"]
    node4 --> node25
    node5 --> node25
    node25 --> node26["Leak Detection Sensor"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Settling and Service Tank Bunker System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tankA["HFO Storage Tank"] -- steam coil heating --> tankA
    tankA --> pump1["Transfer Pump"]
    pump1 --> tankB["Settling Tank 20 m3"]
    tankB --> pump2["Booster Pump"]
    pump2 --> tankC["Service Tank 10 m3"]
    tankC --> eng1["Main Engine"]
    tankC --> eng2["Aux Diesel Generator"]
    tankC --> eng3["Aux Boiler"]
    tankB --> cent1["Purifier Centrifuge"]
    cent1 --> tankB
    cent1 --> tankD["Sludge Tank"]
    tankD --> incin["Incinerator"]
    tankC --> drain["Daily Service Drain"]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Double Hull vs Single Hull Compliance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Vessel Size Greater Equal 5000 DWT"] --> step2{"Existing Hull Type?"}
    step2 -- Single Hull --> step3["Phase Out by 2010 MARPOL Reg 13F"]
    step2 -- Double Side Only --> step4["Convert to Full Double Hull"]
    step2 -- Double Hull --> step5["Issue IOPP Certificate"]
    step4 --> step6["Verify h gap Greater Equal 2 m"]
    step6 -- Yes --> step5
    step6 -- No --> step7["Modify Structure or Retrofitt"]
    step7 --> step6
    step3 --> step8["Replace with New Double Hull Tanker"]
```

## 4.4 Subsystem Interaction Matrix

| Subsystem | Inputs | Outputs | Monitoring |
|-----------|--------|---------|------------|
| Cargo Tank | Crude via manifold | Crude to COW/pump | Level gauge, temperature, pressure |
| Inert Gas | Boiler exhaust | IG to tankers | O₂ analyser (< 8 %) |
| Settling Tank | HFO from bunker | HFO to service | Sample valve, thermometer |
| Centrifuge | Heated HFO | Clean HFO + sludge | Vibration, throughput |
| Sludge Tank | Centrifuge waste | Incineration | Level sensor |
| Bilge Holding | OWS reject | Re-process/shore | Oil content meter |
| SBT | Seawater via dedicated pump | Seawater overboard | Level gauge |
| Double Hull | None (passive) | None (containment) | Leak sensor, void inspection |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: Define Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT). Why is it mandated under MARPOL Annex I?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT)** is a dedicated tank, completely separated from the cargo oil system, used exclusively for ballast water. Its piping and pumping arrangements are independent of cargo oil lines.

**Reasons for Mandate (MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 14):**

1. Eliminates the historical practice of using cargo tanks for ballast, which produced oily ballast discharge and massive pollution during deballasting.
2. Ensures that ballast water pumped overboard never contains oil.
3. Mandatory for **new crude oil tankers of 20,000 DWT and above** and **new product carriers of 30,000 DWT and above**, delivered after 1 June 1982.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**Q: List any three types of oil tanks found in a marine engine room and state their function.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **HFO Bunker Storage Tank** – Stores heavy fuel oil received from shore; volume typically 100–2000 m³ depending on ship size and voyage duration.
2. **Settling Tank** – Allows water and sediment to separate from HFO via gravity; minimum retention time **24 hours**.
3. **Service Tank (Day Tank)** – Directly feeds the main engine and boilers; smaller volume (2–20 m³) with quick replenishment from settling tank.
4. **Sludge Tank** – Collects oil residue from purifiers and OWS reject; periodically discharged ashore or incinerated onboard.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q: With neat sketches, explain the different types of oil storage tanks used on board ships. Discuss the MARPOL requirements for segregated ballast tanks and double hull design.**

#### (a) Classify and explain the oil storage tanks used on board ships. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Oil storage tanks on board ships are classified as follows:

**1. By Location and Function:**

- **Bunker Tanks** – Large storage tanks for HFO/MDO used as fuel.
- **Settling Tanks** – Gravity separation of water and sediments.
- **Service Tanks** – Day tanks supplying engines directly.
- **Sludge Tanks** – Residue from purifier and OWS reject.
- **Bilge Tanks** – Collection of oily water from engine room.
- **Cargo Tanks** – Hold oil as cargo (tankers only).
- **Cofferdams** – Empty isolating spaces (between cargo bunker tanks).

**2. By Structural Type:**

- **Integral Tanks** – Hull structure forms the tank walls (lightweight, but pollution-prone).
- **Independent Tanks** – Self-supporting tanks, subdivided into **Type A** (prismatic, gravity analysis), **Type B** (prismatic, model-tested), and **Type C** (cylindrical pressure vessels).

**3. By Construction Material:**

- Mild steel with epoxy coating (HFO tanks).
- Stainless steel (chemical/product tankers).
- Double-hull steel with cathodic protection.

#### (b) Explain MARPOL Annex I requirements for SBT and double hull. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT) Requirements:**

- Applicable to **new crude carriers ≥ 20,000 DWT** and **new product carriers ≥ 30,000 DWT** (delivered after 1 June 1982).
- SBT capacity must be sufficient to meet ballast and draft requirements throughout the voyage.
- SBT piping must be **permanently separated** from cargo oil lines.
- Dedicated ballast pumps must be installed.
- No cargo oil contamination is permitted during ballast operations.

**Double Hull Requirements:**

- Under **MARPOL Annex I Reg. 13F**, all tankers of **5000 DWT and above**, delivered after 6 July 1996, must have **double hull** (double sides + double bottom).
- Minimum wing tank width = **2 m** at any cross-section.
- Minimum double-bottom height = **B/20** or **2 m**, whichever is less (for B = ship breadth).
- Existing single-hull tankers were phased out progressively, with the final phase-out date being **2010** for pre-MARPOL tankers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **Reg. 13F (double hull)** date (6 July 1996) with the **Reg. 14 (SBT)** date (1 June 1982). Memorize both distinctly. Also, do not interchange **double side** and **double bottom** dimensions — both must be verified separately.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**Q: Describe the oil transfer, settling, and treatment system on board a typical merchant vessel. Explain the role of OWS (Oily Water Separator) in maintaining MARPOL compliance.**

#### (a) Describe the oil transfer, settling, and treatment system on board. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The oil transfer, settling, and treatment system follows a sequential flow:

**Stage 1 — Transfer (from shore to bunker tank):**
- Oil is received at the **ship manifold** with **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** system.
- Transferred via shore supply pumps through bunker pipelines.
- Recorded by **flow meters** and **level gauges** in the bunker storage tank.

**Stage 2 — Settling (Bunker → Settling Tank):**
- Settling tank has **steam heating coils** to maintain HFO at 60–80 °C.
- Gravity separation removes **water and sediments** (longer settling = better separation).
- Retention time ideally **24 hours** minimum.

**Stage 3 — Treatment (Centrifugation):**
- **Purifier centrifuge** removes remaining water and solid impurities.
- **Clarifier** further polishes the oil before service tank entry.
- Sludge is discharged to **sludge tank**.

**Stage 4 — Storage and Use (Service Tank):**
- Day-tank feeds the **main engine** and **auxiliary boilers**.
- Continuous monitoring by **viscosity controllers** (target ≤ 180 cSt at burner).

#### (b) Explain the OWS role in MARPOL compliance. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** treats bilge water generated in the engine room before discharge overboard. Its role in MARPOL compliance:

**Operating Principle:**
- Uses **gravity separation** + **coalescence plates** + **filter** to remove oil globules ≥ 15 µm.
- Effluent passes through an **oil content meter (15 ppm monitor)**.

**MARPOL Discharge Limits (Annex I, Reg. 15 & 16):**

- Oil concentration in discharged bilge water ≤ **15 ppm** (parts per million).
- Discharge permitted only when the ship is **en route** (not in port) and the **OWS is operating**.
- The **15 ppm monitor** must be operational; otherwise, overboard discharge is automatically blocked.

**Integration with Bilge System:**

- Engine room bilge → **bilge holding tank** → **OWS** → overboard (≤ 15 ppm).
- OWS reject (high oil content) → **sludge tank** for disposal ashore.

**Continuous Compliance:**

- IOPP Certificate (Reg. 18) is issued only after OWS is inspected and approved by the Class Surveyor.
- Port State Control officers can detain ships if OWS is bypassed or tampered with.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** A common error is to state that OWS discharge is allowed "anywhere at sea." This is incorrect — it is **only permitted when the ship is proceeding en route**, not when stationary or in a special area (e.g., Mediterranean, Baltic, Antarctic under Reg. 10).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Oil storage systems on ships include **cargo tanks, bunker tanks, settling/service tanks, sludge tanks, SBT, and double hull voids**.
- **MARPOL Annex I** is the governing regulation: **Reg. 13F** (double hull), **Reg. 14** (SBT), **Reg. 15/16** (OWS ≤ 15 ppm), **Reg. 18** (IOPP Certificate).
- **API gravity formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + ^\circ API} \times 1000$ kg/m³.
- **Tank volume (rectangular):** $V = L \times B \times H \times \eta_{fill}$ with $\eta_{fill} = 0.95$.
- **Settling tank retention time:** minimum **24 hours** for HFO.
- **Double hull minimum spacing:** **2.0 m** for tankers ≥ 5000 DWT (MARPOL Reg. 13F).
- **Discharge limit of OWS:** **15 ppm** (mg/L).
- **Flash point of HFO for carriage:** **≥ 60 °C**.
- **Kinematic viscosity of HFO at 50 °C:** up to **380 cSt**.
- **Critical dates to memorize:**
  - **1 June 1982** → SBT enforcement for new tankers.
  - **6 July 1996** → Double hull enforcement for tankers ≥ 5000 DWT.
  - **2010** → Phase-out of all pre-MARPOL single-hull tankers.
- **Floating behaviour:** Oils with API > 10 float; oils with API < 10 may sink (rare — e.g., Orimulsion spills).
- **Defence-in-Depth principle:** Eliminate → Reduce → Treat → Contain → Recover.
- **Crude Oil Washing (COW):** Uses crude itself to strip residues, reducing tank-cleaning discharges.
- **Segregated ballast:** Independent piping, dedicated pumps, no contamination risk.
- **Cofferdams:** Structural isolation between hot tanks (bunker) and adjacent compartments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always link tank design to its **pollution-prevention function**. KTU questions often test *why* a particular tank type is mandated, not just *what* it is.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treatment of Pollutant After Recovery
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# TREATMENT OF POLLUTANT AFTER RECOVERY

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Treatment of Pollutant After Recovery

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Treatment of Pollutants After Recovery** refers to the systematic sequence of physical, chemical, biological, and thermal engineering operations applied to marine pollutants that have been mechanically recovered from the marine environment (ocean surface, water column, or seabed) so as to render them harmless, recyclable, or suitable for safe final disposal in compliance with **MARPOL 73/78**, **IMO Conventions**, and the **National Oil Spill Contingency Plan (NOSCP)** of India.

The recovered pollutant is rarely in a usable form directly. For example:
- Recovered oil-water emulsions (often called "chocolate mousse" from sea spills) may contain **30–60% water**.
- Recovered oily debris (shoreline waste) carries sand, biological matter, and salt.
- Recovered sediments from dredging carry heavy metals and hydrocarbons.

Hence, **post-recovery treatment** is the *value-addition* phase that converts a hazardous waste stream into segregated clean streams (oil, water, solids).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Recovery Room**
> Think of pollutant recovery as a rescue operation where an injured patient (oil slick) is pulled out of the sea. The patient arrives at the hospital (treatment facility) covered in mud, mixed with debris, and unstable. The doctors (treatment processes) must first stabilize the patient (physical separation), remove toxins (chemical treatment), rebuild healthy tissue (biological treatment), and finally decide whether the patient can go home (reuse) or needs to be sent to a specialized ward (secure disposal). Skipping any stage leads to relapse — that is, secondary pollution or environmental damage on land.

### 1.3 Why Post-Recovery Treatment is Critical

| Stage | Recovered State | Environmental Risk if Untreated |
|---|---|---|
| Surface oil recovery | Oil + water emulsion | Re-release of free oil during storage |
| Shoreline cleanup | Sand + oil mixture | Leachate contaminating groundwater |
| Ballast water | Bilge + hydrocarbons | Toxic discharge into port waters |
| Dredged sediment | Heavy metals + organics | Bioaccumulation in food chain |

The **ultimate goal** is to achieve the **3R hierarchy**:
- **Recover** (oil/water/solids)
- **Recycle** (refined oil back to refinery feedstock)
- **Reuse** (treated water for industrial cooling, solids for road base)

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard treatment efficiency metrics in KTU/IMO evaluations:
> - **Oil-Water Separation Efficiency (η):** $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$
> - **Target Effluent Quality:** Oil content in discharged water $\le$ **15 ppm** (MARPOL Annex I standard for oily bilge water).
> - **Heavy Metal Residue:** $\le$ **2 mg/L** for landfill leachate.
> - **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD$_5$):** $\le$ **30 mg/L** for coastal discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Recovered Marine Pollutants

The recovered material is broadly classified into three phases:

1. **Liquid phase** — oil-water emulsions, bilge water, contaminated ballast water.
2. **Solid phase** — oiled sand, debris, sorbent pads, plastics, sediments.
3. **Sludge phase** — semi-solid mixtures (oil + water + solids), commonly called "tank bottoms" or "slop."

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Treatment Stages

```
Stage 1 (Primary)   → Physical Treatment (gravity, centrifugal, filtration)
Stage 2 (Secondary) → Chemical Treatment (emulsion breaking, oxidation, adsorption)
Stage 3 (Tertiary)  → Biological Treatment (biodegradation, phytoremediation)
Stage 4 (Residual)  → Thermal/Solidification Treatment (incineration, stabilization)
```

### 2.3 Primary Treatment — Physical Methods

#### 2.3.1 Gravity Separation (API Separator)
- Relies on **Stokes' Law** for differential settling of oil droplets in water.
- Used as the **first stage** in oil recovery facilities.
- Effective for droplets $> 150 \mu m$.
- Residence time: **2 to 4 hours** in a properly designed separator.

#### 2.3.2 Centrifugal Separation
- Uses **hydrocyclones** and disc-stack centrifuges.
- Driven by the difference in **specific gravity** of oil (typically 0.85–0.95) and seawater (1.025).
- High G-force ($>$ 2000 g) enables separation of emulsified droplets down to **5–10 $\mu m$**.

#### 2.3.3 Filtration
- **Media filters** (sand, anthracite, walnut shell) for solid removal.
- **Membrane filters** (microfiltration, ultrafiltration) for fine emulsions.
- Used as a **polishing step** after gravity/centrifugal separation.

### 2.4 Secondary Treatment — Chemical Methods

#### 2.4.1 Demulsification (Chemical Emulsion Breaking)
- Demulsifiers are surfactants that destabilize the oil-water interface.
- Common demulsifier families: **polyethylene glycol esters**, **sulfonates**, **polyamines**.
- Dosage: typically **50–500 ppm** depending on emulsion stability.

#### 2.4.2 Chemical Oxidation
- Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) like **ozonation (O$_3$)**, **Fenton reaction (Fe$^{2+}$/H$_2$O$_2$)**, and **UV/H$_2$O$_2$**.
- Breaks down persistent organic pollutants (POPs) such as PAHs.

#### 2.4.3 Adsorption
- **Activated carbon** is the benchmark adsorbent for hydrocarbons and dissolved organics.
- Operating principle: surface area ($>$ **1000 m²/g**) captures pollutant molecules via van der Waals forces.

### 2.5 Tertiary Treatment — Biological Methods

#### 2.5.1 Bioremediation
- Uses **hydrocarbon-degrading microbes** (*Alcanivorax borkumensis*, *Pseudomonas putida*, *Rhodococcus*).
- Nutrients (N, P) are added as fertilizers (e.g., **Inipol EAP-22**) to accelerate microbial metabolism.
- Suitable for low-concentration residuals (**10–1000 ppm**).

#### 2.5.2 Biostimulation vs. Bioaugmentation
- **Biostimulation:** Adding nutrients (N, P) to indigenous microbes.
- **Bioaugmentation:** Introducing exogenous specialized microbial consortia.

### 2.6 Residual Treatment — Thermal / Solidification

#### 2.6.1 Incineration
- High-temperature oxidation ($>$ **1200 °C**) destroys hydrocarbons completely.
- Energy recovery through waste heat boilers.

#### 2.6.2 Solidification / Stabilization (S/S)
- Binds pollutants in a cement or polymer matrix.
- Used for **oiled sediments** and **heavy-metal-contaminated sludges**.
- Leachate compliance tested via **TCLP (Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure)**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU board examinations on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stokes' settling velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{g \, d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \, \mu}$ | API separator design |
| 2 | Separation efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Generic treatment evaluation |
| 3 | Centrifugal acceleration | $a_c = \omega^2 r = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | Hydrocyclone performance |
| 4 | First-order biodegradation | $C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | Bioremediation kinetics |
| 5 | Adsorption capacity (Langmuir) | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}$ | Activated carbon design |
| 6 | Fenton reaction rate | $r = k \cdot [\text{Fe}^{2+}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]$ | AOP reactor sizing |
| 7 | Mass balance on separator | $Q_{in} C_{in} = Q_{out} C_{out} + Q_{sludge} C_{sludge}$ | Continuous flow design |
| 8 | Power for centrifuge | $P = \dot{m} \, \Delta(\omega^2 r^2)/2$ | Energy estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, students are often asked to derive or apply **Stokes' Law** to justify oil-water separation time and hydrocyclone cut-size. Master the boundary conditions: laminar flow ($Re < 1$), droplet size range, and density contrast.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore platforms** use hydrocyclones for produced-water treatment (re-injection or overboard discharge).
- **Ports and shipyards** use API + CPI (Corrugated Plate Interceptor) separators for oily bilge water.
- **Spill response vessels** carry **centrifuges and coalescers** for on-board treatment of recovered oil.
- **Refineries** treat recovered marine oil as a feedstock, blending it with crude after desalination.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Law Applied to Oil Droplet Settling

When an oil droplet of diameter $d$ settles under gravity through seawater, three forces act on it:

1. **Gravitational force** (downward): $F_g = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \, \rho_o \, g$
2. **Buoyant force** (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \, \rho_w \, g$
3. **Drag force** (opposing motion): $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$ (Stokes' drag for laminar flow, $Re < 1$)

At terminal velocity, the net force is zero:

$$
F_g - F_b - F_D = 0
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \, \rho_o \, g - \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \, \rho_w \, g - 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s = 0
$$

Simplifying — factor out the common $\pi d$:

$$
\dfrac{\pi d^2}{6} (\rho_w - \rho_o) g = 3 \pi \mu \, v_s
$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$
v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o) g}{18 \, \mu}
$$

This is the **fundamental Stokes settling velocity** used to size API separators, lamella plates, and gravity tanks.

**Practical numerical example (KTU-style):**

Given:
- Oil droplet diameter $d = 150 \, \mu m = 150 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Seawater density $\rho_w = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Oil density $\rho_o = 880 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Dynamic viscosity of seawater $\mu = 1.05 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$
- Gravity $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

$$
v_s = \dfrac{(150 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (1025 - 880) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(2.25 \times 10^{-8}) \times 145 \times 9.81 = 3.20 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Denominator:

$$
18 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 1.89 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_s = \dfrac{3.20 \times 10^{-5}}{1.89 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 1.69 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}
$$

This corresponds to **about 6.1 m/hour** — justifying the typical 2–4 hour residence time in API separators for full separation of $150 \mu m$ droplets.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: First-Order Biodegradation Kinetics

In bioremediation, the disappearance of a hydrocarbon (e.g., naphthalene) is often modeled as a first-order decay:

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k \, C
$$

Integrating from $C_0$ at $t=0$ to $C(t)$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) = -k t
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-k t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **half-life** $t_{1/2}$ is a common KTU sub-question: $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$
>
> For marine diesel, typical $k = 0.05 \, \text{day}^{-1}$ at 25 °C, giving $t_{1/2} \approx 14$ days. This is why oily shoreline recovery must continue for **weeks to months**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Separation Efficiency of an API Separator

Consider a single-pass separator with inlet oil concentration $C_{in}$ and outlet $C_{out}$. A fraction $f$ of the oil is skimmed off as recovered oil. The mass balance on oil:

$$
Q_{in} \, C_{in} = Q_{out} \, C_{out} + Q_{recovered} \, C_{recovered}
$$

For a separator where $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$ (no sludge) and $C_{recovered}$ is taken as pure oil ($C_{recovered} \approx \rho_o$ per unit volume), the efficiency $\eta$ on water side is:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often expect students to state this as the **definition** of efficiency, then apply it numerically.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Treatment Technology

| Pollutant State | Best Primary Method | Best Secondary Method | Best Tertiary Method | Disposal Route |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free oil on water (slick) | Booms + skimmers | Centrifuge | Activated carbon | Refinery feedstock |
| Emulsified oil (mousse) | Heating + demulsifier | Centrifuge | AOP (Fenton) | Refinery or incineration |
| Oiled sand/debris | Sand washing | Solvent extraction | Bioremediation | Road base / landfill |
| Oily bilge water | API separator | Coalescer + filter | Membrane UF | Overboard discharge (if $\le$ 15 ppm) |
| Dredged oily sediment | Dewatering | S/S with cement | Phytoremediation | Confined disposal facility |
| Recovered plastic debris | Sorting + washing | Shredding + washing | Pyrolysis | Recycling into pellets |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A recovered oil-water emulsion contains **5% w/w oil** (oil density = 900 kg/m³). The treatment train is: API separator → hydrocyclone → activated carbon. The API separator removes 80% of oil, the hydrocyclone removes 90% of remaining oil, and the carbon polisher removes 95% of residual. Calculate the **final effluent oil concentration** and check compliance with the **MARPOL 15 ppm** standard.

**Step 1 — Initial concentration:**
$$
C_{in} = 5\% \times \rho_w \approx 50{,}000 \, \text{ppm (assumed 1 L of emulsion} \approx 1 \text{ kg)}
$$

**Step 2 — After API separator (80% removal):**

$$
C_1 = C_{in} \times (1 - 0.80) = 50{,}000 \times 0.20 = 10{,}000 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 3 — After hydrocyclone (90% removal):**

$$
C_2 = C_1 \times (1 - 0.90) = 10{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}000 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 4 — After carbon adsorption (95% removal):**

$$
C_{out} = C_2 \times (1 - 0.95) = 1{,}000 \times 0.05 = 50 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check:**

$$
C_{out} = 50 \, \text{ppm} > 15 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Result: NON-COMPLIANT.** Additional treatment (membrane UF or AOP) is required.

> [!NOTE]
> This type of **stage-wise efficiency problem** is a **classic KTU 14-mark Part B question**. Always show step-by-step multiplication with intermediate concentrations clearly labeled.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Post-Recovery Treatment Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Recovered Pollutant Stream]:::raw --> B{Phase Sorting}
    B -->|Liquid Phase| C[Liquid Phase Treatment Train]
    B -->|Solid Phase| D[Solid Phase Treatment Train]
    B -->|Sludge Phase| E[Sludge Phase Treatment Train]
    C --> C1[Stage1 Primary Physical]
    C1 --> C2[Stage2 Secondary Chemical]
    C2 --> C3[Stage3 Tertiary Biological]
    C3 --> C4{Discharge Standard Met?}
    C4 -->|Yes| C5[Overboard Discharge 15ppm]
    C4 -->|No| C6[Return to Stage2 Loop]
    D --> D1[Sand Washing]
    D1 --> D2[Solvent Extraction]
    D2 --> D3[Bioremediation Pile]
    D3 --> D4{Leachate Test}
    D4 -->|Pass| D5[Reuse as Road Sub-base]
    D4 -->|Fail| D6[Confined Landfill]
    E --> E1[Centrifuge Dewatering]
    E1 --> E2[Thermal Desorption]
    E2 --> E3[Stabilization or Incineration]
    classDef raw fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef raw fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — API Separator System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET[Inlet Bay]
        A1[Inlet Weir]
    end
    subgraph SEP[Separation Zone]
        A2[API Separator Tank]
        A3[Corrugated Plate Packs]
    end
    subgraph OUTLET[Outlet Bay]
        A4[Effluent Weir]
    end
    subgraph SKIM[Oil Recovery Line]
        A5[Skimmer Pipe]
        A6[Oil Collection Sump]
    end
    subgraph SLUDGE[Sludge Line]
        A7[Bottom Scraper]
        A8[Sludge Hopper]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A2 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A2 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
```

### 4.3 Treatment Decision Logic — Hydrocyclone vs. Centrifuge vs. Gravity Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Selection Logic]) --> Q1{Droplet Size Known?}
    Q1 -->|No > 150 um| Q2[Use API Gravity Tank]
    Q1 -->|Yes 5 to 150 um| Q3{Throughput Required?}
    Q3 -->|< 50 m3 per hr| Q4[Use Hydrocyclone]
    Q3 -->|> 50 m3 per hr| Q5{Emulsion Stable?}
    Q5 -->|No| Q6[Use Disc Stack Centrifuge]
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q7[Add Demulsifier First]
    Q7 --> Q6
    Q2 --> STOP([Final Disposal])
    Q4 --> STOP
    Q6 --> STOP
```

### 4.4 Process Sequence — Oily Sediment (Solid Phase) Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Oiled Sand from Shoreline] --> P2[Screening and Pre Wash]
    P2 --> P3[Hot Water Sand Washing]
    P3 --> P4[Settling Tank]
    P4 --> P5{Oil Concentration Check}
    P5 -->|> 1000 ppm| P6[Solvent Extraction with Hexane]
    P6 --> P7[Distillation to Recover Oil]
    P5 -->|< 1000 ppm| P8[Landfarming or Biopile]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[TCLP Leachate Test]
    P9 -->|Pass| P10[Reuse for Civil Construction]
    P9 -->|Fail| P11[Stabilization with Cement]
    P11 --> P12[Confined Disposal Facility]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define the term "demulsification" in the context of marine pollutant treatment. List any two common chemical demulsifiers used for breaking oil-water emulsions.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Demulsification** is the chemical process of destabilizing and breaking an oil-water emulsion (e.g., "chocolate mousse" formed during oil spills) into separate oil and water phases, allowing their recovery and individual treatment.

Mechanism: Demulsifiers are surfactant molecules that displace the natural surfactants (asphaltenes, resins) stabilizing the emulsion film at the oil-water interface, causing droplet coalescence.

Two common demulsifiers:
1. **Polyethylene glycol esters** — non-ionic surfactants effective on water-in-oil emulsions.
2. **Polyamine-based demulsifiers** — cationic surfactants for tight emulsions.
3. (Bonus) **Sulfonate-based blends** — used in refinery desalting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State Stokes' Law for an oil droplet settling in water. What are the limitations of this law when applied to API separator design?*

**Model Answer:**

Stokes' settling velocity:

$$
v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o) g}{18 \, \mu}
$$

Limitations:
1. Valid only for **laminar flow** ($Re < 1$) — fails for large droplets in turbulent flow.
2. Assumes **rigid, spherical droplets** — invalid for irregular oil globules or those with internal circulation.
3. **Density contrast must be sufficient** — fails for very stable emulsions with $\rho_o \approx \rho_w$.
4. **Viscosity assumed constant** — actual seawater viscosity varies with temperature and salinity.
5. **No account of droplet-droplet interaction** — assumes dilute dispersion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *KTU University Exam — June 2024*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **treatment train for recovered oil-water emulsion** from a marine oil spill. Identify the function of each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An oil spill response team recovers **10,000 L of oil-water emulsion** containing **8% by volume of oil**. The emulsion is passed through a three-stage treatment system:
- Stage 1 (API separator): **75% oil removal**
- Stage 2 (hydrocyclone): **85% oil removal**
- Stage 3 (carbon adsorption): **90% oil removal**

Calculate the **final oil concentration (ppm)** and verify if the discharge complies with the **MARPOL 15 ppm** limit. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners award full marks only when the student provides: (i) a labelled diagram, (ii) the role of each unit, and (iii) sequential reasoning.

**Treatment Train for Recovered Oil-Water Emulsion:**

| Stage | Unit | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Storage / Settling Tank** | Allows gross separation; removes sand and free water. |
| 2 | **Pre-heater** | Heats emulsion to 50–60 °C to reduce viscosity and break emulsion. |
| 3 | **Demulsifier Dosing Unit** | Adds chemical demulsifier (50–500 ppm) to destabilize the interface. |
| 4 | **API Separator** | Gravity-based bulk oil-water separation using Stokes' principle. |
| 5 | **Hydrocyclone** | Centrifugal separation for fine emulsified droplets (5–150 µm). |
| 6 | **Activated Carbon Adsorber** | Polishes water by adsorbing dissolved hydrocarbons. |
| 7 | **Effluent Monitoring** | In-line oil-in-water analyzer checks compliance with **MARPOL 15 ppm**. |
| 8 | **Recovered Oil Storage** | Skimmed oil sent to refinery as feedstock. |

**Sequential logic:** Each stage progressively removes oil of decreasing droplet size, ensuring the final discharge is environmentally compliant.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Volume = 10,000 L
- Initial oil concentration = 8% v/v = **80,000 ppm** (1 L ≈ 10$^6$ mg, so 80,000 mg/L = 80,000 ppm).

**Step 1 — After API separator (75% removal):**

$$
C_1 = 80{,}000 \times (1 - 0.75) = 20{,}000 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 2 — After hydrocyclone (85% removal):**

$$
C_2 = 20{,}000 \times (1 - 0.85) = 3{,}000 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 3 — After carbon adsorber (90% removal):**

$$
C_{out} = 3{,}000 \times (1 - 0.90) = 300 \, \text{ppm}
$$

**Compliance check:**

$$
C_{out} = 300 \, \text{ppm} \gg 15 \, \text{ppm (MARPOL limit)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER VALUATION PITFALL — Common Mistake #1:**
> Students often forget to convert **percent to ppm** at the start. Remember: **1% = 10,000 ppm**. So 8% = 80,000 ppm, NOT 8 ppm. Marks are lost here in 30% of answer scripts.
>
> **Common Mistake #2:**
> Students multiply efficiencies instead of using the **(1 − η)** factor. Always subtract from 1 for residual concentration.

**Result:** The effluent is **NON-COMPLIANT** with MARPOL. A **fourth stage** (membrane ultrafiltration or advanced oxidation) is needed to bring the residual below 15 ppm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *KTU University Exam — Dec 2023*

**(a)** Explain the principle, application, and limitations of **bioremediation** for the treatment of oil-contaminated sediments recovered from shoreline cleanup operations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive **Stokes' Law** for an oil droplet settling in seawater. A spherical oil droplet of diameter **200 µm** settles in seawater (density 1025 kg/m³, viscosity $1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). Oil density is **870 kg/m³**. Calculate the **terminal settling velocity**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Principle:**
Bioremediation uses **hydrocarbon-degrading microorganisms** (bacteria, fungi) to metabolize petroleum hydrocarbons into CO$_2$, H$_2$O, and biomass. Indigenous marine microbes such as *Alcanivorax borkumensis* and *Pseudomonas putida* naturally degrade n-alkanes and PAHs.

**Two approaches:**
- **Biostimulation** — adding nutrients (N, P fertilizers) to enhance indigenous microbial activity.
- **Bioaugmentation** — introducing exogenous specialized microbial consortia.

**Application in shoreline cleanup:**
- Recovered oiled sand is placed in **biopiles** or **landfarming** units.
- Nutrients (e.g., **Inipol EAP-22**, a urea-based oleophilic fertilizer) are added.
- Aeration via tilling promotes aerobic degradation.
- Typical degradation rate: **0.05–0.5 day$^{-1}$** depending on oil type and temperature.

**Limitations:**
1. **Slow kinetics** — weeks to months; not suitable for emergency response.
2. **Weather-dependent** — cold climates reduce microbial activity.
3. **Limited to biodegradable compounds** — resistant tars and asphaltenes remain.
4. **Requires monitoring** — nutrient levels, pH, oxygen, toxicity must be controlled.
5. **Cannot handle high concentrations** ($>$ 50,000 ppm) — requires pre-treatment.
6. **Potential for incomplete degradation** producing more toxic intermediates.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Derivation** (as presented in Section 3.1):

Force balance at terminal velocity: $F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$

$$
\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_o g - \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_w g - 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o) g}{18 \mu}
$$

**Numerical calculation:**

Given:
- $d = 200 \, \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\rho_w = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_o = 870 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.05 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute $d^2$:**

$$
d^2 = (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute density difference:**

$$
\rho_w - \rho_o = 1025 - 870 = 155 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o) g = (4 \times 10^{-8}) \times 155 \times 9.81 = 6.08 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
18 \, \mu = 18 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 1.89 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 5 — Terminal velocity:**

$$
v_s = \dfrac{6.08 \times 10^{-5}}{1.89 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Final answer:**

$$
v_s \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s} \approx 11.6 \, \text{m/hr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER VALUATION PITFALL — Common Mistake #1:**
> Writing $\rho_w - \rho_o$ as **negative** if oil density is mistakenly used first. Always subtract the lighter fluid (oil) from the heavier fluid (water).
>
> **Common Mistake #2:**
> Forgetting to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ in the diameter. Remember: $1 \mu m = 10^{-6}$ m. Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Treatment of Pollutants After Recovery**

- [x] **Definition:** Treatment refers to all post-recovery operations (physical, chemical, biological, thermal) to render recovered marine pollutants safe or reusable.
- [x] **3R Principle:** **Recover → Recycle → Reuse** is the governing philosophy.
- [x] **MARPOL Standard:** Oily water discharge must contain $\le$ **15 ppm** oil.
- [x] **Primary treatment** uses **gravity, centrifugal, and filtration** methods (Stokes' Law).
- [x] **Secondary treatment** uses **demulsifiers, oxidants (Fenton, O$_3$), and adsorption (activated carbon)**.
- [x] **Tertiary treatment** uses **bioremediation** (biostimulation, bioaugmentation) for residual hydrocarbons.
- [x] **Residual treatment** uses **incineration** ($>$ 1200 °C) or **solidification/stabilization** (S/S with cement).
- [x] **Stokes' Law formula** $v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o) g}{18 \mu}$ — applicable only for **laminar flow** ($Re < 1$).
- [x] **First-order biodegradation** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ with $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$.
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm** $q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ for activated carbon design.
- [x] **Hydrocyclones** handle 5–150 µm droplets; centrifuges handle fine emulsions below 10 µm.
- [x] **Bioremediation** is **slow** (weeks-months) and **climate-sensitive** — not a quick-fix method.
- [x] **TCLP test** determines landfill compliance for stabilized oily solids.
- [x] **Numerical trap:** Always convert **% to ppm** by multiplying by **10,000** before stage-wise efficiency calculations.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** Show **every step** in stage-wise efficiency problems and state the **compliance verdict** explicitly at the end.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMO Marine Engine Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IMO Marine Engine Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**IMO Marine Engine Regulations** constitute the international legal framework established primarily under **MARPOL Annex VI** (Regulations for the Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships) and the **NOx Technical Code 2008**, which govern the emission of harmful pollutants from marine diesel engines installed on seagoing vessels. The framework is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and applies to vessels operating under the flag of, or calling at ports of, signatory states.

The regulatory scope covers three principal pollutant categories:

- **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)** — controlled via the three-tier emission regime (Tier I, II, III).
- **SOx (Oxides of Sulfur)** — regulated via fuel sulfur content limits and Emission Control Areas (ECAs).
- **CO₂ and GHG (Greenhouse Gases)** — controlled via the **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** and **Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL Annex VI** entered into force on **19 May 2005** and represents the principal international convention addressing air pollution from ocean-going shipping. The annex has undergone multiple amendments, with progressively stricter emission limits aligned with the IMO Initial GHG Strategy (adopted 2018, revised 2023).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Vehicle PUC Certificate" Concept**

Imagine a car's exhaust being tested every year at a pollution-testing center (similar to India's PUC — Pollution Under Control certificate). Each car must meet emission caps based on its engine size, year of manufacture, and area of operation (metro cities enforce stricter standards).

The IMO Marine Engine Regulations work **identically** at a global maritime scale:

| Car / Two-Wheeler (Land Analogy) | Marine Engine (IMO) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Bharat Stage (BS-VI) emission norm | **MARPOL Annex VI Tier I / II / III** |
| PUC certificate / fitness testing | **EIAPP** (Engine International Air Pollution Prevention) Certificate + **IAPP Certificate** |
| Banned fuel types in cities (BS-IV fuel ban) | **Sulfur cap (0.10% in ECAs)** — IMO 2025 fuel rules |
| BS-VI applies only to vehicles sold after a date | **Tier limits apply to engines installed (keel-laid) on/after specific dates** |
| Catalytic converter retrofitting | **Scrubbers, SCR units, EGR, dual-fuel LNG retrofits** |

Just as a commercial truck entering Delhi must comply with **BS-VI** while one running only in rural Rajasthan may follow older norms, a ship entering the **Baltic Sea ECA** must burn fuel with ≤ **0.10% sulfur**, whereas in open ocean waters the cap is **0.50%** (post-2020).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

- **EIAPP Certificate**: Engine International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate — issued per engine after type approval and on-board NOx verification.
- **IAPP Certificate**: International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate — issued to the ship, covers all air pollution equipment.
- **EEDI Reference Line**: A baseline value of design CO₂ efficiency, set by IMO, that new ship designs must beat.
- **SECA / ECA**: Sulfur Emission Control Area / Emission Control Area — geographic zones with tighter sulfur (and in some cases, NOx) limits.
- **Tier III NOx**: The strictest NOx standard applicable **only inside designated NOx Tier III ECAs** (e.g., the North Sea, Baltic Sea, North American ECA, and U.S./Canadian Caribbean ECA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlight for Examiners**: Every student is expected to know that **Tier III does NOT apply globally** — only in approved NOx ECAs. This is a high-frequency board question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — MARPOL Annex VI Architecture

The regulatory framework operates in **three parallel enforcement streams**, each targeting a specific pollutant class. Below is the structured logic.

### Stream A — NOx Regulation (Regulation 13 of MARPOL Annex VI)

NOx emissions are regulated strictly based on the **rated engine speed (n) in rpm** and the **keel-laying date** of the ship:

1. **Tier I** — Applies to ships keel-laid on or after **1 January 2000** but before 2011.
2. **Tier II** — Applies to ships keel-laid on or after **1 January 2011** (global).
3. **Tier III** — Applies to ships keel-laid on or after **1 January 2016** operating **inside NOx Tier III ECAs**.

The governing equation for Tier II NOx limits:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier II}} = \frac{44 \times n^{-0.23}}{\text{g/kWh}}
$$

The governing equation for Tier III NOx limits:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier III}} = \frac{9 \times n^{-0.20}}{\text{g/kWh}}
$$

> **Engineering Utility**: This speed-dependent formula reflects the physical reality that **slow-speed two-stroke engines** (n ≈ 90–120 rpm) inherently produce higher specific NOx per kWh due to longer combustion duration and higher peak temperatures. Faster engines benefit from the inverse-power decay in allowable emission.

### Stream B — SOx Regulation (Regulation 14 of MARPOL Annex VI)

SOx is controlled indirectly via the **fuel oil sulfur content (FOSC)** cap:

- **Global Cap (outside ECAs)**: ≤ **0.50% m/m** sulfur (effective since 1 January 2020 — popularly known as **"IMO 2020"**).
- **ECA Cap (inside ECAs)**: ≤ **0.10% m/m** sulfur (effective since 1 January 2015).

**Why two control mechanisms?** Because SOx in exhaust is stoichiometrically tied to fuel sulfur content. So instead of measuring exhaust SOx, IMO measures fuel sulfur upstream.

The approximate emission factor (per gram of fuel burned):

$$
\text{SO}_2 \text{ emission} = 2 \times \text{FOSC} \times \text{Fuel Mass Consumed}
$$

Where the factor of **2** arises because each sulfur atom binds with two oxygen atoms (S → SO₂).

### Stream C — CO₂ / GHG Regulation (Chapter 4 of MARPOL Annex VI)

Two complementary instruments:

1. **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** — applicable to **new ships** ≥ 400 GT.
2. **SEEMP (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan)** — applicable to **all ships** in service.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Boundary / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Tier II NOx limit (130 ≤ n < 2000 rpm) | $\text{NOx} = 44 \cdot n^{-0.23}$ | g/kWh | n is rated engine speed in rpm |
| 2 | Tier III NOx limit (130 ≤ n < 2000 rpm) | $\text{NOx} = 9 \cdot n^{-0.20}$ | g/kWh | Only inside NOx Tier III ECAs |
| 3 | Constant-rpm Tier I limit | $\text{NOx} = 17.0$ (n<130), $5.0$ (n≥2000) | g/kWh | Piecewise constant for slow/fast engines |
| 4 | Constant-rpm Tier II limit | $\text{NOx} = 14.4$ (n<130), $3.4$ (n≥2000) | g/kWh | Piecewise constant for slow/fast engines |
| 5 | Constant-rpm Tier III limit | $\text{NOx} = 3.4$ (n<130), $2.0$ (n≥2000) | g/kWh | ECA-only application |
| 6 | SO₂ mass emitted per kg fuel | $m_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \cdot \text{FOSC} \cdot m_{\text{fuel}}$ | g | FOSC expressed as mass fraction |
| 7 | EEDI formula (general) | $\text{EEDI} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} \left( C_{F,i} \cdot SFC_i \cdot P_{ME,i} \right) - \text{P}_{\text{eff}} \cdot C_{F,\text{Aux}} \cdot SFC_{\text{Aux}}}{\text{Transport Work}}$ | g CO₂ / (tonnes·nm) | Power-based design metric |
| 8 | Reference line (general cargo, 2015–2024) | $\text{Required EEDI} = a \cdot b^{-c}$ | g CO₂ / (tonnes·nm) | a, b, c are IMO-tabulated ship-type coefficients |
| 9 | Attained EEDI reduction factor | $\dfrac{\text{Reference EEDI} - \text{Attained EEDI}}{\text{Reference EEDI}} \times 100$ | % | Must be ≥ Phase reduction % |
| 10 | Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) | $\text{SFOC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{P_{\text{engine}} \cdot t}$ | g/kWh | Lower is better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP**: In any KTU question asking for Tier II or Tier III NOx limits, **always identify the rpm range first** (n<130, 130≤n<2000, or n≥2000). The piecewise structure (constant vs. power law) is the most common source of lost marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards** must verify EEDI compliance at the **design stage** — influencing hull form, propeller choice, and engine selection.
- **Engine manufacturers** (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) offer "Tier II-ready" and "Tier III-ready" engine models with SCR after-treatment.
- **Bunker fuel suppliers** must produce **ULSFO (Ultra-Low Sulfur Fuel Oil, ≤0.10% S)** and **VLSFO (Very Low Sulfur Fuel Oil, ≤0.50% S)** grades.
- **Port State Control Officers** verify the IAPP and EIAPP certificates plus the **BDN (Bunker Delivery Note)** during port calls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Tier II NOx Compliance Calculation

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A marine diesel engine has a rated speed of **720 rpm** and rated power of **12,000 kW**. Determine whether the engine meets **MARPOL Annex VI Tier II** NOx requirements, assuming its measured NOx emission rate is **8.5 g/kWh**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Operating rpm Range

The rated speed is $n = 720$ rpm.

Compare against the boundaries:
- Lower boundary: $130 \text{ rpm} \le n$ → satisfied.
- Upper boundary: $n < 2000 \text{ rpm}$ → satisfied.

Therefore, the **power-law formula** applies (not the constant-end values).

### Step 2 — Apply the Tier II NOx Formula

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{limit}} = 44 \cdot n^{-0.23}
$$

Substituting $n = 720$:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{limit}} = 44 \cdot (720)^{-0.23}
$$

Evaluate the exponent term:

$$
720^{-0.23} = e^{-0.23 \cdot \ln(720)}
$$

Since $\ln(720) \approx 6.5793$:

$$
-0.23 \cdot 6.5793 = -1.5132
$$

$$
e^{-1.5132} \approx 0.2202
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{limit}} = 44 \cdot 0.2202 \approx 9.69 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare Measured vs. Limit

Measured emission = $8.5$ g/kWh.

$$
\text{NO}_{\text{x, measured}} = 8.5 \;<\; \text{NO}_{\text{x, limit}} = 9.69
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

**Result:** The engine **COMPLIES** with Tier II NOx requirements (margin = $9.69 - 8.5 = 1.19$ g/kWh).

> **Engineering Interpretation**: A positive compliance margin ensures the engine passes the **on-board NOx verification test** required by the NOx Technical Code during sea trials and major engine overhauls.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — SO₂ Emission from Fuel

**Problem:** A ship burns **1.0 tonne (1,000 kg) of HFO** with sulfur content **2.8% m/m** in open ocean. After installing scrubbers, it enters an ECA burning **ULSFO** with sulfur content **0.08% m/m**. Calculate the SO₂ emitted in each case and verify compliance.

### Case 1 — Open Ocean Burning HFO

Fuel sulfur mass fraction: $S_{\text{HFO}} = 0.028$.

$$
m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{HFO}} = 2 \cdot 0.028 \cdot 1000 = 56 \text{ kg SO}_2
$$

**IMO Cap (post-2020)**: 0.50% m/m →

$$
\text{Cap SO}_2 = 2 \cdot 0.0050 \cdot 1000 = 10 \text{ kg SO}_2
$$

The vessel emits $56$ kg > $10$ kg → **NON-COMPLIANT** in ECA.
But since 2.8% > 0.50%, this fuel **CANNOT be burned even in open ocean** under IMO 2020.

### Case 2 — ECA Burning ULSFO

$$
m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{ULSFO}} = 2 \cdot 0.0008 \cdot 1000 = 1.6 \text{ kg SO}_2
$$

ECA cap = $2 \cdot 0.0010 \cdot 1000 = 2.0$ kg SO₂.

**Result:** $1.6 < 2.0$ → **COMPLIANT** in ECA.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — EEDI Attained Value (Simplified)

**Problem:** A new bulk carrier has:
- Main engine power $P_{ME} = 15,000$ kW
- Auxiliary engine power $P_{AE} = 1,200$ kW
- SFOC of main engine $SFC_{ME} = 175$ g/kWh
- SFOC of auxiliary engine $SFC_{AE} = 200$ g/kWh
- Shaft generator / power take-out $P_{\text{eff}} = 0$ (ignored)
- $C_F$ (CO₂ emission factor for HFO) $= 3.114$ t CO₂ / t fuel
- Deadweight $DWT = 60,000$ tonnes
- Service speed $V_{\text{ref}} = 14$ knots

Calculate the **Attained EEDI**.

### Step 1 — Numerator (Power × SFOC × CF)

$$
\text{Num} = C_F \cdot \left( SFC_{ME} \cdot P_{ME} + SFC_{AE} \cdot P_{AE} \right)
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\text{Num} = 3.114 \cdot \left( 175 \cdot 15000 + 200 \cdot 1200 \right)
$$

Compute inner bracket:

$$
175 \cdot 15000 = 2{,}625{,}000 \text{ g/h} = 2625 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
200 \cdot 1200 = 240{,}000 \text{ g/h} = 240 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 2625 + 240 = 2865 \text{ kg fuel/h}
$$

Convert to tonnes: $2.865$ t fuel/h.

$$
\text{Num} = 3.114 \cdot 2.865 = 8.9216 \text{ t CO}_2 / \text{h}
$$

### Step 2 — Denominator (Transport Work)

$$
\text{TW} = DWT \cdot V_{\text{ref}}
$$

Convert $V_{\text{ref}} = 14$ knots to nautical miles per hour: $14$ nm/h.

$$
\text{TW} = 60{,}000 \cdot 14 = 840{,}000 \text{ tonnes·nm}
$$

### Step 3 — EEDI Calculation

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{att}} = \frac{8.9216}{840{,}000} \cdot 10^{6} \text{ g CO}_2 / (\text{tonnes·nm})
$$

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{att}} = \frac{8.9216 \cdot 10^{6}}{8.4 \cdot 10^{5}} = 10.62 \text{ g CO}_2 / (\text{tonnes·nm})
$$

### Step 4 — Reference Line (Approximation for Bulk Carrier, Phase 2)

For bulk carriers of $60{,}000$ DWT, IMO reference line (Phase 2, ~2025) ≈ $11.5$ g CO₂/(t·nm) (interpolated).

### Step 5 — Conclusion

The attained EEDI ($10.62$) is **below** the reference line ($11.5$), so the design is **compliant** with Phase 2.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compliance Decision Flow

| Parameter | Tier I | Tier II | Tier III (ECA only) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Keel-laid (general) | 2000–2010 | 2011 onwards | 2016 onwards |
| NOx @ n=100 rpm | 17.0 g/kWh | 14.4 g/kWh | 3.4 g/kWh |
| NOx @ n=720 rpm | 13.05 g/kWh | 9.69 g/kWh | 2.97 g/kWh |
| NOx @ n=2500 rpm | 5.0 g/kWh | 3.4 g/kWh | 2.0 g/kWh |
| Geographic scope | Global | Global | ECA only |

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction from Tier I to Tier III is approximately **80%** for slow-speed engines — reflecting IMO's commitment to **cut NOx emissions from international shipping**.

## 3.5 Bonus Insight — Why the rpm Split?

The piecewise design acknowledges two physical regimes:

1. **Slow-speed two-stroke engines** ($n < 130$ rpm): Long-stroke, large-bore engines dominating large merchant ships. They produce higher specific NOx due to longer combustion duration at higher peak in-cylinder temperatures. Hence, the allowable limit is a constant ceiling.

2. **High-speed four-stroke engines** ($n \geq 2000$ rpm): Compact auxiliary engines where rapid combustion limits peak temperature, naturally yielding lower NOx — hence the lower constant limit.

3. **Medium-speed range** ($130 \le n < 2000$ rpm): A continuous power-law bridges the two regimes, matching the empirical trend that NOx scales approximately as $n^{-0.2}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — IMO Marine Engine Regulations Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["IMO MARINE ENGINE REGULATORY FRAMEWORK"] --> B["MARPOL ANNEX VI"]
    A --> C["NOX TECHNICAL CODE 2008"]
    A --> D["IMO GHG STRATEGY 2023"]

    B --> B1["REGULATION 13 - NOx LIMITS"]
    B --> B2["REGULATION 14 - SOx AND PARTICULATE MATTER"]
    B --> B3["REGULATION 22 - EEDI AND SEEMP"]

    B1 --> B1a["TIER I - 2000 TO 2010"]
    B1 --> B1b["TIER II - 2011 ONWARDS GLOBAL"]
    B1 --> B1c["TIER III - 2016 ONWARDS IN ECA ONLY"]

    B2 --> B2a["GLOBAL SULFUR CAP 0.50 PERCENT IMO 2020"]
    B2 --> B2b["ECA SULFUR CAP 0.10 PERCENT"]
    B2 --> B2c["BUNKER DELIVERY NOTE BDN REQUIRED"]

    B3 --> B3a["EEDI FOR NEW SHIPS 400 GT AND ABOVE"]
    B3 --> B3b["SEEMP FOR ALL SHIPS IN SERVICE"]
    B3 --> B3c["CII CARBON INTENSITY INDICATOR"]

    C --> C1["ENGINE TYPE APPROVAL CERTIFICATION"]
    C --> C2["ON-BOARD NOx VERIFICATION TEST"]

    D --> D3["MID-TERM MEASURES FUEL STANDARD"]

    B1a --> E1["NOX LESS THAN 17.0 g PER kWh"]
    B1b --> E2["NOX LESS THAN 44 n TO THE POWER MINUS 0.23"]
    B1c --> E3["NOX LESS THAN 9 n TO THE POWER MINUS 0.20"]

    B2a --> F1["VSFO OR ULSFO BUNKERING"]
    B2b --> F2["ULSFO SCRUBBER OR LNG"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    P1 --> P2{"DOCUMENTS PRESENT?"}

    P2 -- NO --> P3a["DETENTION OR DEFICIENCY"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["ENGINE SPEED AXIS N IN RPM"] --> N2["REGION A: N LESS THAN 130 - CONSTANT LIMIT"]

    N2 --> N2a["SLOW SPEED TWO STROKE - DOMINANT IN TANKERS BULK CARRIERS"]

    N2a --> R1["TIER I 17.0 - TIER II 14.4 - TIER III 3.4"]
```
