# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define MARPOL Annex VI. List its three primary pollutant categories that it regulates.**

**Model Answer:**

MARPOL Annex VI, titled *"Regulations for the Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships,"* is the international convention adopted under the International Maritime Organization (IMO) that came into force on **19 May 2005**. It sets legally binding limits on airborne emissions from seagoing vessels.

The three primary pollutant categories regulated are:

1. **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)** — via Regulation 13, three-tier emission limits.
2. **SOx (Oxides of Sulfur) and Particulate Matter** — via Regulation 14, sulfur-in-fuel caps.
3. **CO₂ and Other Greenhouse Gases** — via Chapter 4 (Regulations 19–22), EEDI and SEEMP.

*(Self-contained, 3-mark-ready answer.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is an Emission Control Area (ECA)? Name any two ECAs currently designated under MARPOL Annex VI.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Emission Control Area (ECA)** is a designated sea zone where stricter emission control measures apply than in open international waters, due to environmental sensitivity, dense shipping traffic, or population exposure.

Two designated ECAs are:

1. **Baltic Sea ECA** (SOx since 2006, NOx Tier III from 2021).
2. **North Sea ECA** (SOx since 2007, NOx Tier III from 2021).
3. *(Optional third)* **North American ECA** (covers U.S. and Canadian coasts — SOx and NOx Tier III from 2016).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the MARPOL Annex VI Tier I, Tier II, and Tier III NOx emission limits for marine diesel engines. Discuss the role of rated engine speed in determining the applicable limit, with suitable formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

**Background:** Regulation 13 of MARPOL Annex VI imposes NOx emission limits on marine diesel engines depending on the **date of keel-laying** (i.e., when construction began) and the **rated engine speed (n)** in rpm. The limits apply in three tiers:

1. **Tier I**: Ships keel-laid **on or after 1 January 2000** but before 1 January 2011.
2. **Tier II**: Ships keel-laid **on or after 1 January 2011** — applicable globally.
3. **Tier III**: Ships keel-laid **on or after 1 January 2016** — applicable only inside designated **NOx Tier III ECAs** (e.g., North Sea, Baltic Sea, North American, U.S./Canadian Caribbean).

**The role of rated engine speed (n):** Because NOx formation in diesel combustion depends on peak in-cylinder temperature and duration, which in turn correlates with engine speed, IMO specifies **three speed regimes**:

- **n < 130 rpm** → constant ceiling (slow-speed two-stroke engines).
- **130 ≤ n < 2000 rpm** → power-law formula (medium-speed engines).
- **n ≥ 2000 rpm** → constant lower ceiling (high-speed engines).

**Tier II NOx Formulas (key exam content):**

For $n < 130$ rpm:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier II}} = 14.4 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

For $130 \le n < 2000$ rpm:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier II}} = 44 \cdot n^{-0.23} \text{ g/kWh}
$$

For $n \ge 2000$ rpm:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier II}} = 3.4 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Tier III NOx Formulas:**

For $130 \le n < 2000$ rpm:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier III}} = 9 \cdot n^{-0.20} \text{ g/kWh}
$$

For $n \ge 2000$ rpm:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier III}} = 2.0 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The transition from Tier II to Tier III achieves a **~80% reduction** in allowable NOx for slow-speed engines — a major driver of **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** after-treatment adoption.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A marine main propulsion engine has a rated speed of 144 rpm and a measured NOx emission of 6.8 g/kWh. Verify whether it complies with Tier III NOx requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify rpm regime:**

Rated speed $n = 144$ rpm, which lies in the range $130 \le n < 2000$ rpm. Therefore, the **power-law Tier III formula** applies:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier III}} = 9 \cdot n^{-0.20}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the limit:**

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{limit}} = 9 \cdot (144)^{-0.20}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
(144)^{-0.20} = e^{-0.20 \cdot \ln(144)} = e^{-0.20 \cdot 4.9698} = e^{-0.9940} \approx 0.3702
$$

$$
\text{NOx}_{\text{limit}} = 9 \cdot 0.3702 \approx 3.33 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

Measured NOx $= 6.8$ g/kWh; Limit $= 3.33$ g/kWh.

$$
6.8 > 3.33
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

**The engine DOES NOT comply** with Tier III. Non-compliance margin $= 6.8 - 3.33 = 3.47$ g/kWh.

**Engineering remedy:** Install **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** after-treatment to reduce NOx by 80–95%, OR retrofit to dual-fuel LNG operation.

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the SOx emission control regulations under MARPOL Annex VI. Explain the concept of Emission Control Areas (ECAs) and how fuel sulfur content is linked to SOx emissions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Background:** Regulation 14 of MARPOL Annex VI controls SOx emissions from ships. Rather than measuring SOx in the exhaust directly, the regulation controls the **sulfur content of the fuel oil** before combustion — a pragmatic upstream control strategy.

**Sulfur Caps:**

- **Global cap (outside ECAs)**: ≤ **0.50% m/m** sulfur in fuel, effective **1 January 2020** ("IMO 2020" rule).
- **ECA cap (inside ECAs)**: ≤ **0.10% m/m** sulfur in fuel, effective **1 January 2015**.

**SOx-to-Fuel Sulfur Link:** During combustion, sulfur in fuel converts to SO₂ (and partially to SO₃) in proportion to the sulfur mass. The stoichiometric conversion is:

$$
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2
$$

Hence SO₂ emission per kg of fuel burned is:

$$
m_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \cdot S_{\text{fuel}} \cdot m_{\text{fuel}}
$$

**Why ECAs?** ECAs are zones of high environmental sensitivity — coastal ecosystems, dense populations, or enclosed seas — where additional reductions are warranted. Current ECAs include the Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American, and U.S./Canadian Caribbean ECA.

**Compliance Pathways for Ship Operators:**

1. **Switch to compliant fuel** (ULSFO ≤ 0.10% in ECAs; VLSFO ≤ 0.50% global).
2. **Install Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / scrubbers)** — continue burning HFO but wash SO₂ from exhaust.
3. **Switch to alternative fuels** — LNG, methanol, biofuels.

**Enforcement:** Port State Control officers verify compliance via the **Bunker Delivery Note (BDN)** — a mandatory document from the fuel supplier stating sulfur content — and may take **fuel samples** for laboratory verification (in-service MARPOL Annex VI sampling).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A bulk carrier burns 25 tonnes of IFO 380 in 24 hours while transiting the Indian Ocean. The fuel sulfur content is 3.5% m/m. Determine (i) the SO₂ emitted per hour, and (ii) whether this complies with current IMO global sulfur limits.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Fuel consumption: $25{,}000$ kg per 24 h.
- Hourly consumption: $m_{\text{fuel}} = 25{,}000 / 24 \approx 1041.67$ kg/h.
- Sulfur content: $S = 0.035$ (mass fraction).

**Step (i) — SO₂ emitted per hour:**

$$
m_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \cdot S \cdot m_{\text{fuel}} = 2 \cdot 0.035 \cdot 1041.67
$$

$$
m_{\text{SO}_2} = 0.07 \cdot 1041.67 \approx 72.92 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step (ii) — Compliance Check:**

IMO global cap (post-2020) = $0.50\% = 0.0050$ mass fraction.

SO₂ allowed per hour (compliant fuel at same consumption):

$$
m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{cap}} = 2 \cdot 0.0050 \cdot 1041.67 = 10.42 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
72.92 \;>\; 10.42 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel **DOES NOT COMPLY** with the IMO 2020 global sulfur limit. The fuel (3.5% S) is illegal under MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 14 since 1 January 2020.

**Required Action:** The ship must bunker **VLSFO** (≤ 0.50% S) or install and operate an **approved scrubber** (EGCS) to continue using high-sulfur HFO while meeting emission equivalency.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the piecewise structure** of NOx limits — applying the power-law formula at n < 130 rpm or n ≥ 2000 rpm. (Penalty: 2–3 marks per question.)
> 2. **Confusing Tier III applicability** — writing Tier III as "global" rather than "ECA-only." (Penalty: 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Using post-combustion SOx directly** instead of the upstream fuel-sulfur control logic. (Penalty: 2 marks.)
> 4. **Missing the stoichiometric factor of 2** when computing SO₂ from sulfur mass. (Penalty: 1 mark.)
> 5. **Not mentioning Bunker Delivery Note (BDN)** in SOx compliance answers — it is the *enforcement document*. (Penalty: 1–2 marks.)
> 6. **EEDI calculation errors** — most commonly in unit conversion (g vs. tonnes, knots vs. nm/h). (Penalty: 2–3 marks.)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MARPOL Annex VI** is the centerpiece convention — entry into force **19 May 2005**.
- **NOx Tiers**:
  - Tier I → Tier II → Tier III represents progressive **80% reduction** in slow-speed engine emissions.
  - The rpm-regime split is **n < 130**, **130 ≤ n < 2000**, and **n ≥ 2000**.
  - The Tier II formula for mid-speed is **$44 \cdot n^{-0.23}$ g/kWh**; Tier III mid-speed is **$9 \cdot n^{-0.20}$ g/kWh**.
- **SOx regulation is upstream** — via **fuel sulfur content**, not exhaust measurement.
  - **Global cap = 0.50% S** ("IMO 2020").
  - **ECA cap = 0.10% S** (since 2015).
- **SO₂ mass** from fuel: $m_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \cdot S_{\text{fuel}} \cdot m_{\text{fuel}}$.
- **ECAs** currently: Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American, U.S./Canadian Caribbean.
- **EEDI** — applies to **new ships ≥ 400 GT**; reference line-based.
- **SEEMP** — applies to **all ships in service**; plan-based energy management.
- **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** — new regulation (2023) requiring annual rating A–E.
- **Certificates to remember**: **EIAPP** (engine), **IAPP** (ship), **BDN** (fuel delivery).
- **IMO GHG Strategy (2023 revision)**: Net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping targeted by **2050**, with indicative checkpoints at 2030 (≥ 20% reduction, striving for 30%) and 2040 (≥ 70%, striving for 80%).
- **Alternative fuels** gaining traction: **LNG, methanol, ammonia, hydrogen** — all intended to support the 2050 net-zero pathway.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements for Survey and Issuance of International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Requirements for Survey and Issuance of International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Academic Definition

The **International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP Certificate)** is a statutory document issued to a ship in accordance with the requirements of **MARPOL Annex VI** (Regulations for the Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships), under the authority of the International Maritime Organization (IMO). It certifies that the ship has been surveyed in accordance with the provisions of the survey regulations and that its equipment, systems, fittings, arrangements, and materials comply with the applicable requirements of MARPOL Annex VI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Authority:** The IAPP Certificate is mandated under **Regulation 5** of MARPOL Annex VI. It applies to all ships of **400 GT and above** engaged in international voyages, and to fixed and floating drilling rigs and other platforms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the IAPP Certificate as the **"Pollution Clearance Stamp"** for a ship's air emissions — analogous to a vehicle's PUC (Pollution Under Control) certificate in road transport. Just as a car must be inspected periodically to ensure its exhaust does not pollute the air, a ship must undergo systematic surveys to verify that its engines, boilers, incinerators, and fuel systems do not release harmful gases (SOx, NOx, ODS) into the atmosphere. Without this certificate, the ship is legally forbidden from operating internationally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference Metric for KTU:**
> - **Gross Tonnage (GT) Threshold:** **≥ 400 GT** for international voyages
> - **Certificate Validity:** **5 years** from date of issue
> - **Issuing Authority:** Flag State Administration or Recognized Organization (RO) on its behalf

### 1.3 Categories of Ships Requiring the IAPP

| Category | Description | IAPP Required? |
|---|---|---|
| **Ships ≥ 400 GT (International)** | Engaged in international voyages | **Yes (Mandatory)** |
| **Ships < 400 GT** | Below threshold | Not required by Annex VI |
| **Drilling Rigs / Platforms** | Fixed/Floating | Yes, where applicable |
| **Warships / Naval Auxiliaries** | Non-commercial | Exempt (State-owned) |

### 1.4 Connection to MARPOL Annex VI Chapters

The IAPP Certificate is the legal proof of compliance with regulations covering:

1. **Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS)** – Regulation 12
2. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)** – Regulation 13 (Tier I, II, III)
3. **Sulphur Oxides (SOx) and Particulate Matter (PM)** – Regulation 14
4. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC)** – Regulation 15
5. **Shipboard Incineration** – Regulation 16
6. **Reception Facilities** – Regulation 17
7. **Fuel Oil Quality** – Regulation 18
8. **Air Pollution from Platforms** – Regulation 19
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why IAPP Survey Exists

The IAPP survey framework exists to:
- Enforce the **polluter-pays principle** through technical compliance verification.
- Ensure that emission control technologies (SCR, EGR, SOx scrubbers) are functional throughout the ship's operational life.
- Provide port State control (PSC) officers with a standardized, internationally recognized compliance document.
- Drive continuous technological improvement in marine propulsion and auxiliary systems.

### 2.2 The Four Mandatory Survey Types

The IAPP certification regime comprises four distinct survey categories, each with specific timing requirements:

| Survey Type | Timing Requirement | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Survey** | Before the ship is put into service OR before IAPP is issued for the first time | Full verification of all MARPOL Annex VI requirements before commissioning |
| **Annual Survey** | Within 3 months before or after each anniversary date of the IAPP | General inspection to confirm continued compliance |
| **Intermediate Survey** | Within 3 months before or after the **2nd or 3rd anniversary date** (RO/Admin choice) | Substantive inspection replacing one of the annual surveys between renewals |
| **Renewal Survey** | Before the IAPP certificate expires | Full survey comparable to Initial Survey; required for 5-year re-issuance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interval Logic:** A typical 5-year IAPP cycle includes 1 Initial → 3 Annual (with one replaced by Intermediate) → 1 Renewal Survey.

### 2.3 Detailed Compliance Verification Requirements

The surveyor must confirm compliance with the following technical areas:

#### 2.3.1 Ozone-Depleting Substances (Regulation 12)
- No **ODS-containing equipment** (older CFC-based chillers, Halon fire-fighting systems) is permitted on board.
- Records of deliberate ODS emissions must be maintained in the **Ozone-Depleting Substances Record Book**.

#### 2.3.2 NOx Compliance (Regulation 13)
- Diesel engines > 130 kW must meet Tier limits based on keel-lay date:
  - **Tier I:** $NO_x \leq 17.0 \text{ g/kWh}$ (Pre-2000 keels)
  - **Tier II:** $NO_x \leq 14.4 \text{ g/kWh}$ (2011 onwards)
  - **Tier III:** $NO_x \leq 3.4 \text{ g/kWh}$ (2016 onwards, in ECA)
- **EIAPP Certificate** (Engine International Air Pollution Prevention) required for each engine.
- Technical file, parameter file, and on-board monitoring software verified.

#### 2.3.3 SOx and Particulate Matter (Regulation 14)

$$S_{fuel} \leq \begin{cases} 0.10\% \, m/m & \text{inside ECA (Emission Control Area)} \\ 0.50\% \, m/m & \text{outside ECA (Global cap)} \end{cases}$$

Compliance demonstrated by:
- Use of **low-sulphur fuel oil (LSFO)**
- Approved **exhaust gas cleaning systems (EGCS / scrubbers)**
- Alternative compliance methods approved by Administration

#### 2.3.4 VOC Systems (Regulation 15)
- **VOC Management Plan** required for tankers carrying crude oil.
- Vapor return piping and vapor collection systems verified.

#### 2.3.5 Shipboard Incineration (Regulation 16)
- Incinerator must be **IMO Type-approved** (per IMO Resolution MEPC.244(66)).
- Garbage categories that may be incinerated verified.
- Operational log maintained.

#### 2.3.6 Fuel Oil Quality (Regulation 18)
- **Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN)** with sulphur content retained for at least **3 years**.
- **MARPOL Annex VI Fuel Oil Sample** retained onboard for 12 months.
- Fuel oil changeover procedure verified for ECA transit.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Compliance Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All boundary conditions use LaTeX vertical bars $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid markdown breakage.

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Limit | Applicable Vessels |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass threshold for IAPP | $GT$ | $\geq 400$ GT | All international ships |
| Engine power threshold for NOx | $P$ | $> 130$ kW | Diesel engines |
| Global sulphur cap | $S_{global}$ | $\leq 0.50\% \, m/m$ | Outside ECA |
| ECA sulphur cap | $S_{ECA}$ | $\leq 0.10\% \, m/m$ | Inside ECA (Baltic, North Sea, N. American, Mediterranean) |
| Tier I NOx | $E_{NO_x}^{I}$ | $\leq 17.0$ g/kWh | Keel laid ≤ 2000 |
| Tier II NOx | $E_{NO_x}^{II}$ | $\leq 14.4$ g/kWh | Keel laid 2011–2015/2020 |
| Tier III NOx | $E_{NO_x}^{III}$ | $\leq 3.4$ g/kWh | ECA vessels, keel laid ≥ 2016 |
| BDN retention period | $t_{BDN}$ | $\geq 3$ years | All Annex VI ships |
| Fuel oil sample retention | $t_{FOS}$ | $\geq 12$ months | All Annex VI ships |
| IAPP certificate validity | $T_{IAPP}$ | $5$ years | All Annex VI ships |
| Anniversary tolerance | $\Delta t$ | $\pm 3$ months | Annual/Intermediate surveys |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IAPP regime drives design choices in:
- **Engine manufacturers:** Forced to develop Tier-III-compliant SCR/EGR systems.
- **Shipyards:** Must integrate exhaust scrubber spaces and fuel-flexible fuel systems.
- **Bunker suppliers:** Required to provide low-sulphur fuels and accurate BDNs.
- **Classification Societies (IACS members):** Act as Recognized Organizations performing surveys on behalf of flag states.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Logical Derivation: The IAPP Survey Cycle

The IAPP survey schedule is derived directly from MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 5. Below is the exhaustive logical derivation of the 5-year compliance cycle.

#### Step 1: Establish the Certificate Validity

The base validity of the IAPP Certificate is defined by international convention as:

$$T_{IAPP} = 5 \text{ years}$$

This forms the master period over which all other survey intervals are calculated.

#### Step 2: Determine the Anniversary Date

If the IAPP certificate is issued on calendar date $D_0$, the **anniversary date** is the same calendar date in each subsequent year. Formally:

$$D_{anniversary} = D_0 + n \cdot (1 \text{ year}), \quad n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$$

#### Step 3: Define the Annual Survey Window

For each anniversary, the Annual Survey must be conducted within a tolerance window of $\pm 3$ months:

$$D_{annual} \in [D_0 + n \cdot 365 - 90, \; D_0 + n \cdot 365 + 90] \text{ days}$$

#### Step 4: Replace One Annual with Intermediate

The Intermediate Survey is conducted at either the 2nd or 3rd anniversary (at the option of the Administration/RO). It is more thorough than the Annual Survey.

#### Step 5: Renewal Survey Concludes the Cycle

The Renewal Survey must be completed before the certificate expires:

$$D_{renewal} \leq D_0 + 5 \cdot 365 = D_0 + 1825 \text{ days}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Survey Type vs. Inspection Depth

| Survey Component | Initial | Annual | Intermediate | Renewal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NOx Technical File check** | Full | Spot check | Comprehensive | Full |
| **EIAPP Certificate validation** | Yes | Verify validity | Verify validity | Yes |
| **SCR/EGR system test** | Yes | Visual | Operational | Full |
| **SOx scrubber (EGCS) test** | Yes | Visual | Functional | Full |
| **Fuel oil changeover drill** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| **BDN review (12 months)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **BDN review (full 5 yrs)** | No | No | No | Yes |
| **ODS Record Book check** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Incinerator log review** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **VOC Management Plan** | Yes | Verify | Verify | Yes |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Procedural Flow for IAPP Issuance

**Step 1: Pre-Application**
The shipowner submits application to the **Recognized Organization (RO)** or Flag Administration with:
- Ship particulars (GT, propulsion type, keel-lay date, engine specs)
- Class status
- Existing statutory certificates

**Step 2: Document Review**
Surveyor reviews:
- Technical File for each marine diesel engine
- EIAPP Certificates
- VOC Management Plan (if applicable)
- Bunker Delivery Notes history
- Ozone-Depleting Substances Record Book
- Incinerator type-approval certificate

**Step 3: On-Board Physical Survey**
The surveyor verifies:
- Equipment identification numbers
- Piping and instrumentation diagrams
- Emission control system (scrubber/SCR/EGR) installation
- Sampling points and measurement devices
- Fuel oil changeover procedures

**Step 4: Functional Testing**
- Engine NOx measurement (on-board NO_x monitoring)
- Scrubber washwater pH, turbidity, PAH monitoring
- Incinerator combustion temperature verification ($\geq 850°C$ for IMO Type approval)

**Step 5: Compliance Endorsement**
If all tests pass, the IAPP Certificate is signed and stamped.

**Step 6: Records & Filing**
- Survey Report filed with RO/Administration.
- IAPP Certificate issued for 5 years.
- Supplemental Report form issued for any deficiency (must be rectified before next survey).

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Survey Window Calculation

**Given:**
- IAPP Certificate originally issued on **15 March 2022**
- We need to determine the window for the **3rd Annual Survey**

**Solution:**

The 3rd anniversary date is:

$$D_3 = 15 \text{ March} 2022 + 3 \text{ years} = 15 \text{ March } 2025$$

The Annual Survey window (with $\pm 3$ months = $\pm 90$ days) is:

$$D_{window} \in [15 \text{ Dec } 2024, \; 14 \text{ June } 2025]$$

Therefore, the ship must be presented for survey any day within this 6-month window. If the Administration elects this anniversary for the **Intermediate Survey**, the survey depth increases from spot-check to comprehensive.

### 3.5 Derivation of Sulphur Compliance Logic

To demonstrate SOx compliance under Regulation 14, the surveyor verifies one of three pathways:

$$\text{Compliance} = \begin{cases} \text{Pathway A:} & S_{fuel} \leq 0.50\% \, (0.10\% \text{ in ECA}) \\ \text{Pathway B:} & \text{EGCS installed and operational} \\ \text{Pathway C:} & \text{Alternative method approved by Administration} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The surveyor must verify the **EGCS continuous monitoring** parameters including SO_2/CO_2 ratio, washwater discharge pH ($\geq 6.5$ at overboard), and PAH levels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IAPP Survey Cycle — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Construction / New Acquisition] --> B{Keel Laid Date & GT?}
    B -->|GT >= 400| C[Initial Survey - Pre-Commissioning]
    B -->|GT < 400| Z[Exempt from IAPP - Annex VI]
    C --> D[Issue IAPP Certificate - 5 Year Validity]
    D --> E[Year 1: Annual Survey]
    E --> F[Year 2: Annual OR Intermediate Survey]
    F --> G[Year 3: Annual OR Intermediate Survey]
    G --> H[Year 4: Annual Survey]
    H --> I[Year 5: Renewal Survey]
    I --> J{Compliance Achieved?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Re-Issue IAPP - New 5 Year Cycle]
    J -->|No - Deficiency| L[Issue Supplemental Report]
    L --> M[Rectification within 3 Months]
    M --> J
    K --> E
```

### 4.2 IAPP Survey Scope — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SURVEY[Survey Scope Verification]
        A1[NOx Compliance - Reg 13]
        A2[SOx and PM - Reg 14]
        A3[ODS Ban - Reg 12]
        A4[VOC Systems - Reg 15]
        A5[Incineration - Reg 16]
        A6[Fuel Oil Quality - Reg 18]
    end
    subgraph DOC[Document Verification]
        B1[EIAPP Certificates]
        B2[Bunker Delivery Notes]
        B3[Technical Files]
        B4[ODS Record Book]
        B5[VOC Management Plan]
    end
    subgraph EQUIP[Equipment Verification]
        C1[SCR and EGR Systems]
        C2[EGCS Scrubbers]
        C3[Fuel Oil Tanks and Piping]
        C4[Incinerator IMO Type Approval]
        C5[Sampling and Monitoring]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B4
    A4 --> B5
    A5 --> C4
    A6 --> C3
    B1 --> C5
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> C1
    SURVEY --> OUT[Issue IAPP Certificate]
    DOC --> OUT
    EQUIP --> OUT
```

### 4.3 IAPP Issuance Authority Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Flag State Administration] -->|Authorizes| B0[Recognized Organization RO]
    A0 -->|Direct Issue| C0[IAPP Certificate]
    B0 -->|Conducts Surveys| D0[Survey Report]
    D0 -->|Recommend| C0
    C0 -->|5 Years| E0[Ship Operates Internationally]
    E0 -->|PSC Inspection| F0[Port State Control Verify]
    F0 -->|Valid| G0[Continue Operations]
    F0 -->|Deficiency| H0[Detain or Rectify]
```

### 4.4 Survey Type Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Survey Trigger Event] --> q1{Is it the first time?}
    q1 -->|Yes| init[Initial Survey - Full Scope]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Within 5-year cycle?}
    q2 -->|No| renew[Renewal Survey - Full Scope]
    q2 -->|Yes| q3{Is it the 2nd or 3rd anniversary?}
    q3 -->|Yes, chosen by Admin| inter[Intermediate Survey - Comprehensive]
    q3 -->|No| ann[Annual Survey - Spot Check]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**List the four types of survey required for the issuance and maintenance of the IAPP Certificate under MARPOL Annex VI.**

**Model Answer:**

The four mandatory survey types are:

1. **Initial Survey** — Conducted before the ship is put into service or before the IAPP Certificate is issued for the first time, ensuring full compliance of all equipment, systems, and materials.

2. **Annual Survey** — Conducted within **3 months before or after** each anniversary date of the IAPP Certificate, providing general verification of continued compliance.

3. **Intermediate Survey** — Conducted within 3 months before or after the **2nd or 3rd anniversary date** (at the Administration's option), replacing one Annual Survey with a more substantive inspection.

4. **Renewal Survey** — Conducted before the certificate expires, with the same scope as the Initial Survey, enabling re-issuance of a fresh 5-year certificate.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**State the applicability threshold and the validity period of the IAPP Certificate.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Applicability Threshold:** The IAPP Certificate is required for **all ships of 400 GT and above** engaged in international voyages, as well as for fixed and floating drilling rigs and other platforms.
- **Validity Period:** The IAPP Certificate is valid for a period of **5 years** from the date of issue, subject to compliance with annual and intermediate surveys.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the survey procedure and the technical requirements to be verified for the issuance of the IAPP Certificate under MARPOL Annex VI. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the requirements of MARPOL Annex VI with respect to NOx emissions, including Tier I, II, and III standards, and the role of the EIAPP Certificate. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Survey Procedure and Technical Requirements for IAPP Issuance (7 Marks)**

The issuance of the IAPP Certificate requires an **Initial Survey** prior to the ship being put into service or before the certificate is issued for the first time. The procedure is as follows:

**Step 1: Application and Document Review (2 Marks)**
The shipowner submits a formal application to the Flag Administration or its Recognized Organization (RO). The surveyor reviews the EIAPP Certificates, the NOx Technical File, the Bunker Delivery Notes, the Ozone-Depleting Substances Record Book, the VOC Management Plan (if applicable), and the shipboard incinerator type-approval certificate.

**Step 2: On-Board Physical Inspection (2 Marks)**
The surveyor physically inspects all emission-related equipment, including diesel engines, exhaust gas cleaning systems (EGCS / scrubbers), Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) systems, fuel oil tanks and piping, incinerators, and VOC vapor return systems. Identification numbers and the conformity of installation with approved drawings are verified.

**Step 3: Functional Testing (2 Marks)**
Functional tests are conducted, including NOx on-board monitoring, scrubber washwater quality (pH, turbidity, PAH), incinerator combustion chamber temperature ($\geq 850^\circ C$ for IMO Type approval), and fuel oil changeover procedures for ECA transit.

**Step 4: Issuance (1 Mark)**
Upon satisfactory completion, the IAPP Certificate is signed and stamped by the surveyor/Administration, with validity of 5 years. Any deficiency is recorded in a Supplemental Report, requiring rectification within 3 months.

---

**(b) NOx Emission Requirements and EIAPP Certificate (7 Marks)**

Under **MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 13**, marine diesel engines with power output greater than **130 kW** installed on board a ship must comply with NOx emission limits depending on the **keel-lay date** and operational area:

**Tier I** (Keel laid on or after 1 January 2000):
$$NO_x \leq 17.0 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Tier II** (Keel laid on or after 1 January 2011):
$$NO_x \leq 14.4 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Tier III** (Keel laid on or after 1 January 2016, operating in ECA):
$$NO_x \leq 3.4 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Role of the EIAPP Certificate (3 Marks):**
The **Engine International Air Pollution Prevention (EIAPP) Certificate** is issued per engine, certifying that the engine has been factory-tested and complies with the applicable NOx Tier limit. Each engine must have an EIAPP Certificate, an **Engine Technical File** containing test data, a **parameter file** recording the parent engine's settings, and **on-board NOx monitoring** software. During the IAPP survey, the surveyor verifies the presence, validity, and consistency of the EIAPP Certificate, the Technical File, and the parameter file against the installed engine.

---

#### QUESTION B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the requirements of MARPOL Annex VI regarding sulphur oxides (SOx) and particulate matter (PM) control from ships, and explain the three pathways to demonstrate compliance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the records, documents, and operational procedures that must be verified during an IAPP survey with respect to fuel oil quality and ozone-depleting substances. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SOx and PM Control Requirements under MARPOL Annex VI (7 Marks)**

**Regulation 14** of MARPOL Annex VI limits the sulphur content of fuel oils used on board ships:

**Global Sulphur Cap (since 1 January 2020):**
$$S_{fuel} \leq 0.50\% \, m/m$$

**ECA Sulphur Cap (since 1 January 2015):**
$$S_{fuel} \leq 0.10\% \, m/m$$

**Three Compliance Pathways (5 Marks):**

1. **Pathway A — Low-Sulphur Fuel Oil (LSFO):** The ship burns compliant fuel oil with sulphur content within the prescribed limits. The surveyor verifies Bunker Delivery Notes (BDNs) and the ship's bunker management procedures.

2. **Pathway B — Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / Scrubbers):** The ship uses an approved scrubber to clean exhaust gases, achieving equivalent SOx reduction. The surveyor verifies the scrubber's type approval certificate, continuous emission monitoring system (CEMS), and washwater discharge monitoring (pH ≥ 6.5, PAH limits).

3. **Pathway C — Alternative Methods:** Any other innovative method approved by the Flag Administration, such as LNG fuel or biofuel, that achieves equivalent emission reduction. Surveyor verifies the approval letter and operational compliance.

**Particulate Matter:** PM reductions are achieved indirectly through SOx controls, as lower sulphur fuels and scrubbers also reduce PM emissions.

---

**(b) Records, Documents, and Procedures for Fuel Oil and ODS (7 Marks)**

**Fuel Oil Quality Records (4 Marks):**

1. **Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN):** Must contain sulphur content, density, and supplier details. Retained onboard for at least **3 years**.
2. **Fuel Oil Sample (MARPOL Sample):** A representative sample of each bunker delivery is retained onboard sealed for **at least 12 months** for verification.
3. **Fuel Oil Changeover Procedure:** The ship must document and demonstrate the procedure for switching to low-sulphur fuel when entering an ECA. The procedure includes tank management, fuel system flushing, and timing of the changeover.
4. **Fuel Oil Non-Availability Report (FONAR):** If compliant fuel cannot be obtained, this report must be filed.

**Ozone-Depleting Substances (Regulation 12) (3 Marks):**

1. **ODS Ban:** New installations containing ODS (CFCs, Halons, HCFCs) are prohibited.
2. **Ozone-Depleting Substances Record Book:** All activities involving ODS (re-charging, leaks, disposal) must be recorded.
3. **Equipment Inventory:** Surveyor verifies that no equipment with controlled refrigerants (e.g., R-22) is present without proper records.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EIAPP with IAPP:** Students often mix up the **Engine IAPP (EIAPP)** certificate (per engine) and the **IAPP** (whole-ship). The EIAPP is part of the IAPP survey, not a separate certificate.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3-month tolerance window:** Surveys are NOT exactly on the anniversary date — they are within a 6-month window ($\pm 3$ months).
> 3. **Missing ECA sulphur cap:** Forgetting the 0.10% inside ECA is a frequent KTU marking penalty.
> 4. **Not stating sample retention periods:** BDN ($\geq 3$ years) vs. MARPOL sample ($\geq 12$ months) are different and often confused.
> 5. **Omitting the Intermediate Survey option:** Many students wrongly list only 3 surveys; the Intermediate Survey is the 4th, replacing one Annual Survey.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision:**

- ☐ IAPP Certificate is issued under **MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 5**
- ☐ Applies to ships **≥ 400 GT** engaged in **international voyages**
- ☐ Validity: **5 years**
- ☐ **Four surveys:** Initial, Annual, Intermediate, Renewal
- ☐ Annual Survey window: **± 3 months** from anniversary
- ☐ Intermediate Survey at **2nd or 3rd anniversary** (Admin choice)
- ☐ NOx Tier I: **17.0 g/kWh**, Tier II: **14.4 g/kWh**, Tier III: **3.4 g/kWh** (ECA)
- ☐ NOx applicable to diesel engines **> 130 kW**
- ☐ **EIAPP Certificate** required per engine
- ☐ Global sulphur cap: **0.50% m/m**
- ☐ ECA sulphur cap: **0.10% m/m**
- ☐ Three SOx compliance pathways: LSFO, EGCS, Alternative methods
- ☐ BDN retention: **≥ 3 years**
- ☐ MARPOL Fuel Oil Sample retention: **≥ 12 months**
- ☐ ODS: new installations banned; Record Book maintained
- ☐ Incinerator: IMO Type-approved; combustion temp **≥ 850°C**
- ☐ Issuing authority: Flag State Admin or Recognized Organization (RO)
- ☐ VOC Management Plan required for crude oil tankers
- ☐ Survey conducted by **IACS Classification Societies** (acting as ROs)
- ☐ Deficiencies recorded in **Supplemental Report**; rectified within 3 months
- ☐ PSC officers verify validity during port State control inspections
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** is a performance-based, non-prescriptive regulatory metric developed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **MARPOL Annex VI, Chapter 4** (Resolution **MEPC.245(66)** as amended). It quantifies the **grams of CO₂ emitted per tonne-mile** of transport work for new ships, providing a baseline against which the design efficiency of a vessel is measured.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{EEDI} \;=\; \frac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions of the ship}}{\text{Carrying capacity} \times \text{Distance travelled}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The EEDI is **mandatory** for all newbuild ships of **400 GT and above** engaged in international trade, with progressively tighter reduction phases (Phase 0 → Phase 3) targeting up to **30% reduction** in CO₂ intensity compared to the baseline reference line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship's Fuel Economy Sticker"

Imagine buying a car. Before purchasing, you read the **km/litre or miles-per-gallon** sticker on the windshield. That single number tells you how efficiently the car converts fuel into motion. Now scale this idea up:

- **Car scenario:** A driver wants to know how much fuel is burned per kilometre driven → MPG rating.
- **Ship scenario (EEDI):** A regulator wants to know how much **CO₂ is emitted per tonne of cargo carried per nautical mile** sailed.

In the same way that MPG does **not** prescribe how the car engine must be built (it can be a hybrid, diesel, or turbocharged), the EEDI does **not** dictate *how* a ship should be efficient. The designer is free to use any combination of slow-steaming hulls, waste-heat recovery, LNG fuel, or wind-assist propulsion, provided the **final index value falls below the regulatory reference line** for that ship type and size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The EEDI is a **design index** (measured at the *keel-laying* stage on a hypothetical voyage) — it is **NOT** an operational index. The operational counterpart, **EEXI** (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index) and **CII** (Carbon Intensity Indicator), came later (2021/2023 amendments).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Reference speed (V_ref):** the contractual speed of the ship at **75% of Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR)** of the main engine in calm water.
- **Capacity units:** **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** for tankers, bulk carriers, container ships, gas carriers, and general cargo ships; **Gross Tonnage (GT)** for cruise/passenger ships and ro-ro cargo ships.
- **Emission factor (C_F):** a fuel-specific coefficient (e.g., **3.114 g CO₂/g fuel** for HFO, **2.750** for LNG).
- **CO₂ mass conversion factor:** **1 metric tonne = 1,000,000 g**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" Behind the EEDI

### 2.1.1 Genesis & Regulatory Need
The international shipping sector accounts for approximately **2–3% of global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions**. Without binding targets, projected emissions were forecast to grow **50–250% by 2050**. To break this trajectory, IMO member states adopted a **tiered approach** in 2011:

1. **Technical** → EEDI (new ships) and later EEXI (existing ships).
2. **Operational** → SEEMP (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan) and CII rating.
3. **Market-based** → as future instruments.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic of EEDI
The EEDI is built on the principle that **CO₂ from a ship ∝ Fuel consumed**. Combustion chemistry gives:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} \;=\; \sum_{i} m_{\text{fuel},i} \times C_{F,i}
$$

Where $C_{F,i}$ is the **carbon factor** of fuel $i$. Therefore, the index directly couples **fuel consumption, engine power, and transport work**.

## 2.2 The Five Power Contributors in the EEDI Numerator

The numerator of the EEDI accounts for **all energy consumers on board** during a hypothetical reference voyage:

| # | Power Term | Symbol | Description |
|---|-----------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Main engine power | $P_{ME}$ | Power output at 75% MCR |
| 2 | Auxiliary engine power | $P_{AE}$ | Diesel generators, shaft generators (motor mode) |
| 3 | Boiler / inert-gas generator fuel use | $P_{PTI}$ | Steam-driven systems |
| 4 | Mechanical / shaft propulsion assist | $P_{eff}$ | Shaft motors, energy recovery (subtracted if they reduce net fuel) |
| 5 | Innovative energy efficiency technology credit | $P_{IEE}$ | Wind, solar, waste-heat, etc. (with regulatory cap) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The simplified EEDI formula below is the **only form asked in KTU Module-4 examinations**. Memorise the symbols and units.

| Formula Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $\text{EEDI}$ | Energy Efficiency Design Index | $\text{g CO}_2 \div (\text{tonne} \cdot \text{nm})$ |
| $P_{ME}$ | Main engine output (75% MCR) | kW |
| $P_{AE}$ | Auxiliary engine power | kW |
| $C_{F,i}$ | Carbon emission factor of fuel $i$ | dimensionless (g CO₂ / g fuel) |
| $\text{SFC}_i$ | Specific fuel oil consumption | g / kWh |
| $f_i$ | Fuel mass correction (e.g., non-conventional) | dimensionless |
| $\text{DWT}$ | Deadweight tonnage (or GT) | tonnes |
| $V_{ref}$ | Reference ship speed at 75% MCR | knots (nm/h) |
| $a, b, c$ | Reference line parameters (per ship type) | tabulated by IMO |
| $f_w$ | Wave/weather factor (ice-class, etc.) | dimensionless |

### Master Formula (Conceptual Form Used in KTU)

$$
\text{EEDI} \;=\; \frac{\Big[\, f_i \,\times\, C_{F,i} \,\times\, \text{SFC}_{ME,i} \,\times\, P_{ME} \Big] \;+\; \Big[\, f_j \,\times\, C_{F,j} \,\times\, \text{SFC}_{AE,j} \,\times\, P_{AE} \Big] \;-\; \text{Efficiency Credits}}{f_w \,\times\, \text{DWT} \,\times\, V_{ref}}
$$

### Required (Regulatory) EEDI Reference Line

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} \;=\; \big(1 \,-\, \tfrac{X}{100}\big) \,\times\, a \,\times\, b^{-c}
$$

Where $X$ is the **reduction percentage** corresponding to the current phase:

| Phase | Period | Reduction $X$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Phase 0** | 2013 – 2014 | **0%** |
| **Phase 1** | 2015 – 2019 | **10%** |
| **Phase 2** | 2020 – 2024 | **20%** |
| **Phase 3** | 2025 onwards | **30%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pass Condition:** A new ship is *compliant* only if $\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \leq \text{EEDI}_{\text{required}}$. Otherwise, the vessel cannot receive its **International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For Naval Architects:** Drives optimisation of hull form (low-friction coatings), propeller efficiency (η-propeller), and engine selection (Tier II/III dual-fuel).
- **For Shipowners:** Influences capex decisions (LNG-ready vs. conventional HFO) and charter rate competitiveness.
- **For Classification Societies:** Provides a verifiable, auditable benchmark (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV all have EEDI-verification services).
- **For KTU Students:** Forms the **single most-tested topic** in Module 4 of Marine Pollution (typical 7–14 mark questions).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Conceptual EEDI Formula

We derive the EEDI from first principles by tracing the **energy flow** from the bunker tank to the propeller.

### Step 1 — Fuel-to-CO₂ Conversion
For any fuel $i$ combusted completely, mass conservation of carbon yields:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,i} \;=\; m_{\text{fuel},i} \times C_{F,i}
$$

where $C_{F,i}$ is the **CO₂ mass emission factor** (IMO MEPC.364(79) values):

$$
C_{F,\text{HFO}} = 3.114, \quad C_{F,\text{MGO}} = 3.206, \quad C_{F,\text{LNG}} = 2.750
$$

### Step 2 — Fuel Consumption via SFC and Power
The mass of fuel consumed by an engine per unit time is:

$$
m_{\text{fuel},i} \;=\; \text{SFC}_i \,\times\, P_i
$$

where $\text{SFC}_i$ is in **g/kWh** and $P_i$ is the **engine power output** in kW.

### Step 3 — Substituting into CO₂ Mass

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,i} \;=\; C_{F,i} \,\times\, \text{SFC}_i \,\times\, P_i
$$

### Step 4 — Adding All Power Contributors
The total CO₂ emitted during the reference voyage aggregates over main engine, auxiliaries, and boilers, while subtracting *useful* electrical/mechanical recovery:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=\text{ME}} C_{F,i} \text{SFC}_{ME,i} P_{ME} \;+\; \sum_{j=\text{AE}} C_{F,j} \text{SFC}_{AE,j} P_{AE} \;-\; \text{Credits}
$$

### Step 5 — Transport Work (Denominator)
The **useful transport work** performed by the ship is:

$$
W_{\text{transport}} \;=\; f_w \,\times\, \text{DWT} \,\times\, V_{ref}
$$

where $f_w$ is the dimensionless **correction factor** (e.g., for ice-class vessels or structural enhancement). $V_{ref}$ is in knots → nautical miles per hour.

### Step 6 — Combining Numerator and Denominator
By definition, the **attained EEDI** is the CO₂ mass divided by transport work:

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{total}}}{W_{\text{transport}}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_i C_{F,i}\,\text{SFC}_{ME,i}\,P_{ME} \;+\; \sum_j C_{F,j}\,\text{SFC}_{AE,j}\,P_{AE} \;-\; \text{Credits}}{f_w \,\times\, \text{DWT} \,\times\, V_{ref}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check:** $\frac{\text{g/kWh} \times \text{g/g} \times \text{kW}}{\text{tonne} \times \text{nm/h}}$ simplifies to **g CO₂ per tonne-nautical mile** — the regulatory SI unit. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Exam Style)

**Problem:** A new bulk carrier is being designed with the following parameters:

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| Main engine power (75% MCR), $P_{ME}$ | 12,000 kW |
| Auxiliary engine power, $P_{AE}$ | 800 kW |
| SFC of ME (HFO) | 175 g/kWh |
| SFC of AE (diesel) | 210 g/kWh |
| $C_F$ for HFO | 3.114 |
| $C_F$ for diesel | 3.206 |
| DWT | 80,000 tonnes |
| Reference speed, $V_{ref}$ | 14 knots |
| $f_w$ | 1.0 |

Compute $\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}}$.

### Solution — Stepwise

**Step (i):** Main engine CO₂ emission rate.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,ME} \;=\; 3.114 \,\times\, 175 \,\times\, 12{,}000
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,ME} \;=\; 3.114 \,\times\, 2{,}100{,}000
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,ME} \;=\; 6{,}539{,}400 \;\text{g/h} \;=\; 6.5394 \;\text{t/h}
$$

**Step (ii):** Auxiliary engine CO₂ emission rate.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,AE} \;=\; 3.206 \,\times\, 210 \,\times\, 800
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,AE} \;=\; 3.206 \,\times\, 168{,}000
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,AE} \;=\; 538{,}608 \;\text{g/h} \;=\; 0.5386 \;\text{t/h}
$$

**Step (iii):** Total CO₂ emission rate.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{total}} \;=\; 6{,}539{,}400 \;+\; 538{,}608 \;=\; 7{,}078{,}008 \;\text{g/h}
$$

**Step (iv):** Transport work per hour (attained speed = V_ref).

$$
W_{\text{transport}} \;=\; 1.0 \,\times\, 80{,}000 \,\times\, 14 \;=\; 1{,}120{,}000 \;\text{tonne·nm/h}
$$

**Step (v):** Compute EEDI.

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \;=\; \frac{7{,}078{,}008}{1{,}120{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \;\approx\; 6.32 \;\text{g CO}_2 \div (\text{tonne·nm})}
$$

**Step (vi):** Compliance check (Phase 1, 2015–2019, bulk carrier 80,000 DWT).
For a bulk carrier of this size, the IMO reference value $a \cdot b^{-c}$ is approximately **9.5 g CO₂/tonne·nm**. Applying the 10% Phase-1 reduction:

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} \;=\; (1 - 0.10) \,\times\, 9.5 \;=\; 8.55 \;\text{g CO}_2 \div (\text{tonne·nm})
$$

Since $\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} = 6.32 < 8.55 = \text{EEDI}_{\text{required}}$, the design is **COMPLIANT**. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an EEDI Reduction Strategy

| Strategy | EEDI Impact | Cost Implication | KTU Exam Likelihood |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Hull form optimisation (slenderness) | **−5 to −10%** | Low | ★★★★ |
| Slow-steaming + larger propeller | **−10 to −15%** | Low | ★★★★★ |
| Waste Heat Recovery System (WHRS) | **−5 to −8%** | Medium | ★★★ |
| Dual-fuel LNG main engine | **−15 to −25%** | High | ★★★★★ |
| Air-lubrication / hull coatings | **−2 to −5%** | Medium | ★★ |
| Wind-assist (rotor sails, kites) | **−5 to −15%** | Medium-High | ★★★ |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI) as per IMO regulations. State the units in which it is expressed.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** is a regulatory metric introduced by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **MARPOL Annex VI, Chapter 4** (Resolution MEPC.245(66)). It is a **non-prescriptive, performance-based index** that quantifies the grams of **CO₂ emitted per tonne-nautical mile** of transport work for new ships of 400 GT and above. It is calculated at the design stage and measures the design efficiency of a vessel relative to a baseline reference line.

> **Units:** $\text{EEDI} = \dfrac{\text{g CO}_2}{\text{tonne} \cdot \text{nm}}$

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark units, 1 mark scope and authority.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the different reduction phases of EEDI with their corresponding target reductions and time periods.*

**Model Answer:**
| Phase | Period | Reduction Target |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Phase 0** | 2013 – 2014 | **0%** (baseline) |
| **Phase 1** | 2015 – 2019 | **10%** |
| **Phase 2** | 2020 – 2024 | **20%** |
| **Phase 3** | 2025 onwards | **30%** |

The reduction factor $(1 - X/100)$ is multiplied with the IMO reference-line value to obtain $\text{EEDI}_{\text{required}}$ for that phase.

*(3 marks: 1 mark table structure, 2 marks for correct values.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the conceptual EEDI formula for a ship using main and auxiliary engines, explaining each term. Comment on how the reference speed and deadweight tonnage influence the attained EEDI. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the various phases of EEDI reduction and the regulatory consequence if a new ship fails the EEDI compliance test. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation *(7 marks)*

**Step 1:** Combustion chemistry gives the CO₂ mass emitted per kg of fuel $i$:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,i} \;=\; m_{\text{fuel},i} \,\times\, C_{F,i}
$$

**Step 2:** Fuel flow is determined by **specific fuel consumption** and **engine power**:

$$
m_{\text{fuel},i} \;=\; \text{SFC}_i \,\times\, P_i \quad \text{[g/kWh × kW = g/h]}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,i} \;=\; C_{F,i} \,\times\, \text{SFC}_i \,\times\, P_i
$$

**Step 4:** Summing all consumers (Main Engine ME, Auxiliary Engine AE) and subtracting innovative technology credits:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{total}} \;=\; C_{F,\text{ME}} \,\text{SFC}_{ME}\,P_{ME} \;+\; C_{F,\text{AE}} \,\text{SFC}_{AE}\,P_{AE} \;-\; \text{Credits}
$$

**Step 5:** The denominator is **transport work**:

$$
W_{\text{transport}} \;=\; f_w \,\times\, \text{DWT} \,\times\, V_{ref}
$$

**Step 6:** Final EEDI formula:

$$
\boxed{\text{EEDI} \;=\; \frac{C_{F,\text{ME}}\,\text{SFC}_{ME}\,P_{ME} \;+\; C_{F,\text{AE}}\,\text{SFC}_{AE}\,P_{AE} \;-\; \text{Credits}}{f_w \,\times\, \text{DWT} \,\times\, V_{ref}}}
$$

**Influence of parameters:**
- **Higher $V_{ref}$:** Increases denominator → lowers EEDI (apparent improvement), but simultaneously raises $P_{ME}$ (cubically related to speed) → numerator rises faster. The **net effect is EEDI worsens with speed**, encouraging slower designs.
- **Higher DWT:** Linear increase in denominator with no change to numerator → **EEDI improves** for larger ships (explains why EEDI favours scale).

*(7 marks: 2 derivation, 2 formula writing, 2 parameter analysis, 1 conclusion.)*

#### Part (b) — Reduction Phases & Regulatory Consequences *(7 marks)*

The IMO adopted a **stepwise tightening** of the reference line:

| Phase | Period | Reduction $X$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Phase 0 | 2013–2014 | 0% |
| Phase 1 | 2015–2019 | 10% |
| Phase 2 | 2020–2024 | 20% |
| Phase 3 | 2025+ | 30% |

The **attained EEDI** is calculated for each new ship design. The **required EEDI** is:

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} \;=\; \big(1 - \tfrac{X}{100}\big) \cdot a \cdot b^{-c}
$$

**Regulatory consequence of failure:**
If $\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} > \text{EEDI}_{\text{required}}$, the **Classification Society** cannot issue the **International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate** under MARPOL Annex VI. Without the IAPP certificate, the ship **legally cannot** engage in international voyages. Owners must either:
1. **Redesign** (hull, engine, fuel, or propulsion),
2. **De-rate** the main engine, or
3. Install **innovative energy-efficiency technology** (subject to IMO cap).

*(7 marks: 3 marks table, 2 marks required formula, 2 marks IAPP consequences.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the role of $C_F$ (carbon emission factor) and SFC in the EEDI calculation. Compare the $C_F$ values of HFO, diesel, and LNG fuels, and discuss how fuel choice affects EEDI. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A new container ship is being designed. Given $P_{ME} = 25{,}000$ kW (at 75% MCR), $P_{AE} = 1{,}200$ kW, $\text{SFC}_{ME} = 168$ g/kWh (HFO), $\text{SFC}_{AE} = 195$ g/kWh (diesel), $\text{DWT} = 120{,}000$ t, $V_{ref} = 20$ knots, $f_w = 1.0$. Calculate the attained EEDI. State, with justification, whether the ship passes Phase-2 compliance if the required EEDI is **5.8 g CO₂/tonne·nm**. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Role of $C_F$ and SFC *(7 marks)*

**$C_F$ (Carbon Emission Factor):** A dimensionless multiplier that converts **grams of fuel burned** into **grams of CO₂ emitted**, based on the **carbon-to-CO₂ stoichiometric ratio** of each fuel.

| Fuel | $C_F$ (g CO₂ / g fuel) |
|:---|:---:|
| **HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil)** | **3.114** |
| **MGO / Diesel** | **3.206** |
| **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas)** | **2.750** |

- **LNG has the lowest $C_F$** because methane has the highest H:C ratio → less carbon per unit energy.
- **Diesel has the highest $C_F$** due to its dense hydrocarbon structure.

**SFC (Specific Fuel Consumption):** The mass of fuel an engine consumes **per kWh of useful work output**, expressed in g/kWh. A lower SFC indicates better thermodynamic efficiency.

**Combined effect on EEDI:**
$$
\text{EEDI} \;\propto\; C_F \,\times\, \text{SFC} \,\times\, P
$$

Switching from HFO ($C_F = 3.114$) to LNG ($C_F = 2.750$) reduces the numerator by approximately **12%** per kWh of mechanical work — a major EEDI reduction lever, *in addition* to the typically lower SFC of modern LNG dual-fuel engines.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for C_F and SFC, 2 marks table comparison, 1 mark combined-effect reasoning.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical EEDI Calculation *(7 marks)*

**Step 1:** Main engine CO₂ emission rate.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,ME} \;=\; 3.114 \,\times\, 168 \,\times\, 25{,}000
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,ME} \;=\; 3.114 \,\times\, 4{,}200{,}000
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,ME} \;=\; 13{,}078{,}800 \;\text{g/h}
$$

**Step 2:** Auxiliary engine CO₂ emission rate.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,AE} \;=\; 3.206 \,\times\, 195 \,\times\, 1{,}200
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,AE} \;=\; 3.206 \,\times\, 234{,}000
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,AE} \;=\; 750{,}204 \;\text{g/h}
$$

**Step 3:** Total CO₂ emission rate.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{total}} \;=\; 13{,}078{,}800 \;+\; 750{,}204 \;=\; 13{,}829{,}004 \;\text{g/h}
$$

**Step 4:** Transport work per hour.

$$
W_{\text{transport}} \;=\; 1.0 \,\times\, 120{,}000 \,\times\, 20 \;=\; 2{,}400{,}000 \;\text{tonne·nm/h}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute attained EEDI.

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \;=\; \frac{13{,}829{,}004}{2{,}400{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \;\approx\; 5.76 \;\text{g CO}_2 \div (\text{tonne·nm})}
$$

**Step 6:** Compliance check.
Required EEDI = **5.8 g CO₂/tonne·nm**.
Since $5.76 < 5.80$, the ship **PASSES** Phase-2 compliance. The design margin is **0.04 g CO₂/tonne·nm** (~0.7% safety margin).

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per step for first four steps, 1 mark for division, 1 mark compliance logic, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion** — most students write $P_{ME}$ in MW instead of kW, leading to answers off by a factor of 1000. The examiner *will* award zero if units are inconsistent.
> 2. **Mixing DWT and GT** — passenger/cruise ships use GT, all others use DWT. Wrong choice = 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the reduction phase** — many students compute $\text{EIDI}_{\text{attained}}$ but never compare it with $\text{EIDI}_{\text{required}}$. Always close the loop with a **PASS / FAIL** statement.
> 4. **Using $V_{ref}$ as instantaneous speed in different time units** — $V_{ref}$ must be in knots (nm/h). If the problem gives speed in m/s, convert before substituting.
> 5. **Ignoring $f_w$ for ice-class or restricted-service vessels** — examiners explicitly test the $f_w$ term in 14-mark numerical questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Pass — Memorise This List Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **EEDI full form:** Energy Efficiency Design Index — a **design-time** (not operational) metric.
- [ ] **Governing body:** IMO, under **MARPOL Annex VI, Chapter 4**.
- [ ] **Key Resolution:** MEPC.245(66), as amended by MEPC.263(69) and MEPC.308(73).
- [ ] **Applies to:** All newbuild ships **≥ 400 GT** engaged in international trade.
- [ ] **Units:** **grams CO₂ per tonne-nautical mile** = g CO₂ / (t·nm).
- [ ] **Numerator:** Sum of $C_F \times \text{SFC} \times P$ for ME, AE, boilers, PTI, less innovation credits.
- [ ] **Denominator:** $f_w \times \text{Capacity} \times V_{ref}$ where Capacity = DWT (cargo) or GT (passenger).
- [ ] **Reference speed $V_{ref}$:** Contract speed at **75% MCR** in calm water, in knots.
- [ ] **Reference line:** $\text{EIDI}_{\text{required}} = (1 - X/100) \cdot a \cdot b^{-c}$.
- [ ] **Reduction phases:** **0% → 10% → 20% → 30%** across 2013 → 2025+.
- [ ] **Pass condition:** $\text{EIDI}_{\text{attained}} \leq \text{EIDI}_{\text{required}}$.
- [ ] **Failure consequence:** **IAPP Certificate denied** → ship cannot trade internationally.
- [ ] **$C_F$ values:** HFO = 3.114, Diesel/MGO = 3.206, **LNG = 2.750** (lowest → most favoured).
- [ ] **Innovation credit cap:** IMO allows up to **30% credit** for energy-recovery technologies.
- [ ] **EEDI is different from EEXI** (which applies to *existing* ships from 2023 onwards) and **CII** (operational rating).
- [ ] **SEEMP Part I & II** are mandatory companion documents to EEDI certification.
- [ ] **Reduction levers ranked:** (i) Speed reduction, (ii) Hull optimisation, (iii) Engine de-rating, (iv) Fuel switch to LNG, (v) Waste-heat recovery, (vi) Wind-assist.

---
