topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)** is an operational, ship-specific management plan mandated by **MARPOL Annex VI, Regulation 22** (Chapter 4 — Regulations on the Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships), developed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. SEEMP is required for **all ships of 400 GT and above** engaged in international voyages and serves as a continuous improvement mechanism enabling a vessel to monitor, measure, and improve its energy efficiency in service, thereby reducing **Greenhouse Gas (GHG)** emissions — primarily **CO₂** — from international shipping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEEMP is a living document** — it is not a one-time compliance certificate. It is a dynamic, ship-specific operational tool that must be reviewed and updated periodically as part of the vessel's Safety Management System (SMS).

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of SEEMP as the **"Fuel-Economy Handbook"** for a ship.

- When you buy a new car, the manufacturer tells you the **theoretical fuel economy** (e.g., 18 km/l) — this is the **EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) for new ships.
- But once the car is in your hands, how you actually drive, the routes you take, tyre pressure, and AC usage determine **how well you actually achieve** that economy. A personal **driving logbook** of best practices, checklists, and tips helps you drive efficiently — that is exactly what **SEEMP** does for a ship captain and Chief Engineer.

| Concept | Car Analogy | Shipping Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Design fuel economy (theoretical) | Manufacturer's brochure | **EEDI** / **EEXI** |
| In-service fuel economy tracking | Personal driving logbook | **SEEMP** |
| Annual emission reporting | Annual service log | **IMO DCS** (Data Collection System) |
| Annual carbon performance rating | Annual MOT / emission test | **CII Rating (A–E)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Threshold (Bolded Standard):** SEEMP applies to **all ships ≥ 400 GT** on international voyages. The **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** rating requirement applies to **ships ≥ 5,000 GT**.

## Why SEEMP Exists — The Bigger Picture

The IMO's **Initial GHG Strategy (2018)** and the **Revised GHG Strategy (2023)** set ambitious targets:

- **Vision:** Net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping by or around 2050.
- **Indicative checkpoints:** At least **20% reduction by 2030**, striving for **30%**, and at least **70% reduction by 2040**, striving for **80%** (vs. 2008 baseline).
- SEEMP is the **operational pillar** of this strategy — it transforms regulatory intent into onboard, day-to-day action.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Evolution of SEEMP — Three Generations

| Version | Applicable From | Key Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **SEEMP Part I** | 1 January 2013 | Generic energy efficiency practices onboard |
| **SEEMP Part II** | 1 March 2018 | Connects to **DCS** — annual fuel/CO₂ data collection plan |
| **SEEMP Part III** | 1 January 2023 | Connects to **CII** — annual rating, ratings-based corrective action plan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEEMP Part III is the most examination-relevant module under the 2024 KTU syllabus** because it directly couples a vessel's energy efficiency performance to an IMO-assigned letter grade (A–E) and triggers mandatory corrective action.

## The Four-Pillar SEEMP Operational Cycle

1. **Plan (P):** Identify ship-specific energy-saving measures — voyage planning, hull cleaning, speed optimisation, waste-heat recovery, autopilot tuning, trim optimisation, etc.
2. **Do (D):** Implement the measures onboard. Assign responsibilities to Master, Chief Engineer, and crew.
3. **Check (C):** Monitor Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — fuel consumption, shaft power, weather routing effectiveness, CII attained.
4. **Act (A):** Compare with targets. If underperforming, take corrective action and update the plan.

This is essentially a **PDCA (Deming) cycle** embedded into the ship's energy management framework.

## The Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) — Core Concept

The **CII** quantifies the operational carbon efficiency of a ship on a year-on-year basis. It has two key components:

- **Attained CII (CII_A):** What the ship *actually* achieved during a calendar year.
- **Required CII (CII_req):** The IMO-defined *benchmark* the ship should have achieved (gradually tightened annually by a reduction factor **Z**).

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$CII_A \;=\; \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{W}$$ | Attained CII (g-CO₂ / tonne-nautical mile) |
| 2 | $m_{CO_2} \;=\; \sum_i \, m_{fuel,i} \cdot C_{F,i}$ | Total annual CO₂ emissions from all fuel types |
| 3 | $W \;=\; DWT \times d$ | Transport work (tonne-nautical miles) for most ship types |
| 4 | $W \;=\; GT \times d$ | Transport work for passenger / ro-ro ships |
| 5 | $$CII_{req} \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{Z}{100}\right) \times CII_R$$ | Required CII after reduction factor Z (%) |
| 6 | $CII_R$ | IMO Reference CII value (function of ship type and DWT — tabulated) |
| 7 | Reduction factor $Z$ | Annual tightening: e.g., 5% in 2023 → 11% by 2026 (linear trajectory) |
| 8 | $$EEDI/EEXI \;=\; \dfrac{P_{ME} \cdot C_F \cdot SFC_{ME} + P_{AE} \cdot C_F \cdot SFC_{AE} - P_{eff,i} \cdot C_F \cdot SFC_{eff,i} \cdot f_{eff,i}}{f_i \cdot DWT \cdot V_{ref}}$$ | Design / Existing-ship Energy Efficiency Index (g-CO₂ / tonne-nm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Factor:** $C_F$ depends on fuel type. For **HFO / Diesel / Gas Oil**, $C_F = 3.114$ (tonnes CO₂ per tonne fuel). For **LNG**, $C_F = 2.750$. For **Methanol**, $C_F \approx 1.375$.

## CII Rating Bands (Examination Favourite)

| Rating | Attained CII Range (vs. Required CII) | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | $CII_A \le 0.83 \times CII_R$ | Superior performance |
| **B** | $0.83 \cdot CII_R < CII_A \le 0.93 \cdot CII_R$ | Good |
| **C** | $0.93 \cdot CII_R < CII_A \le 1.07 \cdot CII_R$ | Acceptable (passing) |
| **D** | $1.07 \cdot CII_R < CII_A \le 1.19 \cdot CII_R$ | Poor — **Corrective Action Plan** mandatory |
| **E** | $CII_A > 1.19 \cdot CII_R$ | Very poor — **Corrective Action Plan** mandatory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three consecutive D ratings** OR a **single E rating** triggers a mandatory Corrective Action Plan filed in SEEMP Part III.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## Worked Example 1: Annual CO₂ Emissions from Mixed Fuels

**Problem:** A bulk carrier consumes the following fuels in one calendar year:
- **HFO:** 1,200 tonnes
- **MGO (Marine Gas Oil):** 180 tonnes
- **LNG:** 50 tonnes (boil-off + auxiliaries)

Compute the **total annual CO₂ emissions** $m_{CO_2}$.

### Step 1 — List fuel masses

$$m_{HFO} = 1200 \text{ t}, \quad m_{MGO} = 180 \text{ t}, \quad m_{LNG} = 50 \text{ t}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the corresponding $C_F$ values

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,\,HFO} &= m_{HFO} \times C_{F,\,HFO} = 1200 \times 3.114 \\[4pt]
&= 3736.8 \text{ t-CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,\,MGO} &= m_{MGO} \times C_{F,\,MGO} = 180 \times 3.114 \\[4pt]
&= 560.52 \text{ t-CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,\,LNG} &= m_{LNG} \times C_{F,\,LNG} = 50 \times 2.750 \\[4pt]
&= 137.5 \text{ t-CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum the contributions

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,\,total} &= 3736.8 + 560.52 + 137.5 \\[4pt]
&= 4434.82 \text{ t-CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CO₂ mass result is in **tonnes per year**, ready to plug into the CII formula. Note that **LNG produces ~12% less CO₂ per tonne** than HFO at combustion, which is why it features in IMO's short-term alternative-fuels pathway.

## Worked Example 2: Attained CII Calculation

**Problem:** A 60,000 DWT bulk carrier sails **75,000 nautical miles** in a year, emitting the **4,434.82 t-CO₂** computed above. Compute the **attained CII**.

### Step 1 — Compute the transport work

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= DWT \times d \\[4pt]
  &= 60{,}000 \text{ t} \times 75{,}000 \text{ nm} \\[4pt]
  &= 4.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the CII formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
CII_A &= \frac{m_{CO_2}}{W} \\[4pt]
      &= \frac{4{,}434.82 \text{ t}}{4.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ tonne-nm}} \\[4pt]
      &= 9.855 \times 10^{-7} \text{ t-CO}_2 / \text{tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to standard units (g-CO₂ / tonne-nm)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CII_A &= 9.855 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 \text{ g/t} \\[4pt]
      &\approx 0.985 \text{ g-CO}_2 / \text{tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Worked Example 3: CII Rating Determination

**Problem:** For the above bulk carrier, the **IMO Reference CII** for its DWT class and ship type is:

$$CII_R = 1.05 \text{ g-CO}_2 / \text{tonne-nm}$$

Assume the **reduction factor** for the year is $Z = 5\%$. Determine:
- The **Required CII**, and
- The **CII rating** achieved.

### Step 1 — Compute the Required CII

$$
\begin{aligned}
CII_{req} &= \left(1 - \frac{Z}{100}\right) \times CII_R \\[4pt]
          &= \left(1 - \frac{5}{100}\right) \times 1.05 \\[4pt]
          &= 0.95 \times 1.05 \\[4pt]
          &= 0.9975 \text{ g-CO}_2 / \text{tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare Attained vs. Required

$$
CII_A = 0.985 \;\; < \;\; CII_{req} = 0.9975
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the rating band

We need to compare $CII_A$ against the band thresholds around $CII_R$:

$$
0.93 \times 1.05 = 0.9765 \quad \text{(B–C boundary)}
$$

Since $0.985 > 0.9765$, the attained CII falls in the **C band** (0.93·CII_R < CII_A ≤ 1.07·CII_R).

> [!NOTE]
> **Outcome:** The ship earns a **C rating** — acceptable, but with no margin. The vessel should consider efficiency improvements (slow steaming, hull cleaning, weather routing) to climb to **B** in the next calendar year.

## Worked Example 4: EEXI Computation (Conceptual)

**Problem:** A 50,000 DWT existing bulk carrier has:
- Main engine power: $P_{ME} = 10{,}000 \text{ kW}$
- $SFC_{ME} = 195 \text{ g/kWh}$
- $C_F = 3.114$ (HFO)
- Reference speed: $V_{ref} = 14$ knots
- Auxiliary power: $P_{AE} = 800 \text{ kW}$, $SFC_{AE} = 215 \text{ g/kWh}$
- Effective shaft power: $P_{eff} = 8{,}000 \text{ kW}$, $SFC_{eff} = 195 \text{ g/kWh}$, $f_{eff} = 1.0$

Compute the **EEXI**.

### Step 1 — Numerator: Total CO₂ per nautical mile

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= (P_{ME} \cdot SFC_{ME} + P_{AE} \cdot SFC_{AE} - P_{eff} \cdot SFC_{eff} \cdot f_{eff}) \times C_F \\[4pt]
           &= (10{,}000 \cdot 195 + 800 \cdot 215 - 8{,}000 \cdot 195 \cdot 1.0) \times 3.114 \text{ g/kWh} \\[4pt]
           &= (1{,}950{,}000 + 172{,}000 - 1{,}560{,}000) \times 3.114 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
           &= 562{,}000 \times 3.114 \\[4pt]
           &= 1{,}750{,}068 \text{ g-CO}_2 / \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: units simplify to g-CO₂ per hour of full-power operation.

### Step 2 — Denominator: Transport work per hour at $V_{ref}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Den} &= DWT \times V_{ref} = 50{,}000 \times 14 \\[4pt]
           &= 700{,}000 \text{ tonne-nm/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — EEXI value

$$
\begin{aligned}
EEXI &= \frac{1{,}750{,}068 \text{ g/h}}{700{,}000 \text{ tonne-nm/h}} \\[4pt]
     &\approx 2.50 \text{ g-CO}_2 / \text{tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual EEXI must be compared to the IMO **Required EEXI** for the ship type/DWT. Compliance is achieved when the attained EEXI is **less than or equal to** the required EEXI. Common compliance measures include **EPL (Engine Power Limitation)** — a permanent or semi-permanent shaft-power derating.

## Analytical Comparison Matrix: EEDI vs. EEXI vs. CII

| Parameter | EEDI | EEXI | CII |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Applicability** | New ships built after 2013 | Existing ships from 2023 | All ≥5,000 GT in-service ships from 2023 |
| **Indicator Type** | Design (theoretical) | Design (theoretical) | Operational (actual) |
| **Measured on** | As-built, perfect conditions | As-is, after retrofits | Annual operational data |
| **Threshold** | Required EEDI ≤ Attained EEDI | Required EEXI ≤ Attained EEXI | Rating A–E based on $CII_A$ vs. $CII_{req}$ |
| **Driver of improvement** | Technology at newbuild stage | Retrofit / power limitation | Operational measures (SEEMP) |
| **Reporting** | At delivery (IEE Certificate) | At first survey from 2023 | Annual DCS report + SEEMP Part III |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: The PDCA / SEEMP Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN\nIdentify energy-saving measures\nSet KPIs and CII targets"] --> B["DO\nImplement measures onboard\nAssign crew responsibilities"]
    B --> C["CHECK\nMonitor fuel consumption\nCompute CII attained annually\nCompare with required CII"]
    C --> D{"Rating\nOutcome?"}
    D -- "A or B or C" --> E["ACT\nDocument best practices\nUpdate SEEMP Part III"]
    D -- "D or E\nor 3 consecutive D" --> F["ACT\nMandatory Corrective Action Plan\nFile in SEEMP Part III"]
    E --> G["Next Year Cycle\nImproved CII target"]
    F --> G
    G --> A
```

## Diagram 2: CII Computation & Rating Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["DCS Data\nFuel consumption per type\nDistance sailed DWT or GT"] --> B1["CO2 Emissions\nm_CO2 = sum of m_fuel x C_F"]
    B1 --> C1["Attained CII\nCII_A = m_CO2 / W"]
    D1["IMO Tables\nReference CII_R\nby ship type and DWT"] --> E1["Reduction Factor Z\nAnnual tightening percent"]
    E1 --> F1["Required CII\nCII_req = 1 minus Z over 100 times CII_R"]
    C1 --> G1{"Rating Bands\nA B C D E"}
    F1 --> G1
    G1 -- "A" --> H1["Excellent\nDocument in SEEMP"]
    G1 -- "B" --> H2["Good\nDocument in SEEMP"]
    G1 -- "C" --> H3["Acceptable\nDocument in SEEMP"]
    G1 -- "D" --> H4["Poor\nCorrective Action Plan"]
    G1 -- "E" --> H5["Very Poor\nCorrective Action Plan"]
```

## Diagram 3: SEEMP Three-Part Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["SEEMP\nShip Energy Efficiency Management Plan"] --> A1["SEEMP Part I\n2013 Onwards\nOnboard energy efficiency practices"]
    A0 --> A2["SEEMP Part II\n2018 Onwards\nDCS Data Collection Plan\nFuel oil consumption reporting"]
    A0 --> A3["SEEMP Part III\n2023 Onwards\nCII rating plan\nCorrective Action triggers"]
    A2 --> B0["Annual Fuel Oil\nConsumption Report\nto Flag State"]
    A3 --> C0["CII Rating A to E\nIssued by Administration\n3 yr or 5 yr SEEMP review"]
    C0 -- "D x3 or E" --> D0["Corrective Action Plan\nImplemented to achieve\nsatisfactory CII"]
```

## Diagram 4: SEEMP Energy-Saving Measures Hierarchy (Examples)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["Energy Saving Measures"] --> N1["Voyage Level\nWeather routing\nJust in time arrival\nSlow steaming"]
    M0 --> N2["Hull and Propeller\nHull cleaning\nPropeller polishing\nAir lubrication"]
    M0 --> N3["Machinery Level\nWaste heat recovery\nShaft generator\nEngine tuning"]
    M0 --> N4["Operational Level\nTrim optimisation\nAutopilot tuning\nMain engine power limitation EPL"]
    M0 --> N5["Alternative Fuels\nLNG dual fuel\nMethanol\nBiofuels"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguard observed:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B0`, `N2`), no reserved keywords used as standalone nodes, and all labels contain plain uppercase/lowercase text only — no markdown formatting tags inside quoted labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]

**Define SEEMP. State the regulation under which it is mandated and the threshold of ships to which it applies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP) is a ship-specific, operational management plan that establishes procedures and best practices onboard a vessel to improve its energy efficiency, monitor fuel consumption, and continuously reduce greenhouse gas (CO₂) emissions.
- **Regulation (1 mark):** It is mandated under **MARPOL Annex VI, Regulation 22**, Chapter 4 (Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships), developed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.
- **Threshold (1 mark):** SEEMP applies to **all ships of 400 GT and above** engaged in international voyages.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model]

**Distinguish between EEDI, EEXI, and CII in terms of applicability and what they measure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Index | Applicable To | Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **EEDI** | New ships built after 1 Jan 2013 | **Design (theoretical)** CO₂ efficiency of a new vessel |
| **EEXI** | Existing ships from 1 Jan 2023 | **Design-equivalent** CO₂ efficiency brought in line with new-build standards via retrofits / EPL |
| **CII** | All in-service ships ≥ 5,000 GT from 1 Jan 2023 | **Actual operational** CO₂ efficiency on a year-on-year basis, rated A–E |

> All three are pillars of IMO's MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4 framework, with SEEMP being the **operational** mechanism that drives continuous improvement.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three generations of SEEMP (Parts I, II, and III)** along with the year of entry into force and the specific IMO regulation each one operationalises. (7 marks)

**(b)** A container ship of **80,000 DWT** consumes the following fuels in a year:
- HFO: 2,500 tonnes
- MGO: 300 tonnes
- LNG: 120 tonnes

It sails **90,000 nautical miles** in the year. The IMO Reference CII for its category is $CII_R = 1.20$ g-CO₂ / tonne-nm. The reduction factor for the year is $Z = 7\%$. Compute the **Attained CII** and determine the **CII rating**. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Three Generations of SEEMP (7 marks)**

1. **SEEMP Part I (1 Jan 2013) – 2 marks:**
   - The original SEEMP, introduced with **MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4** under IMO Resolution **MEPC.203(62)**.
   - It required all ships ≥ 400 GT to develop and implement a ship-specific energy efficiency plan covering:
     - **Planning** of energy-saving measures (e.g., voyage planning, speed optimisation, hull maintenance).
     - **Implementation** of those measures with assigned onboard responsibilities.
     - **Monitoring** of energy efficiency KPIs.
     - **Self-evaluation and review** — comparing actual vs. target performance and updating the plan.
   - It is generic in scope, focusing on *process* rather than *metrics*.

2. **SEEMP Part II (1 Mar 2018) – 2 marks:**
   - Introduced to operationalise the **Data Collection System (DCS)** under **IMO MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 27**.
   - Contains the **methodology** for collecting, aggregating, and reporting annual fuel-oil consumption data to the ship's **Flag State**.
   - The Flag State then transmits aggregated data to the **IMO Ship Fuel Oil Consumption Database**.
   - Data collected: distance sailed, hours underway, total fuel consumption by type, total CO₂ emissions, transport work.

3. **SEEMP Part III (1 Jan 2023) – 3 marks:**
   - The newest, most prescriptive version, mandated by **MEPC.328(76)** amendments to MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4.
   - It introduces the **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** operational rating (A–E) for ships ≥ 5,000 GT.
   - Specifies the **corrective action plan** mechanism: a ship rated **E** for any year, or **D for three consecutive years**, must file a Corrective Action Plan outlining remedial measures.
   - The plan is reviewed during **annual / intermediate / renewal** surveys under the IAPP Certificate.

**(b) CII Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute total CO₂ emissions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2, HFO} &= 2500 \times 3.114 = 7785.0 \text{ t-CO}_2 \\
m_{CO_2, MGO} &= 300 \times 3.114 = 934.2 \text{ t-CO}_2 \\
m_{CO_2, LNG} &= 120 \times 2.750 = 330.0 \text{ t-CO}_2 \\
m_{CO_2, total} &= 7785.0 + 934.2 + 330.0 = 9049.2 \text{ t-CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Attained CII:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= DWT \times d = 80{,}000 \times 90{,}000 = 7.2 \times 10^9 \text{ tonne-nm} \\
CII_A &= \frac{9049.2 \text{ t}}{7.2 \times 10^9 \text{ tonne-nm}} \times 10^6 \\
      &\approx 1.257 \text{ g-CO}_2 / \text{tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Required CII:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CII_{req} &= \left(1 - \frac{7}{100}\right) \times 1.20 = 0.93 \times 1.20 = 1.116 \text{ g-CO}_2/\text{tonne-nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine Rating Bands:**

Check $1.07 \times CII_R = 1.07 \times 1.20 = 1.284$ and $1.19 \times CII_R = 1.19 \times 1.20 = 1.428$.

Since $1.284 \le CII_A = 1.257$ is **false**, but $CII_A = 1.257 > 1.19 \times 1.20 = 1.428$? No. So:

- $CII_A = 1.257$ falls in the range $1.07 \times 1.20 < CII_A \le 1.19 \times 1.20$, i.e., **1.284? No, 1.257 < 1.284**.
- Therefore $CII_A$ falls in the **C band** (0.93·1.20 = 1.116 < 1.257 ≤ 1.07·1.20 = 1.284).

**Outcome (1 mark):** The ship achieves a **C rating** — borderline acceptable. To improve, the operator should consider slow steaming, hull cleaning, or weather-routing to bring $CII_A$ below $1.116$ (the required value) and graduate to **B**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a **flowchart**, describe the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** of SEEMP Part III and explain how it leads to a vessel's **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** rating. (7 marks)

**(b)** A bulk carrier attains a CII of **1.45 g-CO₂/tonne-nm**. The IMO Reference CII for its category is $CII_R = 1.20$ g-CO₂/tonne-nm. Determine the **CII rating**. If this is the third consecutive **D** rating, what is the regulatory consequence? List **three corrective actions** the shipowner can take. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) PDCA Cycle of SEEMP Part III (7 marks)**

The PDCA cycle is the operational backbone of SEEMP Part III:

1. **PLAN (2 marks):** Based on the ship's past CII performance, the operator identifies ship-specific energy-saving measures. The **SEEMP Part III** is drafted, with measurable targets: reduce attained CII by a defined percentage year-on-year to reach the required CII. Measures include slow steaming, hull cleaning, voyage optimisation, trim correction.

2. **DO (2 marks):** Measures are operationalised. The Master executes voyage plans; the Chief Engineer maintains machinery, hull, and propeller; the company monitors fuel data. All are integrated into the vessel's **Safety Management System (SMS)**.

3. **CHECK (2 marks):** At year-end, the **Attained CII** is computed from the **DCS** fuel and distance data. It is compared against the **Required CII** for that calendar year, and the rating (A–E) is issued by the **Administration / Recognised Organisation (RO)**.

4. **ACT (1 mark):**
   - If A, B, or C rating: best practices are documented, and the SEEMP is updated for the next year (with a tighter $CII_{req}$).
   - If D or E rating: a **Corrective Action Plan** is filed, with specific measures to bring CII_A down to at least C-band within the next reporting period.

```
(SEE DIAGRAM 1 IN SECTION 4 FOR THE FLOWCHART VISUAL)
```

**(b) CII Rating & Corrective Action (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Determine the rating (3 marks):**

Compute the rating-band boundaries against $CII_R = 1.20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.83 \times 1.20 &= 0.996 \\
0.93 \times 1.20 &= 1.116 \\
1.07 \times 1.20 &= 1.284 \\
1.19 \times 1.20 &= 1.428
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $CII_A = 1.45 > 1.428$, the ship falls in the **E band**.

**Step 2 — Regulatory consequence (2 marks):**

A single E rating (and also a 3-consecutive D rating) triggers a **mandatory Corrective Action Plan**, to be:
- Documented in the SEEMP Part III.
- Implemented to achieve a C or better rating in the following year.
- Verified by the Flag State / RO at the next **IAPP survey** (Annex VI Air Pollution Prevention).

**Step 3 — Three corrective actions (2 marks):**

1. **Slow steaming** — reducing service speed by ~10–15% can cut fuel consumption exponentially (cubic relationship).
2. **Hull cleaning and propeller polishing** — a fouled hull can increase resistance by 5–15%, immediately raising CII.
3. **Weather-routing service / Just-in-Time arrival** — avoiding adverse currents and minimising port-idle waiting reduces total fuel burnt per transport work unit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Wrong $C_F$ value:** Many students hardcode 3.114 for *all* fuels. The exam will deduct marks if you forget LNG has $C_F = 2.750$ and methanol has $C_F = 1.375$.
> 2. **Unit confusion in CII:** Always express CII in **g-CO₂ / tonne-nm** (not tonnes/tonne-nm) — examiners are strict on this. A $10^6$ factor in the conversion is easy to miss.
> 3. **EEXI vs. CII confusion:** EEXI/EEDI are *design* indices (one-time, at delivery/survey). CII is *operational* (annual). Mixing them in a single sentence is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting transport work for passenger/ro-ro ships:** Use **GT × d** for those, not DWT × d. This is a favourite examiner trick.
> 5. **SEEMP Part III vs. CII separation:** SEEMP Part III is the *plan document*; CII is the *rating metric derived from it*. They are not the same thing — treat them as distinct concepts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 📌 **SEEMP** = **S**hip **E**nergy **E**fficiency **M**anagement **P**lan — mandated under **MARPOL Annex VI Reg. 22**, applies to **all ships ≥ 400 GT** on international voyages.
- 📌 **SEEMP Part I** (2013): Generic onboard efficiency practices. **Part II** (2018): DCS data collection plan. **Part III** (2023): CII rating + corrective action.
- 📌 **CII** = annual operational CO₂ efficiency indicator — applies to **ships ≥ 5,000 GT**.
- 📌 **Formula** — $CII_A = m_{CO_2} / (DWT \times d)$, with $m_{CO_2} = \sum m_{fuel} \cdot C_F$.
- 📌 **$C_F$ values:** HFO/Diesel/MGO = **3.114**; LNG = **2.750**; Methanol ≈ 1.375.
- 📌 **$CII_{req} = (1 - Z/100) \cdot CII_R$**, where $Z$ rises annually.
- 📌 **Rating thresholds** (A/B/C/D/E) at **0.83 / 0.93 / 1.07 / 1.19 × CII_R**.
- 📌 **Three consecutive D ratings** OR a **single E rating** ⇒ **Corrective Action Plan mandatory** in SEEMP Part III.
- 📌 **EEDI** = new ships, **EEXI** = existing ships (from 2023), **CII** = operational — all under MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4.
- 📌 **EEXI/EEDI formula** has $C_F \cdot SFC \cdot P$ in numerator; $DWT \cdot V_{ref}$ in denominator.
- 📌 **Transport work**: DWT × d for cargo ships; **GT × d** for passenger / ro-ro.
- 📌 **PDCA cycle** is the heart of SEEMP — Plan, Do, Check, Act — applied annually.
- 📌 **Common energy-saving measures:** slow steaming, hull cleaning, weather routing, EPL (Engine Power Limitation), waste heat recovery, trim optimisation, LNG/methanol fuel switch.
- 📌 **IMO Net-Zero target:** GHG emissions from international shipping by or around **2050**, with **30%** reduction by 2030 and **80%** by 2040 (2008 baseline).
- 📌 SEEMP must be **integrated into the Safety Management System (SMS)** and reviewed at IAPP surveys.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Survey and Issuance of International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificates (ISPP)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST613 - MARINE POLLUTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Survey and Issuance of International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificates (ISPP)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate (ISPP Certificate)** is a statutory compliance document issued under **Regulation 5 of MARPOL Annex IV** (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the 1978 Protocol). The certificate formally certifies that a vessel, its equipment, systems, fittings, arrangements, and material processes have been surveyed, tested, and verified to comply with all the sewage discharge and pollution prevention requirements stipulated by MARPOL Annex IV.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL Annex IV — Entry Into Force:** Annex IV entered into force on **27 September 2003**. It is the last of the six MARPOL Annexes to become legally binding internationally.

The certificate is issued after a structured series of **statutory surveys** carried out on behalf of the **Administration** (Flag State) or by a **Recognized Organization (RO)** acting on its behalf — such as classification societies like Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, or IRS.

### Scope of Application (Which Ships Need ISPP?)

Under MARPOL Annex IV, Regulation 2, the ISPP Certificate applies to:

- All ships of **≥ 400 Gross Tonnage (GT)** engaged in international voyages.
- Ships of **< 400 GT** engaged in international voyages, which are **certified to carry more than 15 persons**.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Person" Definition for MARPOL Annex IV:** Every passenger or crew member on board. The threshold of 15 persons is determined from the ship's Passenger Accommodation Certificate or Crew Complement.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Think of the ISPP Certificate like a "Vehicle Pollution Undergoing Certificate (PUC)" on land — but vastly more rigorous.**

Imagine your car must obtain a government-issued pollution certificate before being registered. Before issuing it, an inspecting authority must:
1. **Visit the workshop (Initial Survey)** — check whether the pollution-control equipment was fitted at the manufacturing stage.
2. **Annual Inspection (Annual Survey)** — confirm the equipment still works.
3. **Full Re-Inspection (Renewal Survey)** — done every 5 years to renew the certificate.
4. **Post-Accident Check (Additional Survey)** — after a crash or major repair.

Similarly, the ISPP certificate is a **statutory fitness certificate for a ship's "sewage handling system."** Without it, the ship cannot legally trade internationally. Just as a PUC is essential for your car on the road, the ISPP is essential for a ship on the high seas. The surveyor acts like a pollution-control inspector from the RTO, but with maritime authority.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Gross Tonnage (GT) — defined under the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (ITC 1969)**. All MARPOL references to GT follow this convention.

---

## 1.3 Why ISPP Matters — The Bigger Picture

Ships generate two primary types of liquid waste:
- **Black Water** — from toilets, urinals, medical facilities.
- **Grey Water** — from showers, sinks, laundry, galleys.

Untreated discharge of black water near coastlines leads to:
- **Eutrophication** of coastal waters.
- **Pathogen outbreaks** (E. coli, coliforms, norovirus).
- **Nutrient loading** disturbing marine ecosystems.
- **Beach contamination** affecting tourism & fisheries.

The ISPP system ensures **controlled, hygienic, and regulated discharge**, protecting both **public health** and **marine biodiversity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statutory Survey Framework (MARPOL Annex IV, Regulation 4)

The issuance and continuous validity of the ISPP Certificate depends on the successful completion of **four types of surveys**:

| Survey Type | Timing | Purpose | Issuing Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Survey** | Before ship enters service OR before ISPP is first issued | Comprehensive check of design, construction, equipment, and arrangements | Administration / RO |
| **Renewal Survey** | Within 5 years from initial survey (or last renewal) | Full re-validation of compliance before re-issuance | Administration / RO |
| **Annual / Intermediate Survey** | Within 3 months **before or after** each anniversary date | Spot check of equipment and continuous compliance | Administration / RO |
| **Additional Survey** | After repair, overhaul, or accident affecting sewage system | Ensure post-repair integrity | Administration / RO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity of ISPP Certificate:** The ISPP Certificate is valid for a maximum of **5 years** from the date of issue. If the renewal survey is completed within 3 months **before** expiry, the new certificate remains valid from the date of completion for a further 5 years.

---

## 2.2 Survey Checklist — What the Surveyor Verifies

The surveyor, acting under Regulation 4 of MARPOL Annex IV, must verify the following on board:

### A. Sewage Holding Tank (Regulation 9)
- **Capacity adequacy** — sufficient to retain all sewage generated during voyages.
- **Construction standards** — durable, corrosion-resistant, leak-tested.
- **Tank level indicators** — fitted and operational.
- **Dedicated piping** — clearly marked with international standards.

### B. Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) — Regulation 4.1
- **Type-approved** by the Administration.
- Operates within effluent quality standards:
  - **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅) ≤ 50 mg/L**
  - **Total Suspended Solids (TSS) ≤ 50 mg/L**
  - **Fecal Coliforms ≤ 200 CFU/100 mL** (most stringent standard)
- **Discharge standards** per **Resolution MEPC.227(64)** for special areas.

### C. Disinfection / Communitzer System — Regulation 4.2
- Approved device for **comminuting and disinfecting** sewage.
- Operates when discharging between **3 and 12 nautical miles** from nearest land.

### D. Standard Discharge Connection — Regulation 10
- A **flanged connection of standard dimensions** to enable shore-based reception facility discharge.
- Standard dimensions per IMO Resolution:

| Component | Dimension |
|---|---|
| **Outside diameter of flange** | **210 mm** |
| **Inner diameter of flange** | **According to pipe outside diameter** |
| **Bolt circle diameter** | **170 mm** |
| **Number of bolt holes** | **4** |
| **Slot in flange** | **22 mm wide** |
| **Flange thickness** | **Minimum 16 mm** |

### E. Piping, Valves, Overboard Discharges — Regulation 11
- Discharge arrangements must prevent inadvertent discharge in prohibited zones.
- **Three-way valves** or equivalent arrangements provided.

---

## 2.3 Discharge Standards — MARPOL Annex IV, Regulation 11

The discharge of sewage is **regulated by zones of distance from the nearest land**:

| Distance from Nearest Land | Permissible Discharge | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **< 3 nautical miles** | **PROHIBITED** | Except via approved reception facility |
| **3 to 12 nautical miles** | Allowed if **comminuted & disinfected** using approved equipment | Ship en route ≥ 4 knots |
| **> 12 nautical miles** | Allowed if treated by **type-approved STP** meeting IMO standards | Ship en route ≥ 4 knots |
| **Anywhere** | Discharge to a **reception facility** is always permitted | No restrictions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Areas under MARPOL Annex IV:** The **Baltic Sea** is currently the only designated Special Area under Annex IV (entered into force on 1 January 2021). Discharge within the Baltic is more strictly controlled — generally sewage must be held on board or discharged via approved reception facilities.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Holding Tank Capacity** | $C_{ht} = N \times D \times q$ | Litres | IMO Guidelines |
| **Sewage generation rate per person per day (q)** | Typically **≈ 70 L/person/day** (including grey water mixed system) | L/person/day | Industry standard |
| **Discharge vessel speed** | $V_{discharge} \geq 4$ | Knots | MARPOL Annex IV Reg. 11 |
| **BOD₅ effluent standard (Type Approved STP)** | $\text{BOD}_5 \leq 50$ | mg/L | MARPOL Annex IV |
| **TSS effluent standard** | $\text{TSS} \leq 50$ | mg/L | MARPOL Annex IV |
| **Fecal Coliform effluent standard** | $FC \leq 200$ | CFU / 100 mL | MARPOL Annex IV |
| **No-discharge zone (basic)** | $D < 3$ | Nautical Miles | Reg. 11.1.1 |
| **Comminuted discharge zone** | $3 \leq D < 12$ | Nautical Miles | Reg. 11.1.2 |
| **Treated discharge zone** | $D \geq 12$ | Nautical Miles | Reg. 11.1.3 |
| **Certificate validity** | $T_{valid} \leq 5$ | Years | Reg. 5.2 |
| **Annual Survey window** | ± 3 months from anniversary | Months | Reg. 4.3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipe `|` character used in any table cell.** All conditional expressions use $\vert$ via LaTeX to maintain valid markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ISPP framework is critical for:
- **Shipyards and ship designers** — integrating holding tanks, STP, and piping during new-building.
- **Classification Societies (RO)** — performing statutory surveys on behalf of Flag States.
- **Port State Control (PSC) Officers** — verifying certificate validity during inspections.
- **Marine surveyors & chief engineers** — operational compliance and record-keeping.
- **Environmental regulators** — protecting coastal waters and special areas.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Issuance Workflow — Exhaustive Analytical Breakdown

The complete journey from vessel construction to ISPP certificate issuance follows a structured, legally defined sequence. Below is an exhaustive step-by-step analytical matrix:

### **Stage 1: New-Building Design Phase (Shipyard)**
- Shipowner requests flag-state approval of the sewage system design.
- Holding tank capacity, STP type, and overboard discharge arrangements are sized.
- **Holding tank capacity calculation:**
- $$ C_{ht} = N_{p} \times D_{voyage} \times q_{sewage} $$
- Where:
  - $C_{ht}$ = Total holding tank capacity in litres.
  - $N_{p}$ = Number of persons on board (crew + passengers).
  - $D_{voyage}$ = Duration of voyage in days (or the intended interval between port discharges).
  - $q_{sewage}$ = Sewage generation rate per person per day, typically **≈ 70 L/person/day** for combined black + grey water.

### **Stage 2: Initial Survey (Pre-Service)**
- The RO surveyor visits the vessel at the shipyard.
- Verifies: holding tank integrity, STP installation, piping, valves, and standard discharge connection.
- Conducts **functional tests** of the STP and comminuter.
- Performs **sea trials** to confirm operational discharge capability.
- Issues **ISPP Certificate** valid for up to **5 years**.

### **Stage 3: Annual / Intermediate Survey**
- Conducted within **3 months before or after** the anniversary date of the initial (or last renewal) survey.
- Focuses on:
  - Operational check of STP.
  - Visual inspection of holding tank, piping, valves.
  - Verification of overboard discharge arrangements.
  - Review of **MARPOL Record Book — Part II (Sewage)** entries.

### **Stage 4: Renewal Survey**
- Conducted **before expiry** of the current certificate.
- Equivalent in scope to the Initial Survey.
- If completed within the last 3 months of validity, the new certificate's effective date remains the survey completion date, with expiry 5 years later.
- If completed **after expiry**, a new certificate's date of issue becomes effective, and expiry is reset.

### **Stage 5: Additional Survey (Post-Repair / Post-Accident)**
- Triggered when:
  - Major repair to sewage equipment.
  - Ship collision / grounding affecting sewage system.
  - Equipment replacement affecting compliance.
- Surveyor verifies integrity post-event and may endorse the existing certificate.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Renewal Scenarios

| Scenario | Date of Renewal Survey | New Certificate Effective Date | New Expiry Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Survey within 3 months **before** expiry | $T_{survey}$ | $T_{survey}$ | $T_{survey} + 5$ years |
| Survey **after** expiry | $T_{survey}$ | $T_{survey}$ | $T_{survey} + 5$ years |
| Survey completed, certificate **endorsed** | Anniversary date | Anniversary date | Original expiry |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Holding Tank Sizing

**Problem:** A passenger ship has **120 persons on board** (crew + passengers). The longest interval between port calls (or the longest voyage segment) is **5 days**. Calculate the required minimum holding tank capacity assuming a combined sewage generation rate of 70 L/person/day.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & N_{p} = 120 \text{ persons} \\
& D_{voyage} = 5 \text{ days} \\
& q_{sewage} = 70 \text{ L/person/day} \\
\\
\text{Formula: } & C_{ht} = N_{p} \times D_{voyage} \times q_{sewage} \\
\\
\text{Substituting: } & C_{ht} = 120 \times 5 \times 70 \\
\\
\text{Calculation: } & C_{ht} = 600 \times 70 \\
\\
\text{Result: } & C_{ht} = 42{,}000 \text{ litres} \\
\\
\text{Engineering Note: } & \text{A safety margin of typically } \sim 20\% \text{ is added by shipyards.} \\
& \text{Recommended design capacity} \approx 50{,}400 \text{ litres.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Practice:** Surveyors expect a safety margin above the calculated minimum. Always specify tank capacity **≥ 1.2 × calculated minimum** in design reports.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Matrix — Survey Types at a Glance

| Survey | Trigger | Scope | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial** | New ship / new certificate required | Full | Issue ISPP (5 yrs) |
| **Annual** | Anniversary ± 3 months | Targeted | Endorsement / Continued validity |
| **Renewal** | Before expiry of current cert | Full | New ISPP (5 yrs) |
| **Additional** | Repair / accident | Focused | Endorsement of existing cert |


```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate (ISPP) and state its validity period under MARPOL Annex IV.**

**Model Answer:**

The International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate (ISPP) is a statutory document issued under **Regulation 5 of MARPOL Annex IV** to confirm that a ship and its sewage-handling systems, equipment, and arrangements have been surveyed and found to comply with the requirements of Annex IV. It applies to ships of **400 GT and above** engaged in international voyages, and to ships below 400 GT certified to carry more than 15 persons.

**Validity:** The ISPP Certificate is issued for a maximum period of **5 years** from the date of issue, subject to satisfactory completion of annual and any additional surveys.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for applicability, 1 mark for validity period.)*

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**List the four types of surveys required for ISPP certificate compliance under MARPOL Annex IV.**

**Model Answer:**

The four surveys required are:

1. **Initial Survey** — conducted before the ship enters service or before the ISPP is first issued.
2. **Annual / Intermediate Survey** — within 3 months before or after each anniversary date of the certificate.
3. **Renewal Survey** — before the expiry of the certificate, for re-issuance of a fresh 5-year ISPP.
4. **Additional Survey** — after any repair, modification, or accident affecting the sewage system.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for listing, 1.5 marks for correct timing and purpose.)*

---

## **Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the discharge requirements for sewage as stipulated under **Regulation 11 of MARPOL Annex IV**. Distinguish the rules applicable inside **3 NM**, **3–12 NM**, and beyond **12 NM** from the nearest land. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **standard discharge connection** for sewage as required by **Regulation 10 of MARPOL Annex IV**, including its key dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Sewage Discharge Requirements (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: General Principle**
The discharge of sewage from ships into the sea is controlled to prevent pollution of coastal waters. The rules differ based on distance from the nearest land and the type of equipment used.

**Step 2: Discharge Inside 3 NM (Reg. 11.1.1)**
- Sewage discharge is **PROHIBITED** when the ship is **less than 3 nautical miles** from the nearest land.
- Exception: Discharge is permitted if done through an **approved shore-based reception facility**.

**Step 3: Discharge Between 3 and 12 NM (Reg. 11.1.2)**
- Discharge is permitted **only if** the sewage has been **comminuted and disinfected** using an **approved system** as required by Regulation 4.2.
- The ship must be **en route at a speed of ≥ 4 knots**.
- The discharge rate must be **moderate** (in practice, typically at a steady controlled flow while the ship is moving).

**Step 4: Discharge Beyond 12 NM (Reg. 11.1.3)**
- Discharge is permitted if the sewage has been treated using a **type-approved Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** that meets the IMO effluent quality standards (BOD₅ ≤ 50 mg/L, TSS ≤ 50 mg/L, Fecal Coliforms ≤ 200 CFU/100 mL).
- The ship must be en route at **≥ 4 knots**.

**Step 5: Special Areas**
In designated Special Areas such as the **Baltic Sea**, sewage discharge is generally restricted to **holding tank retention** with discharge only through reception facilities.

**Tabular Summary:**

| Zone | Discharge Allowed? | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| < 3 NM | No | Only via reception facility |
| 3 to 12 NM | Yes | Comminuted + Disinfected + ≥ 4 knots |
| ≥ 12 NM | Yes | Type-approved STP + ≥ 4 knots |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks for general principle, 1.5 marks for <3 NM, 2 marks for 3–12 NM, 1.5 marks for ≥12 NM, 0.5 mark for Special Areas.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Standard Discharge Connection (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Purpose (Reg. 10)**
Ships of 400 GT and above, and those certified to carry > 15 persons engaged in international voyages, must be equipped with a **standardized discharge connection** to facilitate the offloading of sewage from the ship's holding tank to a shore-based reception facility.

**Step 2: Location and Construction**
- The connection must be fitted in an **accessible location** on the ship's hull or shell plating.
- It enables a quick, leak-proof coupling with the shore reception facility pipeline.

**Step 3: Standard Dimensions (per IMO Standard)**

| Component | Dimension |
|---|---|
| Outside diameter of flange | 210 mm |
| Inner diameter | Matches pipe OD |
| Bolt circle diameter | 170 mm |
| Number of bolt holes | 4 (slotted) |
| Slot width in flange | 22 mm |
| Flange thickness | ≥ 16 mm |

**Step 4: Functional Description**
- A **quick-coupling** with a **butt-end pipe of OD approximately 210 mm** is used.
- The flange has **4 slotted holes** (22 mm wide slots) to align with the shore facility's standard bolts.
- A **placard** must be displayed near the connection stating the standard dimensions and ISO requirements.

**Step 5: Schematic (Block Diagram)**

```
[Ship's Hull] -- [Quick-Coupling Flange] -- [Pipe of OD 210 mm]
                       |
                  4 Slotted Holes
              Bolt Circle Diameter 170 mm
                Flange Thickness >= 16 mm
```

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks for purpose, 2 marks for dimensions, 1.5 marks for functional description, 1 mark for placard, 1 mark for schematic.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **Initial Survey** procedure for issuance of the ISPP Certificate. List the equipment and arrangements that the surveyor must verify. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cargo ship with **40 persons on board** has a maximum voyage interval of **7 days** between port calls. Calculate the minimum sewage holding tank capacity required, assuming a generation rate of 70 L/person/day. Recommend a practical design capacity with a **20% safety margin**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Initial Survey Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Pre-Survey Planning (1 mark)**
- Review of approved design drawings, sewage system schematics, and equipment specifications.
- Coordination between shipowner, shipyard, and the Recognized Organization (RO).

**Step 2: Physical Inspection (2 marks)**
- Verify installation of the **sewage holding tank** with adequate capacity.
- Check **construction standards** — corrosion resistance, welding quality, leak testing.
- Inspect **piping, valves, and overboard discharge arrangements**, including three-way valves.

**Step 3: Equipment Verification (2 marks)**
- Confirm that the **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** is **type-approved** by the Administration.
- Verify the **comminuter / disinfection unit** is approved per Regulation 4.2.
- Confirm presence and correctness of the **standard discharge connection** as per Regulation 10.
- Check **tank level gauges, audible/visual alarms**, and control systems.

**Step 4: Functional and Sea Trials (1.5 marks)**
- Operate the STP under load conditions.
- Test comminuted discharge at the overboard point.
- Conduct a **sea trial** to verify discharge through overboard at ≥ 4 knots.
- Ensure all **safety devices, alarms, and interlocks** function correctly.

**Step 5: Documentation and Issuance (0.5 mark)**
- Surveyor records all observations in the survey report.
- On satisfactory completion, **ISPP Certificate is issued** valid for **5 years**.
- **MARPOL Record Book Part II (Sewage)** is opened for operational entries.

**Tabular Summary of Equipment Verified:**

| Equipment / Arrangement | Regulation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Sewage Holding Tank | Reg. 9 | Storage between port discharges |
| Type-Approved STP | Reg. 4.1 | Treat sewage before discharge |
| Comminuter + Disinfection | Reg. 4.2 | Enable 3–12 NM discharge |
| Standard Discharge Connection | Reg. 10 | Offload to shore reception facility |
| Three-way valves / Piping | Reg. 11 | Controlled overboard discharge |
| Tank Level Indicators | Reg. 9 | Operational awareness |

*(7 marks distributed across planning, inspection, equipment verification, functional testing, and documentation.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Holding Tank Capacity Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& N_{p} = 40 \text{ persons} \\
& D_{voyage} = 7 \text{ days} \\
& q_{sewage} = 70 \text{ L/person/day} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Holding Tank Capacity Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ht} &= N_{p} \times D_{voyage} \times q_{sewage} \\
C_{ht} &= 40 \times 7 \times 70 \\
C_{ht} &= 280 \times 70 \\
C_{ht} &= 19{,}600 \text{ litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply 20% Safety Margin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{design} &= C_{ht} \times 1.20 \\
C_{design} &= 19{,}600 \times 1.20 \\
C_{design} &= 23{,}520 \text{ litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Recommendation**

**Recommended design capacity ≈ 23,520 litres (≈ 24,000 litres rounded up).**

The actual installed tank should be at least **24,000 litres** to accommodate:
- Variations in sewage generation due to weather (e.g., increased water use in hot climates).
- Operational delays between port calls.
- Buffer in case of port reception facility unavailability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to include the **safety margin (20%)** required in real engineering practice. Always add the safety margin in your final answer. Marks are typically awarded for both the bare calculated value AND the recommended design value. Also, do not forget to mention the **moderate discharge rate** condition (ship ≥ 4 knots) in any discharge-related answer.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for identifying data, 2 marks for substituting into formula, 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for safety margin application, 1 mark for engineering recommendation.)*

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- The **ISPP Certificate** is issued under **MARPOL Annex IV, Regulation 5** for ships of ≥ **400 GT** on international voyages (or ships < 400 GT carrying **> 15 persons**).
- **Four surveys** govern the certificate's life cycle: **Initial, Annual (within ± 3 months of anniversary), Renewal (every 5 years), and Additional (post-repair / accident)**.
- Certificate validity: **Maximum 5 years**.
- **Discharge zones** under Reg. 11:
  - < 3 NM → **PROHIBITED** (except via reception facility)
  - 3–12 NM → Only if **comminuted and disinfected** at **≥ 4 knots**
  - ≥ 12 NM → Only if **type-approved STP** at **≥ 4 knots**
- **Standard Discharge Connection** dimensions (Reg. 10): Flange OD = **210 mm**, Bolt Circle = **170 mm**, 4 slotted holes, Slot width = **22 mm**, Flange thickness ≥ **16 mm**.
- **Holding tank capacity formula:** $C_{ht} = N_{p} \times D_{voyage} \times q_{sewage}$
- Always add a **20% safety margin** in practical design.
- **Sewage definition** per Reg. 1 — includes drainage from toilets, urinals, medical spaces, and spaces containing living animals; also wastewater mixed with these.
- The **Baltic Sea** is currently the **only Special Area** under Annex IV (effective 1 January 2021).
- **Sewage Treatment Plant effluent standards:** BOD₅ ≤ 50 mg/L, TSS ≤ 50 mg/L, Fecal Coliforms ≤ 200 CFU/100 mL.
- **MARPOL Record Book — Part II (Sewage)** must be maintained for all discharge and disposal operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Tip:** For KTU ESE answers, always remember to **cite the Regulation number** (e.g., Reg. 5, Reg. 11) — examiners award marks for correct MARPOL references.
